source.title: Release Scallop: Quiet-Window Playbook and Annotated Rollout Notes
source.body:
Context:
This is the "Scallop" quiet-window playbook — a focused, minimal-disruption rollout pattern designed for low-risk model and infra changes where we intentionally reduce background noise and increase signal fidelity for latency/cost regressions. Use for incremental model swaps, batching config tweaks, KV-cache policy changes, and small routing/fallback updates.

Goals:
- Keep customer-facing error/latency impact < 0.1% above baseline
- Ensure telemetry sampling fidelity for tail latency and per-route cost metrics
- Provide an automated, low-friction rollback path with clear ownership
- Validate model-quality signals on live traffic (A/B) without broad exposure

Scope & When to use:
- Non-breaking model parameter changes, quantization profile swaps, and batching policy experiments
- NOT for major behavioral model upgrades, schema changes, or infra re-architecture

Quick 10-day rollout calendar (annotated)
Day -7: Preflight
  - Notify stakeholders: #release-ops, SRE, Observability, Product on planned quiet window dates and expected traffic slice.
  - Create short-running synthetic replay job against canary model using recent prompt set (30m). Link: GH:repo/replays/canary-run-2028-06
  - Lock deploy window in calendar (2h quiet window at low traffic hours), block other releases.
  - Telemetry: enable high-fidelity sampling for target routes (100% for canary slice, 10% baseline).

Day -3: Staging & Smoke
  - Run full smoke on staging with prefill KV calls (prefill KV cache warmers to avoid first-request noise).
  - Confirm batching behavior with realistic token distributions; capture batch size histograms.
  - Create rollout brief in channel and update linked artifacts.

Day -1: Dry Run (internal)
  - Execute dry deploy into a reserved dedicated pool with routing rules mirroring production but with 0% traffic. Validate metrics collection and fallback chain.

Day 0: Quiet Window Execution
  - Hour 0: Flip routing to canary slice (1% traffic) with token-bucket throttle at 500r/s and cadence ramp to 5% over 60m if SLOs hold.
  - Hour 1: Telemetry check: ensure no increase in 95/99p latency > 15% (unless known baseline). Verify per-token unit cost delta.
  - Hour 2: If metrics stable, enable long-tail replay sampling (store 1% of responses for eval).
  - Hour 3: Decision gate: promote to 10% or rollback. Callout owners: Release lead (Ethan Park), SRE lead (Anjali Rao), Product lead (Lina Martinez).

Day +1: Ramp and Guardrail
  - If promoted, ramp to 25% across regions over 6 hours with regional stagger and warmers for high-LAT regions.
  - Verify KV cache hit-rate improvements (or regressions) and batching efficiency.

Day +3: Observability Deep-Dive
  - Run targeted traces for top 10 slowest routes (latency shadowing enabled). Correlate with model token distribution and kernel selection logs.

Day +7: Post-release & Retro
  - Compile rollout metrics, annotated diffs, and short retrospective.

Role-based checklists (quick):
Release Lead:
  - Confirm owner coverage for all decision gates
  - Publish brief and update incident runbook links
SRE:
  - Validate autoscale thresholds and ensure burst capacity policy active
  - Pre-create rollback runbook commands (CLI snippets for traffic flip, capacity shrink)
Observability:
  - Increase trace sampling on affected routes, add tags model.version and route.variant
  - Prepare dashboards for cost-per-token, per-route latency, batch-size distribution
Product/QA:
  - Approve canary prompt set, review sampled outputs for quality regressions
  - Surface any hallucination or content-safety flags immediately

Telemetry & Sampling Plan (detail):
- Baseline sampling remains at 1% for long-term. For Scallop:
  - Canary slice: 100% telemetry (for first 2 hours), then 25% until day+3
  - Long-tail eval: store 1% of generated outputs with metadata (model.version, prompt.template, kv_cache_hit, batch_size) to the eval bucket s3://redwood-evals/scallop/2028-06
  - Shadow traces: enable for top 5 routes only to limit token cost.

Rollback matrix (fast heuristics):
- Automatic rollback triggers:
  - 95p latency > baseline + 20% sustained for 10m
  - Error rate increase > 0.5% absolute for 5m
  - Cost-per-token > baseline + 40% for 30m (unexpected)
- Manual rollback triggers:
  - Model-quality regression flagged by Product on sampled outputs
  - Unexpected coupling with downstream caches or KMS/key fetch failures
- Rollback steps (fast):
  1) Flip routing to last-known-good model variant using the rollout API: curl -X POST /rollouts/revert -d '{"route":"/v1/generate","target":"model@2028-06-01"}'
  2) Clear any stale warmers if necessary: infra/warmers/clear.sh <pool>
  3) Notify #incident and mark incident in Jira ENG-4821

Release Notes Draft (to publish after day+1 if promoted):
Title: Incremental canary: batching & KV policy tweak (Scallop) — 2028-06-14
Summary:
  This release adjusts batching windows and KV cache prefetch behavior for model family rwd-llm-3 to reduce per-token cost and improve tail latency for interactive routes. The change was rolled out under a quiet-window protocol and promoted to 25% traffic after telemetry verification.
Impact & Observed Metrics:
  - 25% reduction in median cost-per-token on sampled routes
  - 3% improvement in median latency; 12% improvement in 95p for low-volume routes
  - No customer-facing errors detected in the 72h window
Notes for customers:
  - No action required. If you pin to a specific model tag, refer to model@2028-06-01 as the stable fallback.
Known issues:
  - A small region (ap-southeast-2) saw transient KV cache miss spikes during initial ramp; warmers fixed the deviation.

Open questions & follow-ups:
- Should we make Scallop an opt-in default for small routing-only rollouts?
- Can we automate the prefill warmer step in the pipeline? (ENG-5010)
- Add a telemetry cost cap to the rollout API to prevent runaway sampling costs?

Action items (owners):
- Automate warmers in CI: Tom Beckett (due 2028-07-01)
- Telemetry cost-cap POC: Marcus Nguyen (due 2028-06-30)
- Post-release quality audit (prompt set): Lina Martinez (due 2028-06-21)

Notes to reviewers: this doc is intentionally prescriptive and narrow in scope. It is a lightweight alternative to a full canary for changes where user-visible behavior is expected to be unchanged but infra/econ regressions need extra signal. Feedback welcome on thresholds and telemetry budgets.

source.title: GPU ops - fatigue & rotation notes (2028-06-12)
source.content:
Context
---
Weekly notes focused on operational load for GPU-ops oncall, short rotation experiments, and quick changes to the triage/playbook to reduce cognitive load during surge windows. This doc is intentionally informal — notes from the 2028-06-12 ops sync.

Attendees
- Ethan Park (owner)
- Priya Nair (oncall lead)
- Marco Rossi (fleet tooling)
- Lina Gomez (SRE rotation coordinator)
- Simon (shadowing new hire)

High-level problem statement
- Ops team reports increased mental/physical fatigue over repeated surge weekends. Recent weekend (2028-06-09) required 18 total manual priming actions across three regions. Small manual steps add up and cause handoff friction.
- We want to reduce per-incident cognitive steps, streamline handoffs, and experiment with shorter oncall windows + overlap to lower fatigue while keeping same coverage.

Current signals (observed last 30 days)
- Median weekly active oncall incidents: 6 (but variance: weekends with spikes had 12+ active items)
- Average incident time-to-first-action during surges: 8.6 minutes (target < 5)
- Manual priming actions per surge: 12–20 (depending on region)
- Controlled prewarm success rate: 74% for targeted models; miss rate correlates with eviction windows in APAC edge nodes.
- Operator subjective score (post-shift survey, n=11): mean 3.1/5 for fatigue. Comments: repetitive context-switching, unclear handoff state, too many ad-hoc Playbook lookups.

Observations from last weekend
- Handoff notes often incomplete: missing which node groups were affected, which prewarm tokens were used, and whether fallback model switching occurred.
- The Slack channel used for surge alerts had >250 messages over 12 hours; important triage comments buried.
- Oncall followed playbook steps manually to create a warm pool in us-west but missed a backfill step in europe-west: caused 9–12 minute tail latency spikes for embedding jobs.

Decisions made in sync
1) Trial schedule change: start a 6-week experiment with 8-hour oncall shifts (instead of 12) during peak weekend coverage (Fri–Sun 14:00–02:00 UTC). Each shift will have a 30-minute overlap with the incoming shift for live handoff.
2) Create a one-screen triage summary card for slack (scripted): key signals + recommended actions. Aim: reduce mental search and speed up first action to < 5 minutes.
3) Mandate a 5-line minimum handoff note in the ticket template for any escalation that crosses a shift boundary. Include: affected region, node groups, last priming run id, fallback model used.
4) Label a small group of senior operators as "Handoff Champions" for each weekend to mentor and ensure quality of handoff notes.

Immediate tactical changes (next 7 days)
- Marco to ship a Slack summary bot (INFRA-2143) that composes a short triage card automatically from monitoring tags. ETA: 2028-06-15.
- Priya to run a 30-minute handoff training session on 2028-06-14 (recorded). Focus: what to include in the 5-line handoff, when to escalate, and how to mark ticket state.
- Lina to update rotation calendar + oncall playbook entries to reflect 8-hour trial schedule. Update Confluence doc by 2028-06-13.
- Ethan to add a short checklist to the GPU operations runbook for prewarm backfill verification (three quick checks that take < 2 minutes).

Longer-term experiments (Q3)
- Automated warm-pool reconstitution: pilot a controller that runs a quick health-check and replenishes warm pools when eviction detected. Goal: reduce manual priming actions by 40%. Requirements doc ticketed as SRE-987.
- Investigate lightweight UI overlays for the Console to surface "handoff status" (one-line state visible on every region dashboard) so that alerts + handoffs are co-located.
- Evaluate rotating to a "mini-sprint" oncall model: 4-day blocks with micro-handoffs within the block to reduce nightly context-switches. Requires HR/comp considerations; propose POC early Q3.

Playbook snippets (to be added to Confluence)
- 5-minute triage checklist: 1) Confirm region and node-group; 2) Confirm model variant and priming id; 3) Check warm-pool health (controller metric); 4) If warm-pool < threshold, run quick reconstitute script; 5) Post short handoff note and set ticket state to "awaiting followup".
- Handoff template (required): "Region / Node group / Last priming id / Actions taken (1–3) / Next steps"
- Quick escalation thresholds: latency P95 > 2× baseline for 5 minutes, or warm-pool miss rate > 20% for > 10 minutes.

Open questions / risks
- 8-hour shifts may increase total handoffs per week (more context switches). The overlap window is designed to mitigate, but we should instrument transfer quality.
- Hiring/compensation concerns if we increase weekend coverage intensity. Need to align with People Ops before extending experiment beyond 6 weeks.
- Automated warm-pool controller could introduce new failure modes if not rate-limited; we need a safety throttle and circuit-breaker.

Action items (owners & due dates)
- Marco: Slack triage bot prototype (INFRA-2143) — due 2028-06-15.
- Priya: Handoff training + recording — due 2028-06-14.
- Lina: Update rota and publish new oncall calendar — due 2028-06-13.
- Ethan: Add 3-step prewarm verification checklist to runbook + sample ticket — due 2028-06-14.
- Marco & SRE: Draft controller safety design doc (SRE-987) — first draft due 2028-06-28.

Notes to self (Ethan)
- Keep the experiment timeboxed and capture fatigue metrics weekly via short pulse survey after each weekend. Use anonymized responses.
- If first-week overlap feels rushed, extend overlap to 45 minutes for the next two weekends.
- Watch for unintentional increase in manual actions during morning handoffs (08:00–10:00 UTC) — this was a recurring pain window last month.

Appendix: quick metrics snapshot (2028-06-09 weekend)
- Peak GPU queue length (global): 184 (peak at 2028-06-09 21:40 UTC)
- Us-west warm-pool miss rate: 18% (spike to 29% during 21:00–22:00 UTC)
- Median TTF action during surge: 8.6 minutes
- Number of distinct manual priming commands executed: 18

References
- Playbook: Confluence: /display/INFRA/GPU+Operations+Runbook
- Tickets: INFRA-2143, SRE-987
- Slack thread: #gpu-ops-surge-2028-06-09 (internal)

End of notes.

(If anyone wants a pared-down PDF for leadership, I can produce a 1-pager summarizing decisions and next steps.)

Open questions / risks
- 8-hour shifts may increase total handoffs per week (more context switches). The overlap window is designed to mitigate, but we should instrument transfer quality.
- Hiring/compensation concerns if we increase weekend coverage intensity. Need to align with People Ops before extending experiment beyond 6 weeks.
- Automated warm-pool controller could introduce new failure modes if not rate-limited; we need a safety throttle and circuit-breaker.

Action items (owners & due dates)
- Marco: Slack triage bot prototype (INFRA-2143) — due 2028-06-15.
- Priya: Handoff training + recording — due 2028-06-14.
- Lina: Update rota and publish new oncall calendar — due 2028-06-13.
- Ethan: Add 3-step prewarm verification checklist to runbook + sample ticket — due 2028-06-14.
- Marco & SRE: Draft controller safety design doc (SRE-987) — first draft due 2028-06-28.

Notes to self (Ethan)
- Keep the experiment timeboxed and capture fatigue metrics weekly via short pulse survey after each weekend. Use anonymized responses.
- If first-week overlap feels rushed, extend overlap to 45 minutes for the next two weekends.
- Watch for unintentional increase in manual actions during morning handoffs (08:00–10:00 UTC) — this was a recurring pain window last month.

Appendix: quick metrics snapshot (2028-06-09 weekend)
- Peak GPU queue length (global): 184 (peak at 2028-06-09 21:40 UTC)
- Us-west warm-pool miss rate: 18% (spike to 29% during 21:00–22:00 UTC)
- Median TTF action during surge: 8.6 minutes
- Number of distinct manual priming commands executed: 18

References
- Playbook: Confluence: /display/INFRA/GPU+Operations+Runbook
- Tickets: INFRA-2143, SRE-987
- Slack thread: #gpu-ops-surge-2028-06-09 (internal)

End of notes.

(If anyone wants a pared-down PDF for leadership, I can produce a 1-pager summarizing decisions and next steps.)
text:
Context
---
Weekly notes focused on operational load for GPU-ops oncall, short rotation experiments, and quick changes to the triage/playbook to reduce cognitive load during surge windows. This doc is intentionally informal — notes from the 2028-06-12 ops sync.

Attendees
- Ethan Park (owner)
- Priya Nair (oncall lead)
- Marco Rossi (fleet tooling)
- Lina Gomez (SRE rotation coordinator)
- Simon (shadowing new hire)

High-level problem statement
- Ops team reports increased mental/physical fatigue over repeated surge weekends. Recent weekend (2028-06-09) required 18 total manual priming actions across three regions. Small manual steps add up and cause handoff friction.
- We want to reduce per-incident cognitive steps, streamline handoffs, and experiment with shorter oncall windows + overlap to lower fatigue while keeping same coverage.

Current signals (observed last 30 days)
- Median weekly active oncall incidents: 6 (but variance: weekends with spikes had 12+ active items)
- Average incident time-to-first-action during surges: 8.6 minutes (target < 5)
- Manual priming actions per surge: 12–20 (depending on region)
- Controlled prewarm success rate: 74% for targeted models; miss rate correlates with eviction windows in APAC edge nodes.
- Operator subjective score (post-shift survey, n=11): mean 3.1/5 for fatigue. Comments: repetitive context-switching, unclear handoff state, too many ad-hoc Playbook lookups.

Observations from last weekend
- Handoff notes often incomplete: missing which node groups were affected, which prewarm tokens were used, and whether fallback model switching occurred.
- The Slack channel used for surge alerts had >250 messages over 12 hours; important triage comments buried.
- Oncall followed playbook steps manually to create a warm pool in us-west but missed a backfill step in europe-west: caused 9–12 minute tail latency spikes for embedding jobs.

Decisions made in sync
1) Trial schedule change: start a 6-week experiment with 8-hour oncall shifts (instead of 12) during peak weekend coverage (Fri–Sun 14:00–02:00 UTC). Each shift will have a 30-minute overlap with the incoming shift for live handoff.
2) Create a one-screen triage summary card for slack (scripted): key signals + recommended actions. Aim: reduce mental search and speed up first action to < 5 minutes.
3) Mandate a 5-line minimum handoff note in the ticket template for any escalation that crosses a shift boundary. Include: affected region, node groups, last priming run id, fallback model used.
4) Label a small group of senior operators as "Handoff Champions" for each weekend to mentor and ensure quality of handoff notes.

Immediate tactical changes (next 7 days)
- Marco to ship a Slack summary bot (INFRA-2143) that composes a short triage card automatically from monitoring tags. ETA: 2028-06-15.
- Priya to run a 30-minute handoff training session on 2028-06-14 (recorded). Focus: what to include in the 5-line handoff, when to escalate, and how to mark ticket state.
- Lina to update rotation calendar + oncall playbook entries to reflect 8-hour trial schedule. Update Confluence doc by 2028-06-13.
- Ethan to add a short checklist to the GPU operations runbook for prewarm backfill verification (three quick checks that take < 2 minutes).

Longer-term experiments (Q3)
- Automated warm-pool reconstitution: pilot a controller that runs a quick health-check and replenishes warm pools when eviction detected. Goal: reduce manual priming actions by 40%. Requirements doc ticketed as SRE-987.
- Investigate lightweight UI overlays for the Console to surface "handoff status" (one-line state visible on every region dashboard) so that alerts + handoffs are co-located.
- Evaluate rotating to a "mini-sprint" oncall model: 4-day blocks with micro-handoffs within the block to reduce nightly context-switches. Requires HR/comp considerations; propose POC early Q3.

Playbook snippets (to be added to Confluence)
- 5-minute triage checklist: 1) Confirm region and node-group; 2) Confirm model variant and priming id; 3) Check warm-pool health (controller metric); 4) If warm-pool < threshold, run quick reconstitute script; 5) Post short handoff note and set ticket state to "awaiting followup".
- Handoff template (required): "Region / Node group / Last priming id / Actions taken (1–3) / Next steps"
- Quick escalation thresholds: latency P95 > 2× baseline for 5 minutes, or warm-pool miss rate > 20% for > 10 minutes.

Open questions / risks
- 8-hour shifts may increase total handoffs per week (more context switches). The overlap window is designed to mitigate, but we should instrument transfer quality.
- Hiring/compensation concerns if we increase weekend coverage intensity. Need to align with People Ops before extending experiment beyond 6 weeks.
- Automated warm-pool controller could introduce new failure modes if not rate-limited; we need a safety throttle and circuit-breaker.

- Priya: Handoff training + recording — due 2028-06-14.
- Lina: Update rota and publish new oncall calendar — due 2028-06-13.
- Ethan: Add 3-step prewarm verification checklist to runbook + sample ticket — due 2028-06-14.
- Marco & SRE: Draft controller safety design doc (SRE-987) — first draft due 2028-06-28.

Notes to self (Ethan)
- Keep the experiment timeboxed and capture fatigue metrics weekly via short pulse survey after each weekend. Use anonymized responses.
- If first-week overlap feels rushed, extend overlap to 45 minutes for the next two weekends.
- Watch for unintentional increase in manual actions during morning handoffs (08:00–10:00 UTC) — this was a recurring pain window last month.

Appendix: quick metrics snapshot (2028-06-09 weekend)
- Peak GPU queue length (global): 184 (peak at 2028-06-09 21:40 UTC)
- Us-west warm-pool miss rate: 18% (spike to 29% during 21:00–22:00 UTC)

References

source.title: Sparrowboard — quick experiments coverage & owner tally
source.description: Compact spreadsheet used for rapid cross-functional headcount/coverage decisions for a set of short-lived experiments and safety rollouts. Meant to be skimmed in 30s by leads and updated in-line during standups.
source.content:
Table (rows are lightweight entries; columns: id | experiment | primary-ask | owner | coverage-tier | est-effort-h | blocker | quick-mitigation | success-metric | linked-ticket)

1 | small-model-quant-safety-check | confirm quantized model parity on 512 tokens | Maya S. | tier-B (oncall fallback) | 6h | infra env flakiness | run on dedicated GPU pool, reduce batch to 1 | 0.5% delta in rouge/LLM-quality on eval-set | JIRA-RE-2147
2 | embedding-cache-ttl-probe | validate 15m vs 60m cache hit % and cost delta | Leo C. | tier-C (passive) | 3h | missing perf traces | attach tracer snippet + synthetic load | +10% hit, -8% cost | JIRA-RE-2152
3 | rollout-minor-tokenizer-fix | ensure no decode regressions for emojis | Priya D. | tier-A (manual gate) | 2h | unclear test coverage | quick test harness: 200 emoji-heavy prompts | 0 regression count | n/a (internal)
4 | routing-fallback-latency-sample | sample end-to-end p99 across eu/us for fallback routes | Rafael O. | tier-B | 8h | transient region quota | force small synthetic load and sample 1h | p99 < 450ms fallback | JIRA-RE-2169
5 | cost-blitz-embedding-downscale | quick experiment: use smaller-dim embeddings for search pilot | Ethan P. | tier-C | 5h | evaluation dataset size small | use stratified sampling + holdout | 95% recall vs baseline | https://github.com/redwood-inference/research/issue/112
6 | canary-stream-conn-cap | sanity-check streaming session limits on dedicated nodes | Maya S. | tier-A | 4h | ephemeral session drop during warmup | warmup script + slow ramp | <0.2% session drop | JIRA-RE-2171

Notes-to-self: quick updates only here; do not replace canonical runbooks. This sheet is intentionally terse. Use it to decide: 1) immediate owner for ad-hoc ops; 2) whether to escalate to SRE or keep as product experiment; 3) rough ETA for next sync.

Open questions: 
- Which experiments require a compliance review (PII extraction risk)? Priya to flag by EOD.
- Can infra provide a 2-hour burst slot for routing-sample (Rafael asks)? Slack #infra-booking.

Quick formulas/heuristics used (documented inline): 
- coverage-tier mapping: tier-A = manual gate + stakeholder review, tier-B = auto fallback + owner watch, tier-C = passive metric sample only.
- est-effort-h is a 50% buffer on engineer-only work (includes test+cleanup).

Actionable next steps (short):
- Maya: run canary-stream warmup script + paste raw sample (due 2028-04-04).
- Leo: attach cache hit graphs (ask observability for 24h snapshot) by tomorrow.
- Priya: quick emoji harness output + note on compliance flag by EOD.
- Rafael: request burst slot via infra, link ticket here when confirmed.
- Ethan: collate all experiment outputs into single short slack thread: #ops-sparrowboard.

Review cadence: weekly quick-scrim (Wed 09:15 PT). If any tier-A shows regressions, escalate to product-ops leadership immediately.

Scratch/brain dump (very informal):
- Potential short follow-up: rollup into a 'mini postmortem' if any metric regresses by >1.5x.
- Keep this doc small; if it grows beyond 8 rows, split per domain (routing / embeddings / serving).

Last quick edits: reduced verbosity, moved long logs to linked-ticket comments.

source.title: Engineer cohort update — Sprint bridge (May 9, 2027)
source.content:
Purpose:
Short, cross-cohort snapshot for the serving-runtime org sync. Intended to capture high-impact status, open risks, and concrete owner-ready actions before the next org-wide update. Keep this doc terse — use linked artifacts for deep dive.

Attendees:
- Ethan Park (owner)
- Priya Nambiar (perf lead)
- Rosa Martinez (SRE)
- Liam O'Connor (runtime eng)
- Jun Wei (infra)
- Maya Singh (product partner)

TL;DR:
- Canary rollout of model-routing v2 started 2027-05-06; initial traffic at 8%. p50 latency improved ~12% on long-tail (seq>1024), p95 unchanged.
- Two infra blockers slowing Dedicated region scaling: NVLink firmware and internal image-signing bug. Both are being tracked; we expect mitigation steps by 2027-05-12.
- Minor user-impacting incident on 2027-05-08 caused 10 minutes of elevated 5xxs for embeddings route; root cause: transient prefix-cache eviction due to race in cache-keeper. Patch merged (PR 2841) and rolled out to canary.

Highlights and context:
1) Model-routing v2 canary
   - Goal: route large-context requests to qkv-optimized runtimes, reduce cost while preserving p50 latency SLOs.
   - Status: 8% production traffic, route rules evaluated against sequence length histograms.
   - Observed: p50 improved ~12% for >1024 sequences. Token throughput on dedicated nodes increased 7% under sustained load.
   - Next: expand canary to 25% on 5/12 if no regression.

2) Prefix/KV cache stabilization
   - Incident 2027-05-08 summary: cache-keeper missed eviction TTLs under GC pressure leading to higher memory churn and an NPE in the eviction path.
   - Action taken: emergency guard added to prevent double-free, patch merged (PR 2841), canary verified.
   - Follow-ups: add unit tests for concurrent eviction, and a small chaos test simulating GC spikes. Owners: Rosa (SRE) + Liam (runtime). Due: 2027-05-16.

3) Dedicated region capacity delays
   - Blockers: NVLink firmware mismatch reported by vendor on 5/05; image signing pipeline failing for some AMI builds.
   - Impact: two customer Dedicated onboarding schedules shifted by 2 weeks.
   - Mitigation plan: Jun is liaising with vendor to stage a firmware roll and provide a temporary node flavor without NVLink for onboarding. Estimated lift 5/12.

Metrics snapshot (most recent 24h average vs prior week):
- p50 generation latency: 210ms (was 238ms)
- p95 generation latency: 1.12s (unchanged)
- tokens/sec per GPU: +7% on optimized workloads
- embeddings 5xx rate: 0.05% peak (incident window), baseline 0.001%
- canary error budget burn: 0.02% (within thresholds)

Open action matrix (owner / short task / due):
- Priya Nambiar / Validate model-routing rules against nightly long-tail dataset + produce comparative report / 2027-05-11
- Rosa Martinez / Add concurrent-eviction unit tests + chaos scenario to CI / 2027-05-16
- Liam O'Connor / Expand canary rollout to 25% and run 6-hour soak test / 2027-05-12
- Jun Wei / Coordinate temporary node flavor and vendor firmware timeline / 2027-05-12
- Maya Singh / Communicate revised Dedicated onboarding timelines to CS and impacted accounts / 2027-05-11
- Ethan Park / Prep short incident blameless postmortem and publish to Confluence / 2027-05-13

Risks & requests (escalations):
- Hardware vendor SLAs: if firmware fix slips past 5/15, we need to enable multi-region onboarding fallback; ask GTM to pause dependent customer commitments. (owner: Jun)
- Capacity forecasting: current autoscaler sim shows brittle behavior at scale bursts. Request SRE + infra pairing session to define tighter thresholds. (owner: Rosa + Jun)
- Observability gaps: KV cache internals lack flamegraphs in the flame dashboard; ask observability team to enable async sampling for runtime hosts. (owner: Priya)

Decisions made in this sync:
- Proceed with cautious canary expansion to 25% on 5/12 provided no p95 regression and error budget remains healthy.
- Emergency patch accepted to stopgap cache eviction failures; full cleanup scheduled in next sprint.

Parking lot / topics for next meeting:
- Quantization regression testing: how we measure hallucination surface area vs cost. (Maya to scope)
- Autoscaler tuning bake-off: agree on target scenarios and SLOs. (Rosa/Jun)
- Playbook refresh: Dedicated onboarding checklist (hand-off to Solutions Eng).

Next sync:
- Weekly cohort bridge, tentative 2027-05-16 10:00 PDT / same Zoom.

Notes + references:
- Incident ticket: SERVE-1298 (see linked artifacts)
- Patch PR: github.com/redwood-inference/serving-runtime/pull/2841
- Long-tail dataset used for model-routing tests: confluence:redwood-inference/serving-runtime/long-tail-datasets

Quick wins identified:
- Short-term: add a TTL safety margin in cache-keeper to avoid race windows (Rosa + Liam, 2 days)
- Medium-term: add nightly long-tail regression job to detect p95 regressions earlier (Priya, 1 sprint)

End of note.

Record prepared by: Ethan Park (serving-runtime)
Reviewers: Priya Nambiar, Rosa Martinez

Highlights and context:
1) Model-routing v2 canary
   - Goal: route large-context requests to qkv-optimized runtimes, reduce cost while preserving p50 latency SLOs.
   - Status: 8% production traffic, route rules evaluated against sequence length histograms.
   - Observed: p50 improved ~12% for >1024 sequences. Token throughput on dedicated nodes increased 7% under sustained load.
   - Next: expand canary to 25% on 5/12 if no regression.

2) Prefix/KV cache stabilization
   - Incident 2027-05-08 summary: cache-keeper missed eviction TTLs under GC pressure leading to higher memory churn and an NPE in the eviction path.
   - Action taken: emergency guard added to prevent double-free, patch merged (PR 2841), canary verified.
   - Follow-ups: add unit tests for concurrent eviction, and a small chaos test simulating GC spikes. Owners: Rosa (SRE) + Liam (runtime). Due: 2027-05-16.

3) Dedicated region capacity delays
   - Blockers: NVLink firmware mismatch reported by vendor on 5/05; image signing pipeline failing for some AMI builds.
   - Impact: two customer Dedicated onboarding schedules shifted by 2 weeks.
   - Mitigation plan: Jun is liaising with vendor to stage a firmware roll and provide a temporary node flavor without NVLink for onboarding. Estimated lift 5/12.

Metrics snapshot (most recent 24h average vs prior week):
- p50 generation latency: 210ms (was 238ms)
- p95 generation latency: 1.12s (unchanged)
- tokens/sec per GPU: +7% on optimized workloads
- embeddings 5xx rate: 0.05% peak (incident window), baseline 0.001%
- canary error budget burn: 0.02% (within thresholds)

Open action matrix (owner / short task / due):
- Priya Nambiar / Validate model-routing rules against nightly long-tail dataset + produce comparative report / 2027-05-11
- Rosa Martinez / Add concurrent-eviction unit tests + chaos scenario to CI / 2027-05-16
- Liam O'Connor / Expand canary rollout to 25% and run 6-hour soak test / 2027-05-12
- Jun Wei / Coordinate temporary node flavor and vendor firmware timeline / 2027-05-12
- Maya Singh / Communicate revised Dedicated onboarding timelines to CS and impacted accounts / 2027-05-11
- Ethan Park / Prep short incident blameless postmortem and publish to Confluence / 2027-05-13

Risks & requests (escalations):
- Hardware vendor SLAs: if firmware fix slips past 5/15, we need to enable multi-region onboarding fallback; ask GTM to pause dependent customer commitments. (owner: Jun)
- Capacity forecasting: current autoscaler sim shows brittle behavior at scale bursts. Request SRE + infra pairing session to define tighter thresholds. (owner: Rosa + Jun)
- Observability gaps: KV cache internals lack flamegraphs in the flame dashboard; ask observability team to enable async sampling for runtime hosts. (owner: Priya)

Decisions made in this sync:
- Proceed with cautious canary expansion to 25% on 5/12 provided no p95 regression and error budget remains healthy.
- Emergency patch accepted to stopgap cache eviction failures; full cleanup scheduled in next sprint.

Parking lot / topics for next meeting:
- Quantization regression testing: how we measure hallucination surface area vs cost. (Maya to scope)
- Autoscaler tuning bake-off: agree on target scenarios and SLOs. (Rosa/Jun)
- Playbook refresh: Dedicated onboarding checklist (hand-off to Solutions Eng).

Next sync:
- Weekly cohort bridge, tentative 2027-05-16 10:00 PDT / same Zoom.

Notes + references:
- Incident ticket: SERVE-1298 (see linked artifacts)
- Patch PR: github.com/redwood-inference/serving-runtime/pull/2841
- Long-tail dataset used for model-routing tests: confluence:redwood-inference/serving-runtime/long-tail-datasets

Quick wins identified:
- Short-term: add a TTL safety margin in cache-keeper to avoid race windows (Rosa + Liam, 2 days)
- Medium-term: add nightly long-tail regression job to detect p95 regressions earlier (Priya, 1 sprint)

End of note.

Record prepared by: Ethan Park (serving-runtime)
Reviewers: Priya Nambiar, Rosa Martinez

source.title: Customer Adoption & Integration Standup — 2027-02-15
source.content:
Purpose: Weekly cross-functional sync to track customer adoption progress, surface deployment blockers, and align next steps for Solutions Engineering + Pro Services. This note captures the 2027-02-15 standup and outstanding actions.

Attendees: Ethan Park (owner), Sana Patel, Liam O'Connor, Priya Nair, Marcos Ruiz (SE), Julia Chen (ProServ), Alex G. (SRE on-call for capacity)

Agenda:
- Quick status by customer (focus on integration milestones)
- Open technical blockers and ramp items
- Capacity scheduling and Dedicated reservations
- Action item review and owners

Summary (high-level):
- Three customers made meaningful progress this week; two have new blockers related to network egress and quantized model mismatch.
- We confirmed a temporary fallback policy to hosted small-quant models for low-latency routes while customers finish quant validation.
- Dedicated capacity reservation for two pilots needs final sign-off from finance (JIRA-7132).

Customer updates:
1) Aster Labs (Health-tech pilot)
- Current phase: validation + pre-production traffic soak.
- This week: completed prefix caching tests and traced a 20% token cost improvement in canonical flows.
- Blocker: KMS handshake failures intermittently during long-running inference sessions (seen during heavy KV cache warmups). Root cause narrowed to an HSM timeout when key rotation coincides with cache flush.
- Mitigation: ProServ running staggered rotation windows; SRE action to adjust HSM timeout (JIRA-7187).
- Next milestone: full pre-prod sign-off and start of canary (target 2027-02-21). Owner: Marcos Ruiz.

2) BlueNova (SaaS customer onboarding)
- Current phase: code drop and routes wiring; early embedding validation passed.
- This week: SDK integration completed for two endpoints; test corpus showed occasional hallucination regressions on domain prompts.
- Blocker: Quality regression on latest quantized 4-bit variant versus their baseline — impacts reranking pipeline.
- Mitigation: Swap to mixed-precision variant for rerank route; Product to open rollback window and flag model quality alert in Console. Pending PR: https://github.com/redwood/inference/pull/964
- Next milestone: complete reranker tuning and resume canary (target 2027-02-18). Owner: Sana Patel.

3) Veridian Financial (enterprise Dedicated pilot)
- Current phase: Dedicated capacity provisioning and compliance onboarding.
- This week: legal/compliance completed initial SOC checklist; infra requested VPC peering confirmations.
- Blocker: Reserved GPU pool start date delayed due to region quota lift — ops submitted quota request; expected resolution 2 business days.
- Notes: We recommended a temporary regional fallback (multi-region routing) to avoid customer downtime during the quota lift. Owner: Julia Chen.

4) MaplePay (payments partner)
- Current phase: API contract stabilization and latency budget testing.
- This week: average p50 latency for chat flows is 92ms (good), but p99 spikes up to 1.6s during bursts.
- Blocker: Bursty upstream traffic causing hot-shard KV cache misses; observed correlation with batch scheduler window alignment.
- Mitigation: SRE to test reduced batching window for this customer route and evaluate CPU overhead. Owner: Alex G. (SRE), due 2027-02-17.

Open technical blockers (cross-cutting):
- HSM/KMS rotation timeouts during long-running sessions (affects Aster Labs). Action: SRE to patch HSM client lib and increase rotation timeout; follow-up JIRA-7187.
- Quantized model mismatch for reranker workflows (affects BlueNova). Action: Product + Applied ML to run A/B evals on 2k prompt set and publish regression artifact.
- Capacity sign-off delays for Dedicated pilots (affects Veridian). Action: Solutions Finance to expedite approvals; ticket JIRA-7132.

Decisions made:
- Temporary fallback policy approved: route to hosted mixed-precision variant if quant validation fails in canary. Policy recorded in CONFLUENCE-ops-runbook-46.
- Use staggered KMS rotation windows for affected customers until HSM client patch is rolled.

Action items (owner / due):
- JIRA-7187: Patch HSM client lib + increase rotation timeout — Alex G. / 2027-02-17.
- A/B eval for reranker quant variant and publish eval report — Priya Nair / 2027-02-18.
- Finance sign-off for Veridian Dedicated pool (JIRA-7132) — Ethan Park & Solutions Finance / 2027-02-16.
- Short-term routing change to mixed-precision model for BlueNova rerank route — Sana Patel / 2027-02-16.
- Test reduced batching window and report CPU/latency tradeoffs for MaplePay — Alex G. / 2027-02-17.

Risks and mitigations:
- Risk: If HSM timeouts persist, customers with long-lived sessions could see failed requests during rotation windows. Mitigation: stagger rotations + patch client + extend timeout; communicate planned maintenance window to customers.
- Risk: Quality regressions from quantization may slow adoption. Mitigation: maintain fallback model variants, expand prompt-based eval sets, and gate rollouts with quality SLOs.

Operational notes / FYIs:
- Capacity forecasting: two Dedicated pilot start dates are within the next 10 business days; need to confirm GPU SKU mappings to avoid mid-sprint reschedules.
- Billing: usage forecasts will be sent to Finance Wednesday for committed capacity estimates.
- Console: Add explicit per-route quality regression flag that ties to alerting in Optimize — Product/Console team to prioritize backlog item.

Next meeting: 2027-02-22 (same time) — focus on canary outcomes for BlueNova + Aster Labs pre-prod sign-off.

Appendix: quick links
- JIRA-7132 (Dedicated pool approval)

source.title: Metronome deploy playpad and fallback spectra (draft)
source.content:
Purpose:
This note captures a compact, repeatable 'metronome' rollout rhythm for medium-risk releases (service + routing + model variant changes). The goal is predictable cadence, fast detection, and graceful fallback to keep client SLAs intact while enabling measured traffic increases.

Scope:
- Applies to Hosted API and Dedicated clusters that serve customer traffic via the Gateway.
- Not for schema-only changes (those follow quick-roll path), nor for full infra replatforms.

High-level rhythm (metronome pattern):
- T+0: canary cohort (1% traffic) for 20 minutes
- T+20m: ramp to 5% for 40 minutes if all gates pass
- T+60m: ramp to 20% for 30 minutes
- T+90m: ramp to 50% for 30 minutes
- T+120m: full rollout (gradual until 100% over 2h) or pause and iterate if any gate trips

Preflight checklist (must be green before T+0):
- [ ] CI: green, no failing unit/integration tests (PR #3721)
- [ ] Perf bench: median latency delta < +10% in canary runtime profile
- [ ] Resource audit: GPU memory headroom >= 15% on candidate pools
- [ ] Config pin: model-version pinned on rollout group A
- [ ] Canary manifest uploaded to k8s: release tag, env, deploy yaml
- [ ] Observability hooks: dashboards linked, alert rules staged and set to notify deploy channel
- [ ] Runbook updated in this doc + shared runbook snapshot attached to Jira (JIRA-RELEASE-1192)
- [ ] Stakeholders notified: SRE, serving-runtime, product-owner, CS duty in rotation

Signal watchlist (primary):
- Errors: total error-rate increase > 0.5pp absolute and > 50% relative over baseline for 5m window => investigate
- Client 5xx: p95 5m > 2% => hold/rampdown
- Latency: p95 token-latency delta > 200ms sustained for 10 minutes => evaluate degrade path
- Queueing: request queue length growth > 3x baseline => throttle or increase capacity
- Throughput: tokens/sec below expected by >15% for 10m => rollback candidate
- Cost signals: GPU utilization > 90% + OOM incidents => stop rollout and revert quant settings

Concrete alert rules (examples):
- alert: CanaryErrorSpike
  expr: increase(api_errors_total{deployment=~"metronome.*"}[5m]) / increase(api_requests_total{deployment=~"metronome.*"}[5m]) > 0.02
  for: 5m
- alert: CanaryP95Jump
  expr: (histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(api_request_duration_seconds_bucket{deployment=~"metronome.*"}[5m])) by (le))) > (baseline_p95 * 1.3)
  for: 10m

Rollout decision matrix (quick):
- Green: no alerts for two consecutive windows => continue ramp
- Caution: single signal (non-critical) => pause at next step, run focused test, if source is transient continue otherwise rollback
- Critical: client-visible 5xx or p95 > 2x baseline for 10m or OOMs => immediate rollback to previous tag and trigger incident path

Fallback spectrum (ordered actions):
1) Canary abort: stop ramp, route remaining canary traffic to previous stable variant
2) Soft-fallback: switch to lighter quantized model-variant for affected region/pool (if configured)
3) Regional failover: route traffic to secondary region (costly, high friction; escalate to SRE lead)
4) Capacity fallback: fall back to Dedicated burst pool (if customer entitlement)

Communications templates (deploy channel):
- At T+0: "Metronome deploy START: tag=v2026.12-metronome, cohort=1%, owners=Ethan, Mariana. Watch: CanaryErrorSpike, CanaryP95Jump. Link: JIRA-RELEASE-1192"
- On pause: "Metronome deploy PAUSED at X% due to alert <name>. Investigating (owners: @Mariana, @Liam). Temporary traffic shifted to previous-stable. ETA: 20m."
- On rollback: "Metronome deploy ROLLBACK to tag=v2026.11 (previous). Root cause triage initiated. Pager on-call if >5 customer incidents."

Hourly rollout calendar (example timeline for a 3h controlled window):
- 00:00 T+0 start - 1% (owner on watch)
- 00:20 T+20m - ramp to 5% (if green)
- 01:00 T+60m - ramp to 20%
- 01:30 T+90m - ramp to 50%
- 02:00 T+120m - begin gradual to full (10% increments every 12m) OR stop and iterate

Notes about pacing:
- Keep a minimum stabilization period of 20m after each ramp; for latency-sensitive surfaces extend to 30m.
- Use traffic shaping by customer segment (internal testing cohort first, then low-risk free-tier users, then paid customers).

Telemetry queries to snapshot (copy to dashboard):
- Canary error rate: sum(rate(api_errors_total{tag=~"v2026.12.*",route=~"/v1/.*"}[5m])) / sum(rate(api_requests_total{tag=~"v2026.12.*",route=~"/v1/.*"}[5m]))
- Model-kv-cache hit rate: sum(rate(kv_cache_hits{pod=~"metronome.*"}[5m])) / sum(rate(kv_cache_lookups{pod=~"metronome.*"}[5m]))
- P95 token gen time: histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(token_generation_seconds_bucket{pod=~"metronome.*"}[5m])) by (le))

Operator play snippets (quick actions):
- If small error spike with low customer impact: pause ramp, clear in-flight requests by delaying next batch, run smoke-tests against canary pods (healthcheck /healthz + sample prompt set), if green resume.
- If OOMs: scale down concurrency, reduce batch size via runtime flag, revert quantization profile if recent change.
- If 5xx across regions: immediate rollback and open incident ticket; set customer comms draft (see next section).

Draft release notes (short summary to include in the release ticket / changelog):
- Title: 'metronome: routing and model variant opt for inference runtime'
- Summary: Small-step rollout of inference runtime changes and model-variant selection improvements that reduce tail latency for interactive chat and improve KV-cache hit rates on warm workloads. Rolling change includes kernel selector tweak, batching guard rails, and an optional runtime flag for conservative quantization.
- Impact: Expect modest median latency improvements (~5-12%) for mixed-length sessions. No breaking API changes. Controlled rollout; older variants remain available via pinning.
- Known issues: Under certain microburst patterns, p95 can degrade for longer sequences; we are monitoring and prepared to throttle.

Post-roll triage checklist (if any alert fired):
- [ ] Capture full 30m traces for affected timeframe (trace ids + snapshot)
- [ ] Attach deployment YAML and runtime flags to JIRA ticket
- [ ] Reproduce locally against canary image in staging with a traffic replay (traffic-replay-job-1192)
- [ ] Postmortem required if >1h degraded or >10 customers impacted

Open questions and TODOs:
- Can we automate the 'soft-fallback' swap to a quantized variant with a single toggle? (Mariana to prototype: PR#?)
- Should pacing be adjusted per-region timezone traffic shapes (APAC morning spike)? Proposal due: 2027-01-07 (Liam)
- Add a smoke prompt set for Dedicated customers that exercises long-sequence tail cases (Zoe to draft prompts)

Quick links and references:
- Runtime PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/runtime/pull/3721
- Canary runbook template: CONFLUENCE-PL-4823
- Incident template: CONFLUENCE-INC-33

Notes to self (informal):
- Keep this doc lean; engineers tend to ignore long prose. The metronome metaphor helps pacing; pair with a simple timer in the deploy channel.
- When in doubt, favor rollback over slow degradation: customer trust > single-deploy velocity.

Revision history:
- 2026-12-03 initial draft (Ethan)
- 2026-12-07 added fallback spectrum and operator play snippets (Mariana)
- 2026-12-10 tuned alerts and added release notes draft (Liam)

-- end --

- Queueing: request queue length growth > 3x baseline => throttle or increase capacity
- Throughput: tokens/sec below expected by >15% for 10m => rollback candidate
- Cost signals: GPU utilization > 90% + OOM incidents => stop rollout and revert quant settings

Concrete alert rules (examples):
- alert: CanaryErrorSpike
  expr: increase(api_errors_total{deployment=~\"metronome.*\"}[5m]) / increase(api_requests_total{deployment=~\"metronome.*\"}[5m]) > 0.02
  for: 5m
- alert: CanaryP95Jump
  expr: (histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(api_request_duration_seconds_bucket{deployment=~\"metronome.*\"}[5m])) by (le))) > (baseline_p95 * 1.3)
  for: 10m

Rollout decision matrix (quick):
- Green: no alerts for two consecutive windows => continue ramp
- Caution: single signal (non-critical) => pause at next step, run focused test, if source is transient continue otherwise rollback
- Critical: client-visible 5xx or p95 > 2x baseline for 10m or OOMs => immediate rollback to previous tag and trigger incident path

Fallback spectrum (ordered actions):
1) Canary abort: stop ramp, route remaining canary traffic to previous stable variant
2) Soft-fallback: switch to lighter quantized model-variant for affected region/pool (if configured)
3) Regional failover: route traffic to secondary region (costly, high friction; escalate to SRE lead)
4) Capacity fallback: fall back to Dedicated burst pool (if customer entitlement)

Communications templates (deploy channel):
- At T+0: \"Metronome deploy START: tag=v2026.12-metronome, cohort=1%, owners=Ethan, Mariana. Watch: CanaryErrorSpike, CanaryP95Jump. Link: JIRA-RELEASE-1192\"
- On pause: \"Metronome deploy PAUSED at X% due to alert <name>. Investigating (owners: @Mariana, @Liam). Temporary traffic shifted to previous-stable. ETA: 20m.\"
- On rollback: \"Metronome deploy ROLLBACK to tag=v2026.11 (previous). Root cause triage initiated. Pager on-call if >5 customer incidents.\"

Hourly rollout calendar (example timeline for a 3h controlled window):
- 00:00 T+0 start - 1% (owner on watch)
- 00:20 T+20m - ramp to 5% (if green)
- 01:00 T+60m - ramp to 20%
- 01:30 T+90m - ramp to 50%
- 02:00 T+120m - begin gradual to full (10% increments every 12m) OR stop and iterate

Notes about pacing:
- Keep a minimum stabilization period of 20m after each ramp; for latency-sensitive surfaces extend to 30m.
- Use traffic shaping by customer segment (internal testing cohort first, then low-risk free-tier users, then paid customers).

Telemetry queries to snapshot (copy to dashboard):
- Canary error rate: sum(rate(api_errors_total{tag=~\"v2026.12.*\",route=~\"/v1/.*\"}[5m])) / sum(rate(api_requests_total{tag=~\"v2026.12.*\",route=~\"/v1/.*\"}[5m]))
- Model-kv-cache hit rate: sum(rate(kv_cache_hits{pod=~\"metronome.*\"}[5m])) / sum(rate(kv_cache_lookups{pod=~\"metronome.*\"}[5m]))
- P95 token gen time: histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(token_generation_seconds_bucket{pod=~\"metronome.*\"}[5m])) by (le))

Operator play snippets (quick actions):
- If small error spike with low customer impact: pause ramp, clear in-flight requests by delaying next batch, run smoke-tests against canary pods (healthcheck /healthz + sample prompt set), if green resume.
- If OOMs: scale down concurrency, reduce batch size via runtime flag, revert quantization profile if recent change.
- If 5xx across regions: immediate rollback and open incident ticket; set customer comms draft (see next section).

Draft release notes (short summary to include in the release ticket / changelog):
- Title: 'metronome: routing and model variant opt for inference runtime'
- Summary: Small-step rollout of inference runtime changes and model-variant selection improvements that reduce tail latency for interactive chat and improve KV-cache hit rates on warm workloads. Rolling change includes kernel selector tweak, batching guard rails, and an optional runtime flag for conservative quantization.
- Impact: Expect modest median latency improvements (~5-12%) for mixed-length sessions. No breaking API changes. Controlled rollout; older variants remain available via pinning.
- Known issues: Under certain microburst patterns, p95 can degrade for longer sequences; we are monitoring and prepared to throttle.

Post-roll triage checklist (if any alert fired):
- [ ] Capture full 30m traces for affected timeframe (trace ids + snapshot)
- [ ] Attach deployment YAML and runtime flags to JIRA ticket
- [ ] Reproduce locally against canary image in staging with a traffic replay (traffic-replay-job-1192)
- [ ] Postmortem required if >1h degraded or >10 customers impacted

Open questions and TODOs:
- Can we automate the 'soft-fallback' swap to a quantized variant with a single toggle? (Mariana to prototype: PR#?)
- Should pacing be adjusted per-region timezone traffic shapes (APAC morning spike)? Proposal due: 2027-01-07 (Liam)
- Add a smoke prompt set for Dedicated customers that exercises long-sequence tail cases (Zoe to draft prompts)

Quick links and references:
- Runtime PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/runtime/pull/3721
- Canary runbook template: CONFLUENCE-PL-4823
- Incident template: CONFLUENCE-INC-33

Notes to self (informal):
- Keep this doc lean; engineers tend to ignore long prose. The metronome metaphor helps pacing; pair with a simple timer in the deploy channel.
- When in doubt, favor rollback over slow degradation: customer trust > single-deploy velocity.

Revision history:
- 2026-12-03 initial draft (Ethan)
- 2026-12-07 added fallback spectrum and operator play snippets (Mariana)
- 2026-12-10 tuned alerts and added release notes draft (Liam)

-- end --
text:
Purpose:
This note captures a compact, repeatable 'metronome' rollout rhythm for medium-risk releases (service + routing + model variant changes). The goal is predictable cadence, fast detection, and graceful fallback to keep client SLAs intact while enabling measured traffic increases.

Scope:
- Applies to Hosted API and Dedicated clusters that serve customer traffic via the Gateway.
- Not for schema-only changes (those follow quick-roll path), nor for full infra replatforms.

High-level rhythm (metronome pattern):
- T+0: canary cohort (1% traffic) for 20 minutes
- T+20m: ramp to 5% for 40 minutes if all gates pass
- T+60m: ramp to 20% for 30 minutes
- T+90m: ramp to 50% for 30 minutes
- T+120m: full rollout (gradual until 100% over 2h) or pause and iterate if any gate trips

Preflight checklist (must be green before T+0):
- [ ] CI: green, no failing unit/integration tests (PR #3721)
- [ ] Perf bench: median latency delta < +10% in canary runtime profile
- [ ] Resource audit: GPU memory headroom >= 15% on candidate pools
- [ ] Config pin: model-version pinned on rollout group A
- [ ] Canary manifest uploaded to k8s: release tag, env, deploy yaml
- [ ] Observability hooks: dashboards linked, alert rules staged and set to notify deploy channel
- [ ] Runbook updated in this doc + shared runbook snapshot attached to Jira (JIRA-RELEASE-1192)
- [ ] Stakeholders notified: SRE, serving-runtime, product-owner, CS duty in rotation

Signal watchlist (primary):

source.title: Late-cycle batch surge debrief
source.content:
Summary:

This doc captures the post-mortem style debrief from the late-cycle batch surge we saw during the 02:00–03:00 UTC window on 2026-11-07. The event manifested as a sustained increase in p99 latency (from ~210ms to ~820ms) and a 16% drop in aggregate throughput for the hosted API tier serving the mixed-chat workload. The behavior correlated with transient KV cache thrashing and kernel scheduling stalls on a subset of GPU nodes.

Who attended:
- Ethan Park (author, oncall)
- Lina Gomez (batching owner)
- Priya Desai (kernels)
- Ravi Menon (SRE)
- Zoe Chen (observability)
- Additional notes from night oncall slack thread #ops-serve-runtime

Timeline and observed signals:
- 02:07 UTC: First automated alert — rolling p99 breach for route /v1/chat (hosted) on region us-west-2.
- 02:09 UTC: Alerts expand to multiple nodes; concurrent increase in 'kernel-sched-queue-length' on nodes a03-a07.
- 02:10–02:20 UTC: KV cache hit-rate drops from 92% to 58% on the affected cohort, followed by active evictions rising 3x (eviction spikes concentrated on short-lived sessions with many context-switches).
- 02:13–02:27 UTC: Periodic microbursts observed — short 10–30s periods with sharp drop in answered tokens/second, then partial recovery.
- 02:31 UTC: SRE rerouted a small percentage of traffic to standby pool (policy: cost-aware fallback). This reduced tail but did not fully return p99 to baseline.
- 02:45 UTC: We saw correlation between a new internal experiment (shadow-sampler variant rolled at 01:30 UTC, PR referenced above) and the start of the anomalies.

Root-cause hypotheses (ranked):
1) Kernel scheduling congestion caused by high churn in KV cache shadow-sampler combined with aggressive micro-batching heuristics. Evidence: nodes with the shadow-sampler enabled show 2.4x higher kernel context-switch counts and the timing aligns with the experiment rollout.
2) KV cache metadata thrash: small sessions with lots of short-lived prefixes produced many tiny cache entries, causing eviction flapping and expensive recompute of prefills. Evidence: average prefill latencies for affected keys increased from ~12ms to ~74ms.
3) Batching edge-case: the micro-batcher’s requeue logic creates hot-path contention when many queues flip between 'eligible' and 'stalled' in a short window. Evidence: queue flip logs show many queues entering/exiting eligibility within <50ms windows.

Repro and tests performed during incident:
- Replayed synthetic workload (session-length distribution matched to production spike) against a canary pool with shadow-sampler toggled. With sampler ON, kernel-sched-queue-length and p99 climbed — reproducible across 3/3 runs.
- Disabled microbatch requeue optimization in a sandbox — reduced queue flip frequency and smoothed prefills but increased average time-to-batch slightly.
- Heap profiles and kernel logs extracted (links in linked_artifacts) showed increased lock contention in kernel scheduler path compute_queue_next().

Immediate mitigations (short-lived):
- Rollback: the shadow-sampler experiment was rolled back at 03:20 UTC and the standby pool weights restored. This brought p99 back toward baseline over ~18 minutes.
- Emergency patch (PR 1284): throttle queue flip frequency by adding a 50ms debounce on microbatch eligibility transitions; deployed to canary and then to affected pool. This softened sharp oscillations.

Longer-term action plan (owners and dates):
- Priya: Kernel scheduler refactor spike (SRV-4721). Goal: reduce lock granularity in compute_queue_next(); prototype by 2026-11-22.
- Lina: Batcher requeue redesign (SRV-4729). Goal: replace flip-based eligibility with token-priority buckets to avoid oscillation. Design doc due 2026-11-18, prototype 2026-12-05.
- Ravi: KV cache policy audit and small-entry aggregation. Goal: implement a lightweight coalescing layer that groups short-lived prefixes for eviction accounting. Proposal 2026-11-16; experiment 2026-12-02.
- Zoe: Observability improvements — add a derived metric "queue-flip-rate" and per-node kernel sched lock time. Metric on dashboards by 2026-11-12.
- Oncall playbook update: add explicit abort condition for shadow experiments if p99 > 2x baseline for >10 min across >5 nodes. Draft updated by 2026-11-11 (Ethan).

Design notes and tradeoffs discussed:
- Debounce vs batching latency: we observed the 50ms debounce significantly reduces oscillation but adds a small extra latency for first-token batches. Tradeoff acceptable for hosted-api where p99 matters more than minimal p50 gains. For Dedicated customers with hard throughput SLAs we may want config knobs.
- KV coalescing complexity: grouping short prefixes reduces eviction churn but increases memory tracking complexity and may mask root causes for some workloads. Start with opt-in experiment.
- Kernel lock refactor: bigger code churn, requires careful stress testing on multi-tenant hosts. Plan to run extended soak across low-traffic regions.

Open questions and unresolved items:
- Why did the shadow-sampler variant amplify churn so much in production but not in pre-prod? Possible differences in session length distribution and queue load; need larger-scale pre-prod traffic replay. (Action: Lina + QA to run a 24h replay by 2026-11-17.)
- Is there a better way to detect micro-oscillation than current heuristics? (Action: Zoe to prototype machine-detection rule using queue-flip-rate + kernel-lock-time.)

Follow-ups (short checklist):
- [ ] Push SRV-4721 kernel-refactor RFC and assign reviewers (Priya) — due 2026-11-15.
- [ ] Batch requeue design doc (Lina) — due 2026-11-18.
- [ ] Add queue-flip-rate metric and update alerts (Zoe) — due 2026-11-12.
- [ ] Run 24h traffic replay in pre-prod with shadow-sampler config (Lina + QA) — run by 2026-11-17.
- [ ] Draft updated experiments abort policy in oncall playbook (Ethan) — due 2026-11-11.

Appendix (observability excerpts):
- p99 latency timeline (us-west-2, /v1/chat): baseline 210ms -> peak 820ms, recovery starting at 02:31 UTC.
- KV hit-rate on affected nodes: 92% -> 58% during burst.
- Kernel sched lock hold-time: median 0.6ms -> 4.8ms.
- Queue flip rate: baseline ~1/s -> 18/s during microbursts.

Notes to reviewers:
- This is intentionally pragmatic — the immediate rollback and the debounce patch mitigated user-visible impact. The larger refactors are non-trivial and will take cross-team coordination. Please add comments inline for design tradeoffs and potential regressions to watch for.

Historical context: we have seen similar small-scale oscillation events in 2026 Q1 when we first shipped microbatch requeue optimizations. Those were addressed with minor batching rule adjustments; this incident exposed a different amplification path (shadow experiments interacting with short-lived sessions).

If you want to run the reproduction locally I left the synthetic driver in rrt/tests/batch_surge_sim (see linked PR).

3) Batching edge-case: the micro-batcher’s requeue logic creates hot-path contention when many queues flip between 'eligible' and 'stalled' in a short window. Evidence: queue flip logs show many queues entering/exiting eligibility within <50ms windows.

Repro and tests performed during incident:
- Replayed synthetic workload (session-length distribution matched to production spike) against a canary pool with shadow-sampler toggled. With sampler ON, kernel-sched-queue-length and p99 climbed — reproducible across 3/3 runs.
- Disabled microbatch requeue optimization in a sandbox — reduced queue flip frequency and smoothed prefills but increased average time-to-batch slightly.
- Heap profiles and kernel logs extracted (links in linked_artifacts) showed increased lock contention in kernel scheduler path compute_queue_next().

Immediate mitigations (short-lived):
- Rollback: the shadow-sampler experiment was rolled back at 03:20 UTC and the standby pool weights restored. This brought p99 back toward baseline over ~18 minutes.
- Emergency patch (PR 1284): throttle queue flip frequency by adding a 50ms debounce on microbatch eligibility transitions; deployed to canary and then to affected pool. This softened sharp oscillations.

Longer-term action plan (owners and dates):
- Priya: Kernel scheduler refactor spike (SRV-4721). Goal: reduce lock granularity in compute_queue_next(); prototype by 2026-11-22.
- Lina: Batcher requeue redesign (SRV-4729). Goal: replace flip-based eligibility with token-priority buckets to avoid oscillation. Design doc due 2026-11-18, prototype 2026-12-05.
- Ravi: KV cache policy audit and small-entry aggregation. Goal: implement a lightweight coalescing layer that groups short-lived prefixes for eviction accounting. Proposal 2026-11-16; experiment 2026-12-02.
- Zoe: Observability improvements — add a derived metric "queue-flip-rate" and per-node kernel sched lock time. Metric on dashboards by 2026-11-12.
- Oncall playbook update: add explicit abort condition for shadow experiments if p99 > 2x baseline for >10 min across >5 nodes. Draft updated by 2026-11-11 (Ethan).

Design notes and tradeoffs discussed:
- Debounce vs batching latency: we observed the 50ms debounce significantly reduces oscillation but adds a small extra latency for first-token batches. Tradeoff acceptable for hosted-api where p99 matters more than minimal p50 gains. For Dedicated customers with hard throughput SLAs we may want config knobs.
- KV coalescing complexity: grouping short prefixes reduces eviction churn but increases memory tracking complexity and may mask root causes for some workloads. Start with opt-in experiment.
- Kernel lock refactor: bigger code churn, requires careful stress testing on multi-tenant hosts. Plan to run extended soak across low-traffic regions.

Open questions and unresolved items:
- Why did the shadow-sampler variant amplify churn so much in production but not in pre-prod? Possible differences in session length distribution and queue load; need larger-scale pre-prod traffic replay. (Action: Lina + QA to run a 24h replay by 2026-11-17.)
- Is there a better way to detect micro-oscillation than current heuristics? (Action: Zoe to prototype machine-detection rule using queue-flip-rate + kernel-lock-time.)

Follow-ups (short checklist):
- [ ] Push SRV-4721 kernel-refactor RFC and assign reviewers (Priya) — due 2026-11-15.
- [ ] Batch requeue design doc (Lina) — due 2026-11-18.
- [ ] Add queue-flip-rate metric and update alerts (Zoe) — due 2026-11-12.
- [ ] Run 24h traffic replay in pre-prod with shadow-sampler config (Lina + QA) — run by 2026-11-17.
- [ ] Draft updated experiments abort policy in oncall playbook (Ethan) — due 2026-11-11.

Appendix (observability excerpts):
- p99 latency timeline (us-west-2, /v1/chat): baseline 210ms -> peak 820ms, recovery starting at 02:31 UTC.
- KV hit-rate on affected nodes: 92% -> 58% during burst.
- Kernel sched lock hold-time: median 0.6ms -> 4.8ms.
- Queue flip rate: baseline ~1/s -> 18/s during microbursts.

Notes to reviewers:
- This is intentionally pragmatic — the immediate rollback and the debounce patch mitigated user-visible impact. The larger refactors are non-trivial and will take cross-team coordination. Please add comments inline for design tradeoffs and potential regressions to watch for.

Historical context: we have seen similar small-scale oscillation events in 2026 Q1 when we first shipped microbatch requeue optimizations. Those were addressed with minor batching rule adjustments; this incident exposed a different amplification path (shadow experiments interacting with short-lived sessions).

If you want to run the reproduction locally I left the synthetic driver in rrt/tests/batch_surge_sim (see linked PR).

source.title: Queue depth and KV cache pressure data extract (Oct 2026)
source.summary: Working data pull and initial analysis for a capacity incident observed across multiple regions in early Oct 2026. Focused on queue depth, per-request KV cache hit/miss pressure, and how long-tail sequence lengths drove scheduler backlog. This is an exploratory extract for the postmortem; queries, sample outputs, and recommended next steps included.
source.objective: Generate a compact dataset that surfaces the relationship between queue depth spikes and KV cache pressure, split by region, model_family, and request length buckets. Provide sample aggregates for the postmortem timeline and an actionable query set infra can reuse.
source.notes: This is intentionally a compact extract (few columns) to make triage fast. Downstream slices (per-instance traces, full token logs) are linked in the Confluence artifacts. Some fields are derived (see queries). Expect sampling bias: we only pulled requests that hit the high-latency SLO (>300ms) and/or were queued >10s during the incident windows.
source.key_findings:
1) Queue depth spikes aligned with sharp increases in kv_misses and avg_kv_mem_mb for model_family=redwood-7b in us-east between 14:00-15:00 UTC on 2026-10-05.
2) Long requests (>=512 tokens) contributed disproportionately to scheduler backlog in eu-west and increased p90 queue times even when absolute queued_requests was moderate.
3) KV cache mem pressure tended to precede queue depth by 1-3 minutes in multiple regions, suggesting eviction thrashing.
4) ap-southeast showed lower absolute queue counts but higher variance in avg_queue_ms, consistent with regional cold-starts and smaller pool sizing.
source.observations_and_hypotheses:
- Hypothesis A: Large long-tail generations populate KV cache with large token keys and cause eviction cycles, increasing misses for subsequent requests that then get queued.
- Hypothesis B: Autoscaler reacted too slowly for redwood-7b due to conservative CPU headroom threshold; autoscale lag + high miss rate produced transient hotspots.
- Hypothesis C: Routing policy directed new traffic to a subset of racks due to regional weights, concentrating KV pressure onto fewer nodes.
source.visuals_to_generate:
- Time-series: queued_requests (per-minute) vs kv_misses (per-minute) overlayed per model_family+region.
- Heatmap: p90_queue_ms by region (x-axis) vs model_family (y-axis) for incident window.
- Scatter: avg_kv_mem_mb vs avg_queue_ms with point size = queued_requests.
- Stacked area: request length buckets (short/medium/long) contribution to queued_requests over time.
source.action_items:
- (Infra) Add a lightweight eviction metric flag exposing per-node eviction_count/min to telemetry (owner: Noah).
- (Serving) Add a temporary routing soft-fail: spread new redwood-7b traffic across additional racks if kv_miss_rate > 30% (owner: Priya).
- (SRE) Backfill a 24-hour detailed trace for 2026-10-05 13:55-15:10 UTC for top 50 request ids (owner: Ethan).
- (Product) Gate large-generation sample rate for low-tier hosts to reduce long-tail pressure while we tune autoscaler (owner: Ivy).
source.next_steps_and_questions:
- Validate whether eviction_count correlates with kv_miss spikes (need new metric).
- Determine why autoscaler thresholds didn't trigger earlier — look into scale policies for redwood-7b pool (ticket: INF-4839).
- Confirm client-side retry/backoff behavior during the incident window: did many clients retry immediately? (linked: INF-4816).
source.risks_and_limitations:
- This extract samples only requests with queue_ms > 100ms or that contributed to high-latency SLO; it is not representative of steady-state traffic.
- Downsampling of token stream stats was enabled for privacy; exact token distributions may be biased.
- Memory estimates for KV (avg_kv_mem_mb) are derived from periodic node reports and may lag spikes by up to 30s.
source.appendix_queries_and_notes:
-- Quick snippet: top offending request ids by combined queue_ms * token_count 
SELECT request_id, region, model_family, sum(queue_ms) AS total_queue_ms, max(total_tokens) AS total_tokens, sum(queue_ms)*max(total_tokens) AS score FROM scheduler.request_queue_metrics_v2 WHERE minute_ts BETWEEN '2026-10-05 13:50:00' AND '2026-10-05 15:15:00' GROUP BY request_id, region, model_family ORDER BY score DESC LIMIT 200;

-- Note: when exporting full traces, use the tracing export job id: telemetry-export-2026-10-12-07.

-- Quick checklist for re-runs: 1) Ensure request.token_stream_stats sampling flag disabled; 2) extend time window +- 30m around incident; 3) run with GROUP BY model_variant to surface custom-client variants.

source.title: Ad-hoc Convergence Grid - Sep 2026
source.body:
Purpose:
Quick, low-friction grid to converge on outstanding one-off asks, cross-team handoffs, and temporary capacity adjustments for the next 2 sprints. Meant to be copied into team backlogs and updated in meeting. Not a replacement for full project plans.

How to use:
- Owner updates their row before the daily 09:30 standup.
- If something moves to a multi-week effort, link a ticket and change status to "in_review" or "escalated".
- Use the "heat" column to indicate immediate attention (H/M/L).

Column headers (flat table):
ID | Initiative / Ask | Owner (team) | Heat | ETA | Status | Blocker short | Quick next step | Est effort (days)

Rows (most recent first):
1 | Emergency routing fallback for EU region (customer outage) | Lena Rodriguez (SRE) | H | 2026-09-06 | in_progress | limited spare GPUs in eu-west-2 | spin 2 temporary nodes + reroute to us-west as fallback | 1-2
2 | Ad-hoc billing dispute export (enterprise finance) | Priya Nair (Finance) | H | 2026-09-08 | in_progress | missing request logs from API gateway | ask infra for 48h log retention snapshot | 0.5
3 | Quick quantization sanity check for p3-compatible variant | Marcus Wu (Applied ML) | M | 2026-09-14 | draft | environment mismatch for CUDA drivers | schedule 1h test with infra images | 2
4 | Customer demo dataset purge request (privacy) | Jordan Kim (Customer Success) | H | 2026-09-07 | in_progress | legal needs confirmation on retention policy | legal lead to confirm PII scope by EOD | 0.25
5 | Temporary routing policy for low-latency tier during conference | Ethan Park (Product Ops) | M | 2026-09-12 | planned | capacity reserve not finalized | finalize commit with Dedicated team | 1
6 | Quick eval: streaming chunk-size perf check | Anya Feldman (Observability) | L | 2026-09-18 | draft | no representative replay data | pull last 24h sample traces | 1
7 | Small docs refresh: public quickstart snippet for private deploys | Samir Desai (DX) | L | 2026-09-20 | planned | waiting on final CLI flag naming | finalize flag name, update snippet | 0.5

Counts snapshot (manual):
- Total rows: 7
- High heat: 3
- Medium heat: 2
- Low heat: 2

Immediate actions (today):
- Lena: confirm temporary node sizes and list of services to reroute by 09:00 (blocking customer impact).
- Priya: post exact time window needed for logs to #finance-ops and tag infra.
- Marcus: attach the failing container logs to JIRA-ML-334 and request matching driver image.

Notes & context (scratchpad):
- This grid started as a 6-row sprint-of requests and keeps growing; want to avoid turning into another backlog. If an item requires >3 days effort, create a ticket and move it out.
- Cross-team handoffs that have unresolved blockers should be escalated in the weekly ops sync (Tues 15:00).
- Privacy/legal items need explicit documented sign-off; do not proceed on purge until legal reply is in thread.

Open questions / items to clarify:
- Do we have a policy for preemptively reserving 10% of Dedicated capacity for events? (Product Ops + Dedicated team decision)
- Can infra provide a 48h emergency log snapshot for billing disputes? If not, what's the alternative?
- Should small docs updates be owned by DX or Product Docs? (Samir flagged ambiguous ownership)

Changelog (brief):
- 2026-09-05: grid created (Ethan)
- 2026-09-06: Lena added EU routing row after incident
- 2026-09-07: Priya updated billing row with additional blocker detail

Quick paste-friendly export (CSV-ish lines):
"1,EU fallback,Lena Rodriguez (SRE),H,2026-09-06,in_progress,limited spare GPUs,spin 2 nodes + reroute,1-2"
"2,Billing export,Priya Nair (Finance),H,2026-09-08,in_progress,missing API logs,ask infra for snapshot,0.5"
"3,Quant check,Marcus Wu (Applied ML),M,2026-09-14,draft,CUDA mismatch,schedule 1h test,2"

Notes to self: keep this doc light and time-bound. Archive to /archive/monthly when >2 weeks old unless ownership transfers to a tracked Jira ticket.

-- Ethan
