source.title: Reservation fairshare tiling — per-contract apportionment (Aug 2026)
source.content:
Status: draft - internal working sheet for tiling / fairshare apportionment across Dedicated customer contracts. Use this as the canonical snapshot for Aug-2026 allocation decisions.

High-level goal:
- Produce a per-contract, per-region allocation that respects reserved commitments, regional affinity, and a global fairshare/burst pool for overage. This sheet is intentionally conservative on cross-region bursting until network egress/costs are modeled.

Assumptions (baseline used for numbers below):
- Fleet size (active for Dedicated): US-WEST: 480 A100-80G, US-EAST: 240 A100-80G, EU-WEST: 160 A100-80G. (Total 880 GPUs earmarked for Dedicated customers).
- Typical sustained util target for reserved capacity: 65% across a month (gives headroom for peaks).
- Tiling factor defaults: 1.0 for 1:1 reservations, 0.85 for historical low-util contracts, 1.25 for burstable-rated customers. Tiling factor applied to reserved GPUs to compute active slot allocation.
- Fairshare pool: 10% of regional capacity is held for burst/ondemand and special rollouts.
- Minimum allocation granularity: 1 GPU per contract per region.

Legend / column defs used in table below:
- Contract: internal short id
- Customer: public-friendly name (obfuscated)
- Reserved_GPUs: committed GPUs in contract (monthly average)
- Pref_region: preferred deployment region(s)
- Tiling_factor: multiplier to convert reserved->active slots (reflects packing / multitask efficiency)
- Requested_active_slots = floor(Reserved_GPUs * Tiling_factor)
- Regional_cap: regional pool before fairshare (see fleet by region)
- Allocated_slots: final slots assigned in region (<= Requested_active_slots)
- Monthly_committed_hours: Reserved_GPUs * 24 * 30 (simple approximation)
- Uplift_notes: any local constraints, KMS, network, VPC needs

Fleet by region (earmarked for Dedicated, baseline):
Region, Physical_GPUs, Fairshare_pool(10%), Usable_for_allocations
US-WEST, 480, 48, 432
US-EAST, 240, 24, 216
EU-WEST, 160, 16, 144

Per-contract requested allocation (raw) — input from account/solutions (snapshot 2026-08-12):
Contract, Customer, Reserved_GPUs, Pref_region, Tiling_factor, Requested_active_slots, Monthly_committed_hours, Notes
C-1934, NimbusHealth, 120, US-WEST, 1.00, 120, 86400, HIPAA + private VPC (requires dedicated subnet)
C-2011, LexaSearch, 80, US-WEST, 1.25, 100, 57600, batch-heavy: can tolerate lower per-request latency
C-2078, FinSight, 60, US-EAST, 0.85, 51, 43200, encryption-in-use, prefers US-EAST for data locality
C-2122, NovaAssist, 40, EU-WEST, 1.00, 40, 28800, EU data residency required
C-2177, AtlasRetail, 150, multi (pref US-WEST/US-EAST), 1.00, 150, 108000, global footprint, flexible region but prefers US-WEST
C-2213, KernelDocs, 24, US-WEST, 0.85, 20, 17280, low sustained util historically
C-2244, AuroraMedia, 60, US-EAST, 1.25, 75, 43200, spiky streaming inference; burst candidate
C-2299, SoteriaAI, 96, EU-WEST, 1.00, 96, 69120, regulatory audit in progress
C-2330, TerraMaps, 36, US-WEST, 1.00, 36, 25920, batch geospatial jobs
C-2390, Enclave, 12, US-EAST, 1.25, 15, 8640, small reserved + burst profile

Raw totals (requested_active_slots) by region (sum of requested slots for contracts that prefer that region):
US-WEST requested raw = NimbusHealth(120) + LexaSearch(100) + AtlasRetail(150, pref multi but counts) + KernelDocs(20) + TerraMaps(36) = 426
US-EAST requested raw = FinSight(51) + AuroraMedia(75) + Enclave(15) + AtlasRetail(150, pref multi but flex->US-EAST counted partially) = 291 (note: AtlasRetail split assumed 50/50 in initial pass -> 75 to US-EAST)
EU-WEST requested raw = NovaAssist(40) + SoteriaAI(96) = 136

Allocation strategy and adjustments applied (manual step-by-step notes):
1) Reserve fairshare pool from each region (10%): US-WEST usable 432, US-EAST usable 216, EU-WEST usable 144.
2) Apply hard-preference contracts first (NovaAssist -> EU-WEST, FinSight -> US-EAST, Nimbus -> US-WEST, KernelDocs -> US-WEST, TerraMaps -> US-WEST, SoteriaAI -> EU-WEST).
3) For flexible/multi-pref contracts (AtlasRetail), attempt 50/50 split by default then adjust to region headroom and network cost.
4) If per-region requested > usable, scale back flexible and lower-tiling contracts first (priority: keep contracts with compliance or SLA higher).

First-pass allocations (manual pro-rated where necessary):
EU-WEST: usable 144, requested 136 -> fits. Allocations:
- NovaAssist: allocated 40 (full)
- SoteriaAI: allocated 96 (full)
Remaining EU-WEST headroom after allocations: 8 GPUs (moved to fairshare top-up).

US-EAST: usable 216, requested 291 (oversubscribe by 75) -> need scale-down. Priority order: FinSight (compliance), AuroraMedia (burst candidate), Enclave (small + burst), AtlasRetail(partial).
- FinSight: allocated 51 (full) -> remaining usable 165
- AuroraMedia: allocate 60 of 75 (scaleback of 15); rationale: burst eligible but large sustained would pressure pool -> remaining usable 105
- Enclave: allocate 12 of 15 (scaleback 3) -> remaining usable 93
- AtlasRetail: originally planned 75 -> scale to 75? But we need to preserve balanced across regions. Allocate 55 to US-EAST and move 20 to US-WEST. After that US-EAST remaining usable 38 (reserve 38 into fairshare).
Note: scaling choices biased to keep compliance/enterprise customers at full allocation.

US-WEST: usable 432, requested raw 426 but note AtlasRetail reallocation + LexaSearch etc -> effective requested = 426 + 20 (from Atlas moved) = 446 -> still fits within 432? 446 > 432 (oversubscribe by 14). Apply scale-down on tiling-sensitive / lower SLA customers: KernelDocs, TerraMaps, LexaSearch adjustable.
- NimbusHealth: allocated 120 (full) -> remaining 312
- LexaSearch: allocate 92 of 100 (scaledown 8) -> remaining 220
- AtlasRetail (moved 20): allocate 20 -> remaining 200
- KernelDocs: allocate 18 of 20 (scaledown 2) -> remaining 182
- TerraMaps: allocate 36 (full) -> remaining 146
Remaining US-WEST headroom (146) -> move 10 GPUs to global fairshare for cross-region burst, leaving 136 for local short-term burst and admin.

Final per-contract Allocated_slots summary:
C-1934 NimbusHealth: 120 (US-WEST)
C-2011 LexaSearch: 92 (US-WEST)
C-2078 FinSight: 51 (US-EAST)
C-2122 NovaAssist: 40 (EU-WEST)
C-2177 AtlasRetail: 75 (US-WEST 45 / US-EAST 30)  [note: earlier split adjusted to 45/30 due to headroom]
C-2213 KernelDocs: 18 (US-WEST)
C-2244 AuroraMedia: 60 (US-EAST)
C-2299 SoteriaAI: 96 (EU-WEST)
C-2330 TerraMaps: 36 (US-WEST)
C-2390 Enclave: 12 (US-EAST)

Total allocated by region after adjustments:
US-WEST allocated = 120 + 92 + 45 + 18 + 36 = 311
US-EAST allocated = 51 + 30 + 60 + 12 = 153
EU-WEST allocated = 40 + 96 = 136
Fairshare pools leftover: US-WEST usable headroom 121 (136 local burst - reserved 15 administrative), US-EAST 63, EU-WEST 8

Risk notes and caveats:
- We bumped several tiling factors as a manual heuristic; need a follow-up calc to simulate per-minute latency SLO impact for multi-tenant packing.
- Cross-region egress/costs not modeled. Moving AtlasRetail capacity cross-region may incur non-trivial egress on customer data flows. Solutions ENG to confirm.
- Burst fairness: current simple 10% fairshare may be insufficient for heavy spiky customers (AuroraMedia). Consider temporary burst credits or scheduled time windows.
- Some contracts (e.g., NimbusHealth) require private subnets and KMS constraints; those GPUs must be on nodes with correct networking—coord with infra-provisioning.

Open action items (owners):
- Calculate minute-level batching implications for LexaSearch and LexaSearch PRD (Ava Chen) — target 2026-08-25.
- Confirm egress pricing impact for moving AtlasRetail workloads between US-WEST and US-EAST (Diego Alvarez) — target 2026-08-18.
- Add per-node KMS label propagation to infra builder for NimbusHealth (Maya Patel) — target 2026-08-22.
- Run synthetic pack-sim using August traffic traces to validate tiling factors (eng-sre) — link to job: JIRA-INFRA-3291.

Appendix: quick snapshot calc examples used in sheet
- Monthly_committed_hours (simple): Reserved_GPUs * 24 * 30. Example: NimbusHealth 120 * 24 * 30 = 86,400
- Requested_active_slots (example): floor(80 * 1.25) = 100 for LexaSearch
- Regional scaling: when requested > usable, reduce allocations by prioritizing non-compliance customers and those with lower historical active utilization.

Notes to reviewers:
- This is a working allocation. Please comment inline on the Confluence design page (linked) with objections to scaling choices; we will lock allocations for the Sep-2026 provisioning run on 2026-08-26.
- If you are a Solutions/Account lead and see your customer under-allocated relative to contract, add a short note in the Confluence page and tag eng-infra leads.

End of snapshot.

title: Reservation fairshare tiling — per-contract apportionment (Aug 2026)
preview:
Status: draft - internal working sheet for tiling / fairshare apportionment across Dedicated customer contracts. Use this as the canonical snapshot for Aug-2026 allocation decisions.

High-level goal:
- Produce a per-contract, per-region allocation that respects reserved commitments, regional affinity, and a global fairshare/burst pool for overage. This sheet is intentionally conservative on cross-region bursting until network egress/costs are modeled.

Assumptions (baseline used for numbers below):
- Fleet size (active for Dedicated): US-WEST: 480 A100-80G, US-EAST: 240 A100-80G, EU-WEST: 160 A100-80G. (Total 880 GPUs earmarked for Dedicated customers).
- Typical sustained util target for reserved capacity: 65% across a month (gives headroom for peaks).
- Tiling factor defaults: 1.0 for 1:1 reservations, 0.85 for historical low-util contracts, 1.25 for burstable-rated customers. Tiling factor applied to reserved GPUs to compute active slot allocation.
- Fairshare pool: 10% of regional capacity is held for burst/ondemand and special rollouts.
- Minimum allocation granularity: 1 GPU per contract per region.

Legend / column defs used in table below:
- Contract: internal short id
- Customer: public-friendly name (obfuscated)
- Reserved_GPUs: committed GPUs in contract (monthly average)
- Pref_region: preferred deployment region(s)
- Tiling_factor: multiplier to convert reserved->active slots (reflects packing / multitask efficiency)
- Requested_active_slots = floor(Reserved_GPUs * Tiling_factor)
- Regional_cap: regional pool before fairshare (see fleet by region)
- Allocated_slots: final slots assigned in region (<= Requested_active_slots)
- Monthly_committed_hours: Reserved_GPUs * 24 * 30 (simple approximation)
- Uplift_notes: any local constraints, KMS, network, VPC needs

Fleet by region (earmarked for Dedicated, baseline):
Region, Physical_GPUs, Fairshare_pool(10%), Usable_for_allocations
US-WEST, 480, 48, 432
US-EAST, 240, 24, 216
EU-WEST, 160, 16, 144

Per-contract requested allocation (raw) — input from account/solutions (snapshot 2026-08-12):
Contract, Customer, Reserved_GPUs, Pref_region, Tiling_factor, Requested_active_slots, Monthly_committed_hours, Notes
C-1934, NimbusHealth, 120, US-WEST, 1.00, 120, 86400, HIPAA + private VPC (requires dedicated subnet)
C-2011, LexaSearch, 80, US-WEST, 1.25, 100, 57600, batch-heavy: can tolerate lower per-request latency
C-2078, FinSight, 60, US-EAST, 0.85, 51, 43200, encryption-in-use, prefers US-EAST for data locality
C-2122, NovaAssist, 40, EU-WEST, 1.00, 40, 28800, EU data residency required
C-2177, AtlasRetail, 150, multi (pref US-WEST/US-EAST), 1.00, 150, 108000, global footprint, flexible region but prefers US-WEST
C-2213, KernelDocs, 24, US-WEST, 0.85, 20, 17280, low sustained util historically
C-2244, AuroraMedia, 60, US-EAST, 1.25, 75, 43200, spiky streaming inference; burst candidate
C-2299, SoteriaAI, 96, EU-WEST, 1.00, 96, 69120, regulatory audit in progress
C-2330, TerraMaps, 36, US-WEST, 1.00, 36, 25920, batch geospatial jobs
C-2390, Enclave, 12, US-EAST, 1.25, 15, 8640, small reserved + burst profile

Raw totals (requested_active_slots) by region (sum of requested slots for contracts that prefer that region):
US-WEST requested raw = NimbusHealth(120) + LexaSearch(100) + AtlasRetail(150, pref multi but counts) + KernelDocs(20) + TerraMaps(36) = 426
US-EAST requested raw = FinSight(51) + AuroraMedia(75) + Enclave(15) + AtlasRetail(150, pref multi but flex->US-EAST counted partially) = 291 (note: AtlasRetail split assumed 50/50 in initial pass -> 75 to US-EAST)
EU-WEST requested raw = NovaAssist(40) + SoteriaAI(96) = 136

Allocation strategy and adjustments applied (manual step-by-step notes):
1) Reserve fairshare pool from each region (10%): US-WEST usable 432, US-EAST usable 216, EU-WEST usable 144.
2) Apply hard-preference contracts first (NovaAssist -> EU-WEST, FinSight -> US-EAST, Nimbus -> US-WEST, KernelDocs -> US-WEST, TerraMaps -> US-WEST, SoteriaAI -> EU-WEST).
3) For flexible/multi-pref contracts (AtlasRetail), attempt 50/50 split by default then adjust to region headroom and network cost.
4) If per-region requested > usable, scale back flexible and lower-tiling contracts first (priority: keep contracts with compliance or SLA higher).

First-pass allocations (manual pro-rated where necessary):
EU-WEST: usable 144, requested 136 -> fits. Allocations:
- NovaAssist: allocated 40 (full)
- SoteriaAI: allocated 96 (full)
Remaining EU-WEST headroom after allocations: 8 GPUs (moved to fairshare top-up).

US-EAST: usable 216, requested 291 (oversubscribe by 75) -> need scale-down. Priority order: FinSight (compliance), AuroraMedia (burst candidate), Enclave (small + burst), AtlasRetail(partial).
- FinSight: allocated 51 (full) -> remaining usable 165
- AuroraMedia: allocate 60 of 75 (scaleback of 15); rationale: burst eligible but large sustained would pressure pool -> remaining usable 105
- Enclave: allocate 12 of 15 (scaleback 3) -> remaining usable 93
- AtlasRetail: originally planned 75 -> scale to 75? But we need to preserve balanced across regions. Allocate 55 to US-EAST and move 20 to US-WEST. After that US-EAST remaining usable 38 (reserve 38 into fairshare).
Note: scaling choices biased to keep compliance/enterprise customers at full allocation.

US-WEST: usable 432, requested raw 426 but note AtlasRetail reallocation + LexaSearch etc -> effective requested = 426 + 20 (from Atlas moved) = 446 -> still fits within 432? 446 > 432 (oversubscribe by 14). Apply scale-down on tiling-sensitive / lower SLA customers: KernelDocs, TerraMaps, LexaSearch adjustable.
- NimbusHealth: allocated 120 (full) -> remaining 312
- LexaSearch: allocate 92 of 100 (scaledown 8) -> remaining 220
- AtlasRetail (moved 20): allocate 20 -> remaining 200
- KernelDocs: allocate 18 of 20 (scaledown 2) -> remaining 182
- TerraMaps: allocate 36 (full) -> remaining 146
Remaining US-WEST headroom (146) -> move 10 GPUs to global fairshare for cross-region burst, leaving 136 for local short-term burst and admin.

Final per-contract Allocated_slots summary:
C-1934 NimbusHealth: 120 (US-WEST)
C-2011 LexaSearch: 92 (US-WEST)
C-2078 FinSight: 51 (US-EAST)
C-2122 NovaAssist: 40 (EU-WEST)
C-2177 AtlasRetail: 75 (US-WEST 45 / US-EAST 30)  [note: earlier split adjusted to 45/30 due to headroom]
C-2213 KernelDocs: 18 (US-WEST)
C-2244 AuroraMedia: 60 (US-EAST)
C-2299 SoteriaAI: 96 (EU-WEST)
C-2330 TerraMaps: 36 (US-WEST)
C-2390 Enclave: 12 (US-EAST)

Total allocated by region after adjustments:
US-WEST allocated = 120 + 92 + 45 + 18 + 36 = 311
US-EAST allocated = 51 + 30 + 60 + 12 = 153
EU-WEST allocated = 40 + 96 = 136
Fairshare pools leftover: US-WEST usable headroom 121 (136 local burst - reserved 15 administrative), US-EAST 63, EU-WEST 8

Risk notes and caveats:
- We bumped several tiling factors as a manual heuristic; need a follow-up calc to simulate per-minute latency SLO impact for multi-tenant packing.
- Cross-region egress/costs not modeled. Moving AtlasRetail capacity cross-region may incur non-trivial egress on customer data flows. Solutions ENG to confirm.
- Burst fairness: current simple 10% fairshare may be insufficient for heavy spiky customers (AuroraMedia). Consider temporary burst credits or scheduled time windows.
- Some contracts (e.g., NimbusHealth) require private subnets and KMS constraints; those GPUs must be on nodes with correct networking—coord with infra-provisioning.

source.title: GPU surge forecast, shelving & staging playbook — Aug 2026
source.content:
Purpose:
This working sheet captures an operational playbook and near-term 12-month forecast for handling short-term and seasonal GPU demand surges across colocation and on-prem private deployments. It focuses on temporary shelving (cold-staged racks), active staging bays, power/cooling gating, and fast spillover options to avoid customer impact when reserved racks hit thermal or PDU limits.

Context & goals:
- Problem: sudden workload spikes combined with constrained rack power/cooling cause either throttling (higher latency) or risky over-provisioning.
- Goal: create a predictable runbook to add temporary capacity with clear limits (power, cooling, floor load) and minimize procurement lead time and ops toil.
- Constraints: colos with single A/B PDUs, 30–48 kW available per 42U rack in target sites, floor tile weight limits, and vendor lead times for mobile racks (10–14 wks).

Definitions (for this doc):
- Shelving: cold-staging racks stored in colo or staging facility with full cabling/caged PDUs ready for quick install.
- Staging bay: warm environment inside colocations or partner staging facility where nodes are power-tested, imaged, and smoke-tested before insertion.
- Surge window: expected 2–28 day period where additional racks or capacity are required.

Quick executive summary (actionable points):
1) Maintain a minimum shelve pool of 8 racks (US-West:4, US-East:2, EU-West:2) ready for rack-in within 72 hours.
2) Enforce power capping policy at 75% of PDU rating during initial plug-in; gradually increase if thermals and SLOs permit.
3) Pre-book 2 staging-bay days per major region per month during peak season (Nov–Feb).
4) Prepare cloud-burst reservations (vendor credits or spot fleet reservations) equal to worst-case 50% surge as a fallback.

12-month forecast summary (baseline + surge scenarios):
- Baseline pool (July 2026): total GPUs = 8,960 (US-West 4,320 | US-East 2,176 | EU-West 2,464) -> occupied racks = 112 (avg 80 GPUs/rack)
- Expected growth (12 mo conservative): +22% -> +1,971 GPUs -> need ~25 additional racks at current density.
- Surge scenarios and recommended action:
  - +10% surge (short spikes): use hot-spare slotting + dynamic batching, no new racks; avoid hitting PDU > 80%
  - +25% surge (seasonal): deploy 8 shelved racks, enable power-shed schedules for non-critical workloads
  - +50% surge (extreme/unplanned): deploy all shelved racks + cloud-burst reservations (preferred) or emergency temp racks (last resort)

Rack planning assumptions and capacity math:
- Target rack density: nominal 80 consumer-grade GPUs/rack (varies by model/airflow).
- Power per rack (avg) at 80 GPUs: 38 kW (peak) / 28.5 kW (typical under load-capped ops).
- Cooling delta per rack: requires 8.5–12.5 kW of chilled-water equivalent per rack depending on containment.
- Floor tile capacity: confirm 1200 kg per tile for all target colocations.

Staging & shelved-rack checklist (pre-flight):
- Hardware preimage: OS image, GPU drivers, runtime container pulled and checksum verified (sha256). Linked artifact: PR #127 for golden image.
- PDU mapping: label A/B feeders, verify dual-feed capability and automated switch scripts.
- Cables and spare parts: bring 2x spare hot-swap PSUs per rack, 1x spare PDU board per colo.
- Acceptance test (30 min): boot -> GPU smoke -> stress 15 min @ 60% sustained -> network perf test -> kv-cache priming test.
- Safety check: verify floor load annotation, aisle containment, and emergency e-stop switch accessible.

Power/cooling gating playbook (operator steps):
1) Pre-validate target rack location in colo tool, check PDU headroom >= 20% before plug-in.
2) Plug-in with power-limit profile 0.75 (enforced via IPMI + vendor utility).
3) Monitor inlet temps and PDU load for first 6 hours; if inlet temp delta <= +4C and PDU <= 90% -> step to profile 0.9 for next 6 hours.
4) If any rack crosses PDU 95% or inlet temps exceed thresholds -> automatically throttle non-critical jobs and issue page to SRE on-call.

Operational runbook & ticket flow:
- Alerting: add metric: colo.pdu.load_pct and colo.inlet.temp_delta -> page if >90% for 10m.
- Jira tickets: INFRA-842 (capacity request), OPS-1291 (staging slot booking), SRE-220 (thermal incident runbook).
- Slack channels: #ops-shelving, #colo-incident -> use standardized message template in Confluence.

Procurement & lead time advice:
- Mobile racks and PDUs: budget 10–14 weeks for standard items; expedite windows can be 4–6 weeks with premium shipping.
- Pre-negotiated colo install windows: secure 3 backup install slots per quarter per region to guarantee <72 hour full-install SLA for shelved racks.

Risk register (top items):
- Single PDU sites: risk of A/B failure; mitigation: prefer sites with proven dual feed or deploy fewer high-power racks there.
- Cooling mismatch: if containment efficacy unknown, stage at reduced power until measured.
- Vendor lead time: maintain contract clause for priority fulfillment for emergency procurement.

Open questions / decisions needed:
- Approve budget for 8 additional shelved racks (capex + annual colo standing network port fees). (Owner: Priya Menon)
- Decide acceptable percentage of monthly cloud-burst spend as insurance vs. buying more shelved capacity. (Owner: Marcus Liu)

Next steps (1–3 weeks):
- Finalize shelved-rack inventory list and storage addresses (Ethan / Ops) -> target date 2026-08-18.
- Run a dry-install on one mobile rack in US-West staging bay and record full measurements (thermal, PDU behaviour) -> Lina -> target 2026-08-14.
- Add automated PDU load dashboards to Console sandbox with alert thresholds -> Eng-Observability -> target 2026-08-21.

Appendix: quick contact matrix
- Shelving ops lead: Ethan Park (ops pager: +1-415-555-0132)
- Staging bay coordinator US-West: Lina Gómez (Slack: @lina)

- Baseline pool (July 2026): total GPUs = 8,960 (US-West 4,320 | US-East 2,176 | EU-West 2,464) -> occupied racks = 112 (avg 80 GPUs/rack)
- Expected growth (12 mo conservative): +22% -> +1,971 GPUs -> need ~25 additional racks at current density.
- Surge scenarios and recommended action:
  - +10% surge (short spikes): use hot-spare slotting + dynamic batching, no new racks; avoid hitting PDU > 80%
  - +25% surge (seasonal): deploy 8 shelved racks, enable power-shed schedules for non-critical workloads
  - +50% surge (extreme/unplanned): deploy all shelved racks + cloud-burst reservations (preferred) or emergency temp racks (last resort)

Rack planning assumptions and capacity math:
- Target rack density: nominal 80 consumer-grade GPUs/rack (varies by model/airflow).
- Power per rack (avg) at 80 GPUs: 38 kW (peak) / 28.5 kW (typical under load-capped ops).
- Cooling delta per rack: requires 8.5–12.5 kW of chilled-water equivalent per rack depending on containment.
- Floor tile capacity: confirm 1200 kg per tile for all target colocations.

Staging & shelved-rack checklist (pre-flight):
- Hardware preimage: OS image, GPU drivers, runtime container pulled and checksum verified (sha256). Linked artifact: PR #127 for golden image.
- PDU mapping: label A/B feeders, verify dual-feed capability and automated switch scripts.
- Cables and spare parts: bring 2x spare hot-swap PSUs per rack, 1x spare PDU board per colo.
- Acceptance test (30 min): boot -> GPU smoke -> stress 15 min @ 60% sustained -> network perf test -> kv-cache priming test.
- Safety check: verify floor load annotation, aisle containment, and emergency e-stop switch accessible.

Power/cooling gating playbook (operator steps):
1) Pre-validate target rack location in colo tool, check PDU headroom >= 20% before plug-in.
2) Plug-in with power-limit profile 0.75 (enforced via IPMI + vendor utility).
3) Monitor inlet temps and PDU load for first 6 hours; if inlet temp delta <= +4C and PDU <= 90% -> step to profile 0.9 for next 6 hours.
4) If any rack crosses PDU 95% or inlet temps exceed thresholds -> automatically throttle non-critical jobs and issue page to SRE on-call.

Operational runbook & ticket flow:
- Alerting: add metric: colo.pdu.load_pct and colo.inlet.temp_delta -> page if >90% for 10m.
- Jira tickets: INFRA-842 (capacity request), OPS-1291 (staging slot booking), SRE-220 (thermal incident runbook).
- Slack channels: #ops-shelving, #colo-incident -> use standardized message template in Confluence.

Procurement & lead time advice:
- Mobile racks and PDUs: budget 10–14 weeks for standard items; expedite windows can be 4–6 weeks with premium shipping.
- Pre-negotiated colo install windows: secure 3 backup install slots per quarter per region to guarantee <72 hour full-install SLA for shelved racks.

Risk register (top items):
- Single PDU sites: risk of A/B failure; mitigation: prefer sites with proven dual feed or deploy fewer high-power racks there.
- Cooling mismatch: if containment efficacy unknown, stage at reduced power until measured.
- Vendor lead time: maintain contract clause for priority fulfillment for emergency procurement.

Open questions / decisions needed:
- Approve budget for 8 additional shelved racks (capex + annual colo standing network port fees). (Owner: Priya Menon)
- Decide acceptable percentage of monthly cloud-burst spend as insurance vs. buying more shelved capacity. (Owner: Marcus Liu)

Next steps (1–3 weeks):
- Finalize shelved-rack inventory list and storage addresses (Ethan / Ops) -> target date 2026-08-18.
- Run a dry-install on one mobile rack in US-West staging bay and record full measurements (thermal, PDU behaviour) -> Lina -> target 2026-08-14.
- Add automated PDU load dashboards to Console sandbox with alert thresholds -> Eng-Observability -> target 2026-08-21.

Appendix: quick contact matrix
- Shelving ops lead: Ethan Park (ops pager: +1-415-555-0132)
- Staging bay coordinator US-West: Lina Gómez (Slack: @lina)

Purpose:
This working sheet captures an operational playbook and near-term 12-month forecast for handling short-term and seasonal GPU demand surges across colocation and on-prem private deployments. It focuses on temporary shelving (cold-staged racks), active staging bays, power/cooling gating, and fast spillover options to avoid customer impact when reserved racks hit thermal or PDU limits.

Context & goals:
- Problem: sudden workload spikes combined with constrained rack power/cooling cause either throttling (higher latency) or risky over-provisioning.
- Goal: create a predictable runbook to add temporary capacity with clear limits (power, cooling, floor load) and minimize procurement lead time and ops toil.
- Constraints: colos with single A/B PDUs, 30–48 kW available per 42U rack in target sites, floor tile weight limits, and vendor lead times for mobile racks (10–14 wks).

Definitions (for this doc):
- Shelving: cold-staging racks stored in colo or staging facility with full cabling/caged PDUs ready for quick install.
- Staging bay: warm environment inside colocations or partner staging facility where nodes are power-tested, imaged, and smoke-tested before insertion.
- Surge window: expected 2–28 day period where additional racks or capacity are required.

Quick executive summary (actionable points):
1) Maintain a minimum shelve pool of 8 racks (US-West:4, US-East:2, EU-West:2) ready for rack-in within 72 hours.
2) Enforce power capping policy at 75% of PDU rating during initial plug-in; gradually increase if thermals and SLOs permit.
3) Pre-book 2 staging-bay days per major region per month during peak season (Nov–Feb).
4) Prepare cloud-burst reservations (vendor credits or spot fleet reservations) equal to worst-case 50% surge as a fallback.

- Baseline pool (July 2026): total GPUs = 8,960 (US-West 4,320 | US-East 2,176 | EU-West 2,464) -> occupied racks = 112 (avg 80 GPUs/rack)

source.title: Dependability Compass — cross-team playmap for model-serving reliability
source.content:
WORKING DRAFT — Dependability Compass: cross-team playmap for model-serving reliability

Purpose and context:
- This doc captures a concrete cross-functional plan to operationalize reliability goals for model-serving surfaces (hosted API, dedicated, private).
- Focus: translate product-level availability and correctness expectations into measurable signals, ownership, integration tasks, and a pragmatic rollout path that minimizes customer impact while improving long-term observability and remediation speed.
- Audience: SRE, Serving Runtime, Platform, Observability, Product-Hosted-API, Solutions Eng, Support.

High-level objectives (what success looks like):
1) Observable, actionable signals for the top three failure modes across model-serving (model-load failures, KV cache regressions, tail-latency spikes).
2) Clear ownership and runbooks for each signal with measurable reduction targets in MTTD/MTTR over rolling 90-day windows.
3) A staged SLO adoption pattern that lets teams opt into strict error/latency targets with canary rollouts and automated fallback policies.
4) Two pilot services on dedicated infra with full pipeline (dashboards, alerts, automated rollback) within Q3 2026.

Guiding principles:
- Incremental: instrument first, then set targets, then automate responses.
- Customer-impact focused: prioritize signals that map to user-visible degradation.
- Low blast radius: use progressive rollout (canary, cohort) for any enforcement.
- Cross-team pairing: each milestone requires an SRE + owning-engineer co-shipper.

Scope (in):
- Model-serving control plane and inference runtime path (request ingress, KV cache, model weights load, token generation latency).
- Observability (metrics, traces, logs) and alerting pipelines used by SRE and product teams.
- Runbooks and automated remediation playbooks.

Out of scope (for this phase):
- Deep model quality evaluation (LLM hallucination metrics) — covered by Optimize/product.
- Billing/charging changes for customers.

Stakeholders and lightweight RACI:
- Eng-SRE (lead): monitoring, alerting, runbooks, incident postmortem cadence — R
- Serving Runtime: implementation of instrumentation, fallback hooks, canary flags — A
- Observability: dashboards, trace sampling, log retention settings — C
- Platform Engineering: infra capacity policies, autoscaling safety nets — C
- Product-Hosted-API: product SLO definition & customer-facing comms — I
- Solutions Eng / Support: pilot adoption and customer escalation play — C
(Notes: R=Responsible, A=Accountable, C=Consulted, I=Informed)

Signals, tentative metrics and starter targets (provisional — iterate after telemetry baseline):
- Request Success Rate (5xx + model-load errors): baseline measurement window = 14d; pilot SLO target = 99.7% per 30d for non-burst endpoints.
- 95th/99th token-latency for inference: capture per-model and per-route; pilot latency SLO = 95th <= 300ms on fast-models, 99th <= 750ms.
- Model load latency and success (artifact fetch / weight deserialization): target = 99.9% successful within 15s.
- KV cache hit ratio and tail latency impact: signal only for services opting into KV caching; target: maintain degradation <10% customer impact during cache warmups.

Monitoring & alerting pattern:
- Three alert tiers: WARNING (signal trending bad), INCIDENT (customer-impacting degradation), OUTAGE (SLA violation imminent or occurring).
- Use metric-derived anomaly windows + heatmap of errors by region/model to reduce noisy pager noise.
- Attach runbook link in alert payload; require initial triage within 10 minutes for INCIDENT.

Runbooks and automation (practical examples):
- Runbook: high 5xx rate from a model-serving pod: steps — 1) check model-weight load errors, 2) check OOMs, 3) scale down circuit-breaker, 4) failover to model-variant v2 with pinned weights, 5) engage fallback routing.
- Automation: when model-load errors > threshold on 3 hosts in 5m, automatically mark model variant as degraded and route new requests to fallback pool. Manual approval required before re-enable.

Canary and rollout pattern (phase gates):
Phase 0 (instrument + baseline) — 3 weeks
- Implement key metrics and traces for pilot services. Pair SRE + runtime eng.
- Build dashboard templates and export baseline numbers.

Phase 1 (soft SLOs + runbooks) — 4 weeks
- Publish soft SLO targets (non-punitive) and runbooks.
- Run canaries on internal traffic only.

Phase 2 (automated fallback + guarded SLOs) — 6 weeks
- Add automated fallback policy for the most common failure (model-load).
- Start rolling SLOs to small customer cohorts behind a feature flag.

Phase 3 (broader enforcement) — 6–8 weeks
- Expand enforcement, ensure platform controls prevent noisy autoscale oscillations.
- Complete postmortem playbooks and SLO reporting.

Sprint-by-sprint starter backlog (first 8 sprints, two-week sprints assumed):
Sprint 1: metrics schema, instrumentation hooks merged, dashboard skeletons, export baseline script.
Sprint 2: alert definitions (warning/incident/outage), basic runbook drafts, link to oncall rotation.
Sprint 3: implement canary flag, traffic routing for canary, label propagation for traces.
Sprint 4: automation prototype for model-load fallback, test harness for rollback.
Sprint 5: pilot on Dedicated infra (two services), SRE-led chaos runbook validation.
Sprint 6: telemetry-driven SLO adjustments, comms template for customers.
Sprint 7: scaling safety-net work (platform autoscale playbooks), cost-impact review.
Sprint 8: finalize runbooks, production rollouts for cohort A, dashboarding for exec summary.

Cross-team dependency map (key dependencies that block progress):
- Serving Runtime needs to expose model load/error tags and a per-request model-variant identifier. Blocker: missing tagging per request.
- Observability needs to add a low-cost trace sampling policy and retention for 30d for pilot metrics. Blocker: retention quota limits in prod logging account.
- Platform must provide dedicated canary pools and feature-flag gating for traffic split. Blocker: infra quotas for dedicated GPUs.

Risk register & mitigation notes:
- Risk: Alert fatigue causing ignored incidents. Mitigation: implement signal aggregation and reduce noisy low-value alerts before escalations.
- Risk: Fallback policies mask underlying reliability problems. Mitigation: ensure fallback triggers incident creation and postmortem requirement.
- Risk: Costs from additional telemetry and retention. Mitigation: use targeted sampling and short retention for verbose traces; keep metrics high-level.

Customer communications and escalations:
- For pilots: proactive notice to pilot customers outlining expected maintenance windows and rollback behavior.
- For broader rollouts: staged emails + in-console banner with opt-out for early customers.
- Support play: Solutions Eng to own first-line customer comms, SRE to own technical explanation and remediation timeline.

Open questions (to be resolved in the next working sync):
- Which product routes should be in the initial two pilot services? proposal: text-generation (low-latency path) + embeddings (throughput path).
- What is acceptable cost delta for adding trace retention for pilot services? target: <10% increase in observability bill for pilot.
- How do we align model-variant pinning semantics across hosted and private customers? need design doc from Serving Runtime.

Next immediate actions (owners + due dates):
- Ethan Park: create dashboard templates and baseline export job — due 2026-06-25.
- Aisha Patel: draft runbook templates and run triage play — due 2026-06-28.
- Mariano Gomez: (Observability) propose sampling/retention plan — due 2026-06-30.
- Ravi Kapoor: check infra quotas for canary pools and report blockers — due 2026-07-02.

Appendix: notes for pairing sessions and quick checklists
- Pairing rule: every change that affects an alert or runbook must have at least one SRE reviewer and one owning-service reviewer.
- Quick triage checklist for oncall: always record first-action timestamp, whether automatic fallback executed, and whether customer impact (yes/no).

Notes to reviewers:
- This is intentionally prescriptive at the tactical level to get cross-team alignment quickly. Expect adjustments to SLO targets after we ingest baseline telemetry.
- Please comment on the proposed pilot services and add any missing failure modes you’ve observed in the past 6 months.

End of document.

- Complete postmortem playbooks and SLO reporting.

Sprint-by-sprint starter backlog (first 8 sprints, two-week sprints assumed):
Sprint 1: metrics schema, instrumentation hooks merged, dashboard skeletons, export baseline script.
Sprint 2: alert definitions (warning/incident/outage), basic runbook drafts, link to oncall rotation.
Sprint 3: implement canary flag, traffic routing for canary, label propagation for traces.
Sprint 4: automation prototype for model-load fallback, test harness for rollback.
Sprint 5: pilot on Dedicated infra (two services), SRE-led chaos runbook validation.
Sprint 6: telemetry-driven SLO adjustments, comms template for customers.
Sprint 7: scaling safety-net work (platform autoscale playbooks), cost-impact review.
Sprint 8: finalize runbooks, production rollouts for cohort A, dashboarding for exec summary.

Cross-team dependency map (key dependencies that block progress):
- Serving Runtime needs to expose model load/error tags and a per-request model-variant identifier. Blocker: missing tagging per request.
- Observability needs to add a low-cost trace sampling policy and retention for 30d for pilot metrics. Blocker: retention quota limits in prod logging account.
- Platform must provide dedicated canary pools and feature-flag gating for traffic split. Blocker: infra quotas for dedicated GPUs.

Risk register & mitigation notes:
- Risk: Alert fatigue causing ignored incidents. Mitigation: implement signal aggregation and reduce noisy low-value alerts before escalations.
- Risk: Fallback policies mask underlying reliability problems. Mitigation: ensure fallback triggers incident creation and postmortem requirement.
- Risk: Costs from additional telemetry and retention. Mitigation: use targeted sampling and short retention for verbose traces; keep metrics high-level.

Customer communications and escalations:
- For pilots: proactive notice to pilot customers outlining expected maintenance windows and rollback behavior.
- For broader rollouts: staged emails + in-console banner with opt-out for early customers.
- Support play: Solutions Eng to own first-line customer comms, SRE to own technical explanation and remediation timeline.

Open questions (to be resolved in the next working sync):
- Which product routes should be in the initial two pilot services? proposal: text-generation (low-latency path) + embeddings (throughput path).
- What is acceptable cost delta for adding trace retention for pilot services? target: <10% increase in observability bill for pilot.
- How do we align model-variant pinning semantics across hosted and private customers? need design doc from Serving Runtime.

Next immediate actions (owners + due dates):
- Ethan Park: create dashboard templates and baseline export job — due 2026-06-25.
- Aisha Patel: draft runbook templates and run triage play — due 2026-06-28.
- Mariano Gomez: (Observability) propose sampling/retention plan — due 2026-06-30.
- Ravi Kapoor: check infra quotas for canary pools and report blockers — due 2026-07-02.

Appendix: notes for pairing sessions and quick checklists
- Pairing rule: every change that affects an alert or runbook must have at least one SRE reviewer and one owning-service reviewer.
- Quick triage checklist for oncall: always record first-action timestamp, whether automatic fallback executed, and whether customer impact (yes/no).

Notes to reviewers:
- This is intentionally prescriptive at the tactical level to get cross-team alignment quickly. Expect adjustments to SLO targets after we ingest baseline telemetry.
- Please comment on the proposed pilot services and add any missing failure modes you’ve observed in the past 6 months.

End of document.

- Immediate gap: Weekend coverage short by ~3 people on heavy-burn weekends (7/10, 7/17). Current volunteers cover daytime but overnight coverage thin.

- Cost trade: Extending on-call incentives (weekend bump pay) vs. lowering non-critical burst capacity during weekend windows.

Open questions / unknowns:
- Can Solutions Eng provide sustained Saturday coverage for the July pilot window?
- Is there budget for weekend incentive for summer coverage? (finance approval needed)
- Are there any expected customer escalations 7/10-7/18 that would require full SE cover?

Quick todos (in-line):

- [ ] Marina: confirm Platform Eng two-day coverage (due 2026-07-06)
- [ ] Aisha: update Confluence rotation + link here (due 2026-07-04)
- [ ] Hannah: block daytime triage hours in calendar for weekends covered (due 2026-07-07)

- This is intentionally lightweight; use as ad-hoc census for the next two weekends and then fold into the master rota in Confluence after 2026-07-10.
- Expect changes as people swap sprints; keep last-updated timestamp on individual rows.

source.title: Ops Orchestra: 72hr Launch Briefing and Stability Runbook (annotated)
source.content:
Purpose:
This is a compact, annotated 72-hour runbook for launching a medium-risk backend change (v2026.06.1) that touches routing, model selection fallbacks, and a small console feature. It is intended for the release conductor, on-call SRE, serving-runtime lead, and product owner. Use as the single source-of-truth during the 72-hour window around a planned cutover.

Scope and assumptions:
- Release scope: routing policy adjustment + canary configuration + console UI toggle for route visibility.
- Deployment model: rolling canary via release controller in Hosted + Dedicated clusters.
- Traffic profile: global traffic, peak 2pm PT, sustained 5k rps.
- Expected risk: medium (behavioral regressions under specific long-tail prompts).

High-level objectives (SLO during launch):
- Maintain 99.5% successful responses (2xx) across all regions.
- P50 latency degradation <= 10% from baseline.
- No more than 0.5% error increase in model inference 5xx/4xx rate.

Roles and responsibilities (run-of-show):
- Release Conductor: Ethan Park (coordination, go/no-go)
- Serving Runtime Lead: Maya Chen (rollout knobs, telemetry hooks)
- On-call SRE: Luis Ortega (alerts, mitigation, rollback)
- Product Owner: Priya Nair (customer comms, business impact)
- Release Engineer (automation): Samir Patel (deploy pipelines, infra changes)

72-hour timeline (compact calendar) - annotated checkpoints:
- T minus 72h (Day -3): Finalize checklist, confirm canary app config in staging, assign rotation slots. Owner: Conductor.
- T minus 48h (Day -2): Run end-to-end smoke tests against synthetic traffic (prefix caching exercised). Capture telemetry baseline. Owner: Serving Runtime Lead + Release Engineer.
- T minus 24h (Day -1): Dry-run on blue environment using production-like dataset size (10% load). Practise rollback path once. Owner: On-call SRE.
- T minus 2h (Day 0 morning): Final go/no-go meeting. Confirm on-call and escalation roster; confirm customer-impact list. Owner: Conductor.
- T0: Begin canary deployment (canary route 1%). Start detailed telemetry collection. Owner: Release Engineer.
- T0 + 15m: Inspect canary health dashboard (error budget, latency, tail latency, model output quality quick-check). Owner: Serving Runtime Lead.
- T0 + 30m: If green, increase canary to 5%. If anomalies, follow mitigation.
- T0 + 90m: If green, increase canary to 20%. Pause 30m.
- T0 + 3h: If green, escalate rollout to 50%/regional distribution.
- T0 + 6h: Full rollout continuation to 100% if all checkpoints passed.
- T0 + 12h: Post-rollout verification and start 24h observation window.

Annotated preflight checklist (must be green before T minus 2h):
- [ ] Release artifact built and SHA-verified (artifact id: serving-runtime:v2026.06.1).
- [ ] Release notes ready (draft below) and pinned to launch doc.
- [ ] Smoke tests passing in staging and blue. Link: https://ci.redwood.ai/job/rel/v2026.06.1.
- [ ] Telemetry hooks enabled (token-level sampling at 1%, full traces for errors).
- [ ] Alert burn-down for canary specific signals (errors, 95th latency, model-exact-match regressions) configured.
- [ ] Capacity check: Dedicated pools at 120% cushion for expected burst.
- [ ] Rollback playbook validated and a fast rollback image ready.
- [ ] Customer comms pre-drafted for potential incidents.
- [ ] Console feature gate toggles validated and decoupled from runtime routing code.

Canary and rollout knobs (practical thresholds):
- Hard fail if: 5xx rate increases > 0.5% absolute AND sustained > 5 minutes.
- Soft pause if: P95 latency increases > 25% OR model output quality regression > 3% on eval harness.
- Escalation to SEV if: user-facing error rate > 1% OR critical customers report failures via PSC within 30 minutes.

Rollback matrix (annotated):
- Symptom: sudden spike in 5xx across regions -> Action: immediate rollback to previous routing policy (Conductor triggers rollback pipeline), page SRE, post-mortem triggered.
- Symptom: increased tail latency only for long-context endpoints -> Action: throttle batch size for affected endpoints (runtime lead), revert quantization change if present.
- Symptom: functional regression on console feature only -> Action: flip console feature gate off (Product Owner) — no runtime rollback required.

Telemetry dashboards and quick checks (what to look at every checkpoint):
- Request volume and distribution by region + route.
- 2xx/4xx/5xx breakdown and errors by stack (model, routing, infra).
- Latency histograms (P50, P95, P99) per route.
- Token cost per request and sudden deltas (Optimize signals).
- Small eval prompt set (10 canonical prompts) run against canary model path for quality regressions; compare BLEU-like signal and hallucination markers.

Communication playbook during launch:
- Internal status cadence: 15m updates during first 2 hours, then 30m until stable. Post on #release-ops channel and update the Confluence launch page.
- Customer-facing comms: prepared email and status page blurb; Product Owner authorizes outbound messaging.
- Incident comms: Use the standard SEV template, include ingress/egress snapshots, and model-output artifact for debugging.

Post-release checklist (first 72h after full rollout):
- [ ] Confirm final telemetry for 24h meets SLOs.
- [ ] Run full eval harness (1k prompts) to detect subtle regressions.
- [ ] Dimensional analysis for cost delta (per-token cost change).
- [ ] Capture rollout lessons and update automated gating thresholds.
- [ ] Schedule a 1-week retrospective with cross-functional attendees.

Draft release notes (to be published in release channel and release page):
- Title: v2026.06.1 - Routing policy improvements and route-visibility console toggle (draft)
- Summary: This release adjusts request routing logic to prefer lower-latency model variants for short-context prompts and adds a console toggle to surface route selection decisions for debugging.
- Notable changes:
  - Adaptive routing: routes short-context requests to a light model variant to reduce average latency by ~8-12% in internal benchmarks.
  - Console: route visibility toggle (off by default) to help support teams trace model selection decisions.
  - Telemetry: added token-cost breakdown by route and a small eval harness report pipeline.
- Migration/rollback notes: No database migrations. Rollback supported via release controller to previous routing policy.
- Known issues (prelim):
  - Long-context endpoints may see a minor latency bump (+5-10% p95) while caching warms; mitigation steps included in runbook.
  - An internal eval prompt surfaced a small quality delta on prompt family X; we are monitoring and have a canary watch on those routes.
- Owner and contacts: Release Conductor (Ethan Park), Runtime Lead (Maya Chen), On-call SRE (Luis Ortega).

Appendix A - Quick commands and links (for the conductor):
- Deploy pipeline (canary): ./ci/deploy --env prod --canary 1 --artifact serving-runtime:v2026.06.1
- Rollback pipeline: ./ci/rollback --to-tag serving-runtime:v2026.05.9
- Telemetry dashboard: https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/launch/v2026-06-1
- Error trace search: https://sentry.redwood.ai/serving-runtime?q=release%3Av2026.06.1

Appendix B - Open questions (to resolve before next release):
- Should the route visibility toggle default to on for internal tenants? (Prod input required)
- Automate canary increases based on model-quality signal rather than latency alone? (requires additional eval infra)
- Add a run of synthetic adversarial prompts to the preflight harness to reduce long-tail regressions.

Notes to self (scratchpad) - quick bullets:
- Consider adding hourly snapshot dumps of the KV cache sizes during first 6 hours post-rollout.
- Check with finance if per-token cost delta needs a pricing notification for high-usage customers.
- Confirm console feature gating is fully decoupled from routing infra (Samir to validate).

End of document.

- Symptom: sudden spike in 5xx across regions -> Action: immediate rollback to previous routing policy (Conductor triggers rollback pipeline), page SRE, post-mortem triggered.
- Symptom: increased tail latency only for long-context endpoints -> Action: throttle batch size for affected endpoints (runtime lead), revert quantization change if present.
- Symptom: functional regression on console feature only -> Action: flip console feature gate off (Product Owner) — no runtime rollback required.

Telemetry dashboards and quick checks (what to look at every checkpoint):
- Request volume and distribution by region + route.
- 2xx/4xx/5xx breakdown and errors by stack (model, routing, infra).
- Latency histograms (P50, P95, P99) per route.
- Token cost per request and sudden deltas (Optimize signals).
- Small eval prompt set (10 canonical prompts) run against canary model path for quality regressions; compare BLEU-like signal and hallucination markers.

Communication playbook during launch:
- Internal status cadence: 15m updates during first 2 hours, then 30m until stable. Post on #release-ops channel and update the Confluence launch page.
- Customer-facing comms: prepared email and status page blurb; Product Owner authorizes outbound messaging.
- Incident comms: Use the standard SEV template, include ingress/egress snapshots, and model-output artifact for debugging.

Post-release checklist (first 72h after full rollout):
- [ ] Confirm final telemetry for 24h meets SLOs.
- [ ] Run full eval harness (1k prompts) to detect subtle regressions.
- [ ] Dimensional analysis for cost delta (per-token cost change).
- [ ] Capture rollout lessons and update automated gating thresholds.
- [ ] Schedule a 1-week retrospective with cross-functional attendees.

Draft release notes (to be published in release channel and release page):
- Title: v2026.06.1 - Routing policy improvements and route-visibility console toggle (draft)
- Summary: This release adjusts request routing logic to prefer lower-latency model variants for short-context prompts and adds a console toggle to surface route selection decisions for debugging.
- Notable changes:
  - Adaptive routing: routes short-context requests to a light model variant to reduce average latency by ~8-12% in internal benchmarks.
  - Console: route visibility toggle (off by default) to help support teams trace model selection decisions.
  - Telemetry: added token-cost breakdown by route and a small eval harness report pipeline.
- Migration/rollback notes: No database migrations. Rollback supported via release controller to previous routing policy.
- Known issues (prelim):
  - Long-context endpoints may see a minor latency bump (+5-10% p95) while caching warms; mitigation steps included in runbook.
  - An internal eval prompt surfaced a small quality delta on prompt family X; we are monitoring and have a canary watch on those routes.
- Owner and contacts: Release Conductor (Ethan Park), Runtime Lead (Maya Chen), On-call SRE (Luis Ortega).

Appendix A - Quick commands and links (for the conductor):
- Deploy pipeline (canary): ./ci/deploy --env prod --canary 1 --artifact serving-runtime:v2026.06.1
- Rollback pipeline: ./ci/rollback --to-tag serving-runtime:v2026.05.9
- Telemetry dashboard: https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/launch/v2026-06-1
- Error trace search: https://sentry.redwood.ai/serving-runtime?q=release%3Av2026.06.1

Appendix B - Open questions (to resolve before next release):
- Should the route visibility toggle default to on for internal tenants? (Prod input required)
- Automate canary increases based on model-quality signal rather than latency alone? (requires additional eval infra)
- Add a run of synthetic adversarial prompts to the preflight harness to reduce long-tail regressions.

Notes to self (scratchpad) - quick bullets:
- Consider adding hourly snapshot dumps of the KV cache sizes during first 6 hours post-rollout.
- Check with finance if per-token cost delta needs a pricing notification for high-usage customers.
- Confirm console feature gating is fully decoupled from routing infra (Samir to validate).

End of document.
text:
Purpose:
This is a compact, annotated 72-hour runbook for launching a medium-risk backend change (v2026.06.1) that touches routing, model selection fallbacks, and a small console feature. It is intended for the release conductor, on-call SRE, serving-runtime lead, and product owner. Use as the single source-of-truth during the 72-hour window around a planned cutover.

Scope and assumptions:
- Release scope: routing policy adjustment + canary configuration + console UI toggle for route visibility.
- Deployment model: rolling canary via release controller in Hosted + Dedicated clusters.
- Traffic profile: global traffic, peak 2pm PT, sustained 5k rps.
- Expected risk: medium (behavioral regressions under specific long-tail prompts).

High-level objectives (SLO during launch):
- Maintain 99.5% successful responses (2xx) across all regions.
- P50 latency degradation <= 10% from baseline.
- No more than 0.5% error increase in model inference 5xx/4xx rate.

Roles and responsibilities (run-of-show):
- Release Conductor: Ethan Park (coordination, go/no-go)
- Serving Runtime Lead: Maya Chen (rollout knobs, telemetry hooks)
- On-call SRE: Luis Ortega (alerts, mitigation, rollback)
- Product Owner: Priya Nair (customer comms, business impact)
- Release Engineer (automation): Samir Patel (deploy pipelines, infra changes)

72-hour timeline (compact calendar) - annotated checkpoints:
- T minus 72h (Day -3): Finalize checklist, confirm canary app config in staging, assign rotation slots. Owner: Conductor.
- T minus 48h (Day -2): Run end-to-end smoke tests against synthetic traffic (prefix caching exercised). Capture telemetry baseline. Owner: Serving Runtime Lead + Release Engineer.
- T minus 24h (Day -1): Dry-run on blue environment using production-like dataset size (10% load). Practise rollback path once. Owner: On-call SRE.
- T minus 2h (Day 0 morning): Final go/no-go meeting. Confirm on-call and escalation roster; confirm customer-impact list. Owner: Conductor.
- T0: Begin canary deployment (canary route 1%). Start detailed telemetry collection. Owner: Release Engineer.
- T0 + 15m: Inspect canary health dashboard (error budget, latency, tail latency, model output quality quick-check). Owner: Serving Runtime Lead.
- T0 + 30m: If green, increase canary to 5%. If anomalies, follow mitigation.
- T0 + 90m: If green, increase canary to 20%. Pause 30m.
- T0 + 3h: If green, escalate rollout to 50%/regional distribution.
- T0 + 6h: Full rollout continuation to 100% if all checkpoints passed.
- T0 + 12h: Post-rollout verification and start 24h observation window.

Annotated preflight checklist (must be green before T minus 2h):
- [ ] Release artifact built and SHA-verified (artifact id: serving-runtime:v2026.06.1).
- [ ] Release notes ready (draft below) and pinned to launch doc.
- [ ] Smoke tests passing in staging and blue. Link: https://ci.redwood.ai/job/rel/v2026.06.1.
- [ ] Telemetry hooks enabled (token-level sampling at 1%, full traces for errors).
- [ ] Alert burn-down for canary specific signals (errors, 95th latency, model-exact-match regressions) configured.
- [ ] Capacity check: Dedicated pools at 120% cushion for expected burst.
- [ ] Rollback playbook validated and a fast rollback image ready.
- [ ] Customer comms pre-drafted for potential incidents.
- [ ] Console feature gate toggles validated and decoupled from runtime routing code.

Canary and rollout knobs (practical thresholds):
- Hard fail if: 5xx rate increases > 0.5% absolute AND sustained > 5 minutes.
- Soft pause if: P95 latency increases > 25% OR model output quality regression > 3% on eval harness.
- Escalation to SEV if: user-facing error rate > 1% OR critical customers report failures via PSC within 30 minutes.

source.title: Price elasticity on-call roster and burn-shock scenarios
source.content:
Purpose:
This is a lightweight operational runbook + checklist for finance + ops to react to rapid price- or demand-driven burn shocks. Combines: a) quick pricing sensitivity snapshots, b) an on-call roster and escalation matrix for finance actions, and c) a short list of headcount/contractor triggers to buy time. The doc is intentionally pragmatic and checklist-driven — use as the first 72h playbook while modeling team prepares a deeper scenario workbook.

Background/context:
- We have observed faster model-cost compression and, in parallel, customer usage bursts tied to specific product releases. Price volatility (model licensing, GPU spot markets) or sudden demand pulses (customer open beta, viral feature) can move monthly burn by >5–8% within a single week.
- This doc sits between the monitoring dashboards (Console: per-route cost and tokens) and the full sensitivity models in the financial workbook.

Quick definitions (for clarity):
- Burn shock: an unexpected increase in monthly infrastructure + run costs above baseline >3% within 7 days, or >8% within 30 days.
- Pricing harvest: rapid pricing adjustment or commitment packaging to reduce marginal unit costs (e.g., slabbing, temporary surcharge).
- HC trigger: defined headcount or contractor action (freeze, redeploy, hire pause) that reduces people-driven operating spend on a 30–90 day horizon.

Immediate 0–6 hour checklist (on-call finance + ops):
1) Triage signal: confirm source — Console alert / Slack #fin-ops / customer escalation / cloud-billing spike. Record time and affected routes.
2) Assign on-call leads (see roster below).
3) Snapshot costs: pull per-route token rate + per-GPU cost for affected pools (copy last 48h).
4) Quick elasticity run: calculate delta-burn under three simple multipliers (x1.15, x1.3, x1.5) on token volume for affected routes; produce % of monthly burn.
5) Publish a one-paragraph alert to execs (Slack @exec-fin) with recommended immediate action (hold promos, brief pricing note, or enable temporary cap).

On-call roster (first pass) — who does what (rotate weekly):
- Finance lead (cost snapshots, invoice impact): Maya Chen (primary), Noah Rivera (backup)
- Product/Monetization (customer-facing messages, temporary price changes): Sofia Martinez (primary)
- Platform ops (capacity, autoscale knobs, cache eviction): Priya Kapoor (primary), Oliver Grant (backup)
- Legal/Contracts (if we need to amend enterprise pricing or apply credits): Legal on-call via pager
- SRE lead (model routing/fallbacks): Eng-SRE on-call alias

Escalation matrix:
- If projected burn delta >5% for rolling 30-day, escalate to Head of Finance within 6 hours.
- If projected burn delta >12% or predicted SLA impact, escalate to COO and open urgent cross-functional incident.

Quick sensitivity table (run in first 3 hours) — textual form for Slack paste:
Scenario | Volume change | Model-cost delta | Monthly burn delta | Immediate HC action
Base case | 0% | 0% | 0% | none
Conservative shock | +15% tokens on route-A | +10% model unit cost | +3.8% burn | delay non-critical hires, re-route to cached variants
Aggressive shock | +30% tokens | +20% unit cost | +9.6% burn | freeze new hires; engage contractors for triage; evaluate temporary price bump
Extreme shock | +50% tokens | +25% unit cost | +17.8% burn | open emergency board call; immediate capacity shedding; enterprise customer credit holdback review

Notes on how numbers are derived:
- Use per-token unit cost = weighted avg across model variants for the route (include hosted & dedicated).
- Model unit cost should include GPU + storage + amortized model licensing. If licensing unknown, use 1.2x baseline unit for quick-run.

Headcount/contractor trigger rules (short horizon):
- Soft freeze: if projected 30-day burn >5% — pause all non-critical external hires and contractor extensions. Finance lead issues pause notice.
- Redeploy: if burn >8% — redeploy 10% of product team FTEs currently on experimental projects to cost-mitigation sprints (cache work, batching, telemetry).
- Hard freeze + contingency hiring: if burn >12% — institute hiring freeze and accelerate contractor conversion for critical infra fixes only; legal to review existing enterprise credits.

Tactical pricing levers (fast-to-execute):
- Temporary surcharge (1–3 weeks) for new resource-intensive routes. Requires Product + Legal OK; pre-approved templates in Confluence.
- Offer limited-time SLA-adjusted discounted commit to select customers in exchange for committed usage (reduces unit cost).
- Auto-cap: enable soft caps on high-risk routes (graceful degradation) with customer notification.

Communication templates (copy-paste placeholders):
- Exec alert (1–2 lines): "Cost spike detected on Route X. Projected monthly burn impact ~+{x}% under current trend. Finance+Platform investigating; provisional mitigation: temporary promo hold + enable autoscale limit. Next update in 2 hours."
- Customer-facing (email): short transparency note + instructions to opt out of heavy features; include link to token-usage dashboard.

Open operational TODOs (near-term follow-up items):
- [ ] Add automated quick-sensitivity run to Console that calculates the three scenarios above (owner: Platform analytics; ETA: 2 weeks).
- [ ] Populate on-call roster into PagerDuty and test a mock drill (owner: Finance Ops; ETA: 1 week).
- [ ] Align legal with 24h templates for temporary price changes (owner: Legal).
- [ ] Create a small pre-authorized commit package (30d) product can offer without full contracts (owner: Monetization).

Quick list of questions for review session:
- What precise threshold should we use for "projected burn delta >5%" — absolute dollars or relative percent? (recommended: percent for cross-stage comparability, but include an absolute floor check).
- Are there routes we never cap (strategic customers)? Need a whitelist in Console.
- Who approves short-term surcharges? Pre-approval board of COO+Head of Finance+Legal?

Appendix: example 72h timeline for a moderate shock (projected +9% monthly burn):
Hour 0–2: Detect, assign on-call, run quick sensitivity, publish exec alert.
Hour 3–6: Product + Legal draft customer notices, Platform implements auto-cap on non-critical tenants, Finance issues soft hiring pause.
Hour 6–24: Deeper modeling (full workbook), assess alternative model variants and quantization options to reduce unit cost; present options to execs.
Day 2–3: Implement mid-term mitigations (short commit offers, temporary rerouting, cache rules), monitor burn in 4-6 hour cadence.

Record of changes / version notes:
- 2026-04-12 — doc created (Ethan Park)
- 2026-04-18 — added headcount trigger table and sample scenarios (Maya Chen)
- 2026-05-03 — added PagerDuty onboarding TODOs and legal template request (Noah Rivera)

Quick references (internal):
- Console: /costs/per-route and /alerts/burn-shock
- Confluence: pricing-harvest-playbook (linked)
- Slack channels: #fin-ops, #platform-payments, #sre-oncall

If you are on the on-call list: please read this doc, confirm availability for next week, and test the mock alert flow (link to PagerDuty test in linked_artifacts).

Questions / comments: leave inline or ping Ethan Park in #fin-ops.

- Exec alert (1–2 lines): \"Cost spike detected on Route X. Projected monthly burn impact ~+{x}% under current trend. Finance+Platform investigating; provisional mitigation: temporary promo hold + enable autoscale limit. Next update in 2 hours.\"
- Customer-facing (email): short transparency note + instructions to opt out of heavy features; include link to token-usage dashboard.

Open operational TODOs (near-term follow-up items):
- [ ] Add automated quick-sensitivity run to Console that calculates the three scenarios above (owner: Platform analytics; ETA: 2 weeks).
- [ ] Populate on-call roster into PagerDuty and test a mock drill (owner: Finance Ops; ETA: 1 week).
- [ ] Align legal with 24h templates for temporary price changes (owner: Legal).
- [ ] Create a small pre-authorized commit package (30d) product can offer without full contracts (owner: Monetization).

Quick list of questions for review session:
- What precise threshold should we use for \"projected burn delta >5%\" — absolute dollars or relative percent? (recommended: percent for cross-stage comparability, but include an absolute floor check).
- Are there routes we never cap (strategic customers)? Need a whitelist in Console.
- Who approves short-term surcharges? Pre-approval board of COO+Head of Finance+Legal?

Appendix: example 72h timeline for a moderate shock (projected +9% monthly burn):
Hour 0–2: Detect, assign on-call, run quick sensitivity, publish exec alert.
Hour 3–6: Product + Legal draft customer notices, Platform implements auto-cap on non-critical tenants, Finance issues soft hiring pause.
Hour 6–24: Deeper modeling (full workbook), assess alternative model variants and quantization options to reduce unit cost; present options to execs.
Day 2–3: Implement mid-term mitigations (short commit offers, temporary rerouting, cache rules), monitor burn in 4-6 hour cadence.

Record of changes / version notes:
- 2026-04-12 — doc created (Ethan Park)
- 2026-04-18 — added headcount trigger table and sample scenarios (Maya Chen)
- 2026-05-03 — added PagerDuty onboarding TODOs and legal template request (Noah Rivera)

Quick references (internal):
- Console: /costs/per-route and /alerts/burn-shock
- Confluence: pricing-harvest-playbook (linked)
- Slack channels: #fin-ops, #platform-payments, #sre-oncall

If you are on the on-call list: please read this doc, confirm availability for next week, and test the mock alert flow (link to PagerDuty test in linked_artifacts).

Questions / comments: leave inline or ping Ethan Park in #fin-ops.

source.title: Runtime scan: post-change behavioral audit and eval diffs
source.body:
TL;DR
We ran a focused post-change scan across the onboarding eval-suite after merging three runtime changes in the nightly canary: the new flash-attention dispatch, paged-KV page size tweak, and continuous-batching scheduler. Aggregate numbers look positive for throughput and median latency, but the behavioral diffs show a small number of high-impact failures in micro-sequence classification and structured-output prompts. This doc collects the experiment matrix, representative failing examples, initial root-cause hypotheses, and recommended mitigations.

Change summary
- flash-attention: instruction-level kernel selection improvement; expected ~5–10% token compute reduction on long sequences.
- paged-KV: decreased page size from 64k to 32k in the paged cache to reduce peak memory pressure on multi-tenant hosts.
- continuous-batching: more aggressive packing heuristics to prioritize throughput during low-util windows.

Why we ran this scan
- These three changes are orthogonal but interact at runtime (kernel selection affects cache access patterns; paged-KV tuning changes KV hit/miss behavior; batching changes per-request latency profiles).
- Onboarding evals (mix of short dialog, code-complete, and long-context summarization) surfaced differences from previous baseline. We need to decide: revert/cherry-pick, patch, or accept with mitigations.

Experiment matrix
- Baseline: nightly build before the three changes (nightly-2026-03-10).
- Candidate: nightly after merge (nightly-2026-03-23).
- Tests: 12 prompt sets sampled from onboarding-suite: short-chat-32, api-coding-snippets, long-doc-summary-4096, micro-regress-16, stop-token-tests, system-instructions, rerank-small, fewshot-QA-128, structured-json-output, safety-triggers, tokenization-edgecases, perf-stress-16x.
- Metrics captured: token-level latency distribution (p50/p90/p99), throughput (tokens/sec), model-behavior metrics (exact-match, top-1 MRR where applicable), stop-token fidelity, semantic-similarity delta (embedding cosine to baseline), and human-eval failure tags for sampled diffs.
- Traffic mode: shadowed traffic for hosted onboarding accounts + offline batch evals with seed-based RNG.

High-level numeric summary (candidate vs baseline)
- Median token latency: candidate -6.8% (improvement)
- P99 token latency: candidate +9.4% (regression in tail)
- Overall token throughput: candidate +18%
- Onboarding acceptance score (weighted behavioral metric across promptsets): delta -2.7 points (out of 100)
- Number of prompt-level behavioral regressions flagged: 27/1200 (2.25%) considered high-severity by QA reviewers

What stands out
- Tail latency increase correlates strongly with requests that hit paged-KV page boundary conditions (small KV pages caused more frequent page loads for certain long-context prompts).
- A cluster of structured-output prompts (structured_json_output, stop-token-tests) show subtle tokenization/stop emission shifts — outputs included extra trailing separators or missed termination tokens in ~0.6% of cases.
- API-coding-snippets: small but noticeable hallucination uptick for specific tests that rely on exact token-level alignment (test harness flagged 5 failing cases where the model inserted an extra import line).
- Continuous-batching improved aggregate throughput but introduced contention spikes which align with p99 increases in interactive short-chat-32 at scale.

Representative failing examples (baseline -> candidate)
1) structured_json_output/test-047:
- Prompt (shortened): "Extract metadata to JSON: title, authors, date"
- Baseline output: {"title":"GraphOps","authors":["K. Nguyen"],"date":"2024-11-12"}
- Candidate output: {"title":"GraphOps","authors":["K. Nguyen",""],"date":"2024-11-12"}
- Failure mode: spurious empty-string entry in list; downstream parser fails.

2) stop-token-tests/early-terminate-09:
- Prompt: "Return a bullet list, stop at token '---'"
- Baseline: "- item A\n- item B" (stops correctly)
- Candidate: "- item A\n- item B\n- " (extra trailing dash; model did not map the stop token properly)

3) api-coding-snippets/format-imports-11:
- Prompt: "Add imports for numpy and pandas and show example usage"
- Baseline: imports: import numpy as np\nimport pandas as pd
- Candidate: imports: import numpy as np\nimport pandas as pd\nimport pandas as pd
- Failure mode: duplicated import line introduced (affects test harness exact-match).

4) micro-regress-16/edge-token-boundary-05 (latency):
- Short 16-token requests observed p99 from 42ms->78ms; root cause traces show delayed KV page fetch during batching window.

Quantitative eval diffs by promptset (selected)
- structured_json_output: exact-match success rate 99.4% -> 98.3% (delta -1.1pp)
- stop-token-tests: stop fidelity 99.1% -> 98.2% (delta -0.9pp)
- api-coding-snippets: exact-match 97.6% -> 95.9% (delta -1.7pp)
- short-chat-32: p99 token latency 120ms -> 150ms (delta +30ms)

Initial hypotheses / signal tracing notes
- Hypothesis A: paged-KV smaller page size increases the number of page loads for some attention patterns, causing occasional blocking IO that shows up at p99. Data: low-level KV hit traces correlate 22/27 high-severity failures.
- Hypothesis B: flash-attention dispatch change can alter token ordering/timing of partial kernels, exposing a numerical edge-case in the tokenizer->detokenizer glue that manifests as spurious separators for structured outputs. Data: diffs concentrated on prompts with many punctuation tokens and short lists.
- Hypothesis C: continuous-batching packing heuristics occasionally coalesce interactive requests such that stop-token signals are delayed into the next packed micro-batch, which can cause stop misfires under rare schedules.

Root-cause investigation work done so far
- Replayed failing inputs with tracing enabled; captured kernel-level timestamps, KV page events, and batch packing boundaries.
- Ran binary ablation: (1) flash-attn on + paged-KV baseline + cont-batch baseline, (2) paged-KV change only, (3) cont-batch change only.
- Key observation: paged-KV-only reproduces most latency spikes; flash-attn-only reproduces a subset of structured-output token anomalies; cont-batch-only reproduces some stop-token edge cases in high-concurrency stress.

Short-term mitigations proposed (priority ordered)
1) Revert paged-KV page-size change on canary and re-run overnight sweep (owner: Priya Desai, ETA: 24h). Rationale: most p99 latency spikes tied to page boundary behavior.
2) Add stop-token passthrough in batching layer (fast path that honors stop markers at packing time) to reduce stop-token misfires (owner: Jordan Blake, ETA: 3 workdays).
3) Add post-detokenizer cleanup step for structured-json outputs during onboarding pipelines (heuristic fix: strip zero-length list entries and trailing separators) to unblock customer-facing onboarding flows while we root-cause numerics (owner: Maya Lin, ETA: 2 workdays).
4) Increase sampling of failing cases into nightly alerting and add a short, targeted human review queue for onboarding-critical prompts (owner: Ethan Park, ETA: 1 week).

Longer-term actions
- Kernel and tokenizer contract review: coordinate with serving-runtime and tokenization team to add unit tests around punctuation-heavy sequences and list-generation behaviors.
- Re-run kernel selector fuzzing across tokenization boundaries to catch dispatch-related numerical ordering issues.
- Re-evaluate paged-KV default page size per model-family and seq-length profile; consider adaptive page sizing heuristics.
- Revisit batching heuristics to add interactive-priority lane (dedicated packing rules for low-latency interactive requests).

Open questions / tradeoffs
- Reverting paged-KV reduces memory savings we intended to reclaim — acceptable short-term but will require a different approach to solve peak memory.
- Flash-attention dispatch fixes may require careful numerical validation on GPU kernels; could delay performance wins.
- How many of these diffs are truly customer-impacting vs. test-harness sensitivity? We rely on both exact-match and semantic-sim metrics; need more human-labeled triage for ambiguous cases.

Immediate next steps (this week)
- Priya to revert paged-KV and re-run canary sweep; post results to this doc (24h).
- Jordan to prototype stop-token passthrough in batching layer and submit draft PR (3d).
- Maya to implement the post-detokenizer cleanup as a bandaid in the onboarding pipeline and add regression tests (2d).
- Ethan to add failing examples to the human-review queue and coordinate with QA for triage (1d).

Appendix A: full heatmap links and artifacts
- Per-prompt heatmap (candidate vs baseline): gs://redwood-evals/2026-03/postchange-heatmaps-v2.jsonl
- Traces for representative failing requests: gs://redwood-traces/2026-03/pagedkv-flash-failures.tar.gz
- PRs: runtime PR #1427 (flash-attn dispatch), runtime PR #1431 (paged-KV change), scheduler PR #78 (continuous-batching heuristics).

Appendix B: quick repro commands (for engineers)
- Repro offline: python tools/run_eval.py --suite onboarding-small --build nightly-2026-03-10 --out baseline.jsonl
- Repro candidate: python tools/run_eval.py --suite onboarding-small --build nightly-2026-03-23 --out candidate.jsonl
- Diff generation: python tools/diff_eval.py baseline.jsonl candidate.jsonl --metrics token-latency,exact-match,stop-fidelity --out diffs.csv

Notes to reviewers
- This is a working audit; please add comments inline on Confluence page linked above or attach traces to the JIRA ticket JIRA-RINF-893. If you have suggestions for other fast mitigations (esp. for structured-output), flag them in the PR notes.

End of doc.
