3) Add per-vector checksum (simple XOR or CRC32) during embed serialization and verify at reranker side. If checksum mismatch present, can identify mid-flight corruption vs explicit truncation.
4) Build microbench in C to exercise quantization postprocess code with buffer sizes that matched failing payloads. Run under valgrind / asan in an isolated env.
5) Temporarily disable quantization pack_bits fastpath (fall back to slower safe path) on a small % of traffic to check reduction in failure rate.

Notes to self / gotchas:
- Watch out for hidden coercion: numpy arrays vs python lists might serialize differently into msgpack (float32 vs float64). If embedding output is float32 and RPC expects float64, some libs re-cast implicitly changing byte size and length calculations.
- When adding checksum, ensure we don't change the vector serialization format for rest of infra — include checksum as separate header field.
- Reranker side logging is noisy; add a correlation id early and propagate across both services to make tracing simpler. Current request IDs are already propagated but sometimes lost on worker retries.

Slack threads / tickets for context:
- #eng-applied-ml: thread ts 167827 (linked above) has initial reports from QA and a quick sample of failing texts.
- INF-2456 (jira): "Intermittent embed dim mismatch causing rerank 500s" — add findings to ticket and update with reproducer.
- PR 412: small guardfix in reranker to return 400 on shape mismatch (merged to staging, pending canary).

Minimal repro (pasteable) - python:
```
import requests
import random

EMBED_URL = 'https://staging.embed.redwood/api/v1/embeddings'
RERANK_URL = 'https://staging.rerank.redwood/api/v1/re-rank'
HEADERS = {'Authorization': 'Bearer xxxxx', 'Content-Type': 'application/json'}

# single row batch stress
for i in range(2000):
    texts = ['doc-%d: ' % i + ''.join(random.choice('abcd\\u200b\\u0000') for _ in range(1200))]
    r = requests.post(EMBED_URL, json={'inputs': texts}, headers=HEADERS, timeout=15)
    ev = r.json()
    vec = ev['vectors'][0]
    if len(vec) != 1024:
        print('FAIL', i, 'len=', len(vec))
        # send to reranker to observe behavior
        rr = requests.post(RERANK_URL, json={'vectors': [vec]}, headers=HEADERS, timeout=15)
        print('RERANK_STATUS', rr.status_code, rr.text)
        break
```

Decision points to escalate:
- If msgpack lib swap stops failures -> raise changelist to move away from fastpath on all replicas.
- If C microbench shows memory reuse bug -> open high-priority bug for quantization lib and quarantine affected builds.
- If checksum shows corruption in flight -> investigate network/proxy layer (envoy filters, MTU, chunking) and engage infra.

Summary / current status:
- Root cause not yet confirmed. Most likely culprit is an off-by-one truncation in embedding postprocessing or serialization edge with fast msgpack path.
- Temporary guards reduce user-facing errors but add latency due to retries. Need to complete A/B msgpack test + checksum instrumentation to pinpoint.

Next actions (for tomorrow):
- Run edge_case_runner with full byte dumps (Maya)
- Deploy msgpack A/B on 10% traffic (Jordan)
- Write small C harness for quantization buffer reuse (me)
- Add lightweight checksum and rollout to staging (for telemetry only)

-- end of bisect journal entry (Ethan)
text:
Quick log / scratchpad while bisecting the embeddings -> rerank anomaly.

Context:
- Symptom: sporadic 500 responses from rerank endpoint and long tail latency spikes (p95->p99) when running embedding+rerank pipeline for search reranks. Some batches return 400s/500s with an error about 'vector dimension mismatch' while other batches from the same input set succeed.
- Reported by QA on 2026-03-08; intermittent, surface-level repro via nightly job.
- Affects: hosted embedding->rerank flow (model combo: text-embedding-4-small + reranker-xl), Dedicated instances occasionally exhibit the same behavior.

Observed timeline (local reproduction attempts):
- 2026-03-09 09:12: kicked off minimal repro (50 queries) against staging rerank service behind envoy. 5 failures (dimension mismatch) and 3 latency spikes > 2s.
- 2026-03-09 10:02: instrumented logs + added embedding vector dump for failed items.
- 2026-03-10 14:30: deployed additional guards in staging (validate dims before calling rerank) — confirmed guard drops failing requests but did not explain root cause.

What "dimension mismatch" looks like in logs:
- ERROR reranker.service: received vectors with shape (16, 1023) but reranker expected 1024
- Trace snippet: RuntimeError: Expected embedding dim=1024, got 1023 at /srv/rerank/model/runner.py:184
- When this happens, upstream embedding service logs show the same request id and embed output shape = 1023. Sometimes the embed RPC includes an extra null token in the payload prior to serialization.

Commands / quick repro tried (notes & results):
1) Direct curl to embedding service (staging):
   curl -X POST https://staging.api.redwood/embed -H 'Authorization: Bearer xxx' -d '{"texts": ["test doc
line2"]}'
   -> response: vectors: [[...1024 floats...]] (ok)
2) Batch size stress test (python):
   python tools/batch_repro.py --batch-sizes 1,2,4,8,16 --n-repeats 100
   -> Failures seen at batch size 8 and 16 only. Pattern: failures roughly 1-2 per 1000 requests.
3) Test with truncated content boundary cases (nulls / unicode):
   - Inserted Unicode BOM, zero-width spaces, and raw 0x00 characters. No direct correlation, though some failing requests contained non-printable chars.
4) Direct reranker call with manually constructed vectors (simulate off-by-one):
   python tools/send_rerank.py --vectors shapes: (16,1023) -> reranker responds 400 with the exact runtime error.

Server-side observations:
- Embedding service is using model text-embedding-4-small with quantization pipeline. There is a final postprocessing step that trims trailing zeros introduced by quantization step 'pack_bits' for some GPUs. Suspect race in that step when batched across multiple inputs.
- Embedding service logs indicate occasional serialization truncation in fastpath (msgpack) when payload > X bytes — suspicious.
- Rerank service expects standardized 1024 dim float32 vectors. It validates shape but only after deserializing — sometimes the validation is bypassed if an exception is raised earlier in the pipeline, causing a 500 instead of 400.

Hypotheses (ranked):
1) Off-by-one truncation in embedding postprocessing (most likely) — some codepath strips one float from vector when buffer boundary aligns a certain way. Triggered by larger batch sizes or particular tokenization outcomes.
2) Serialization bug in msgpack/fastpath used for inter-service RPC — for large vectors the writer occasionally omits the last element.
3) Memory reuse / pooling bug: buffer slice view gets truncated due to concurrent reuse (C extension bug in quantization lib).
4) Model variant mismatch: sometimes embedding call returns 1023 because we routed to an older model variant (wrong tokenizer) — less likely given logs show same model id.
5) Upstream client encoding (frontend) occasionally sending arrays of length 1023 due to a bad padding loop — lower priority because failures also initiated internally (nightly job).

Logs / artifacts attached (local):
- /tmp/repro/2026-03-09/fails-ids.txt -> 17 request ids (used to correlate across services)
- /tmp/repro/2026-03-09/embed-dumps/ -> raw JSON bodies of embed responses for failed requests (vector lengths included)
- sample failing request id example: rq_20260309_9c12f8 -> embed vector length 1023, payload size 8192 bytes, msgpack header: 0x93 0xCA ...

Immediate mitigations applied:
- Added pre-call validation in router: if embed vector len != expected -> return 400 + telemetry (prevents 500s). Deployed to staging 2026-03-10 15:10.
- Increased retries in batch worker: if any vector shape mismatch, retry single-item embed for that text. Reduced user-facing errors but still see latency tail due to retrying.

Reproduction plan (next steps):
1) Narrow down on batch-size boundary: run tight stress test with seeds and instrument low-level serialization bytes. Script: tools/edge_case_runner.sh --batch 8 --seed 1234 --n 20000. Log raw bytes to /tmp/edge_bytes.
2) Swap msgpack lib version to safe (non-fast) implementation on a staging replica to see if failure rate changes. (A/B test)
3) Add per-vector checksum (simple XOR or CRC32) during embed serialization and verify at reranker side. If checksum mismatch present, can identify mid-flight corruption vs explicit truncation.
4) Build microbench in C to exercise quantization postprocess code with buffer sizes that matched failing payloads. Run under valgrind / asan in an isolated env.
5) Temporarily disable quantization pack_bits fastpath (fall back to slower safe path) on a small % of traffic to check reduction in failure rate.

Notes to self / gotchas:
- Watch out for hidden coercion: numpy arrays vs python lists might serialize differently into msgpack (float32 vs float64). If embedding output is float32 and RPC expects float64, some libs re-cast implicitly changing byte size and length calculations.
- When adding checksum, ensure we don't change the vector serialization format for rest of infra — include checksum as separate header field.
- Reranker side logging is noisy; add a correlation id early and propagate across both services to make tracing simpler. Current request IDs are already propagated but sometimes lost on worker retries.

Slack threads / tickets for context:
- #eng-applied-ml: thread ts 167827 (linked above) has initial reports from QA and a quick sample of failing texts.
- INF-2456 (jira): "Intermittent embed dim mismatch causing rerank 500s" — add findings to ticket and update with reproducer.
- PR 412: small guardfix in reranker to return 400 on shape mismatch (merged to staging, pending canary).

Minimal repro (pasteable) - python:
```

import random

EMBED_URL = 'https://staging.embed.redwood/api/v1/embeddings'
RERANK_URL = 'https://staging.rerank.redwood/api/v1/re-rank'
HEADERS = {'Authorization': 'Bearer xxxxx', 'Content-Type': 'application/json'}

# single row batch stress
for i in range(2000):
    texts = ['doc-%d: ' % i + ''.join(random.choice('abcd\\u200b\\u0000') for _ in range(1200))]
    r = requests.post(EMBED_URL, json={'inputs': texts}, headers=HEADERS, timeout=15)
    ev = r.json()
    vec = ev['vectors'][0]
    if len(vec) != 1024:
        print('FAIL', i, 'len=', len(vec))
        # send to reranker to observe behavior
        rr = requests.post(RERANK_URL, json={'vectors': [vec]}, headers=HEADERS, timeout=15)
        print('RERANK_STATUS', rr.status_code, rr.text)
```

- If C microbench shows memory reuse bug -> open high-priority bug for quantization lib and quarantine affected builds.
- If checksum shows corruption in flight -> investigate network/proxy layer (envoy filters, MTU, chunking) and engage infra.

Summary / current status:
- Root cause not yet confirmed. Most likely culprit is an off-by-one truncation in embedding postprocessing or serialization edge with fast msgpack path.
- Temporary guards reduce user-facing errors but add latency due to retries. Need to complete A/B msgpack test + checksum instrumentation to pinpoint.

source.title: Runtime daily scratch & triage — Ethan
source.content:
03/11-12 quick log / brain-dump

Morning: oncall handoff notes -> saw spike in 95p latencies on model-x small (12:10-12:40 UTC).
- Symptoms: sudden tail latency increases (95p from ~120ms -> ~450ms) across 3 nodes in pool-rw-us-west.
- Immediate mitigation applied: rolled traffic from model-x-small@v1.4 -> model-x-small@v1.3 (rollback) at 12:28. Latencies recovered within ~3m.
- Hypothesis: new kernel/attention change in v1.4 interacts poorly with very frequent short sequences (avg seq len 14, many single-token prompts).

Notes from quick triage:
- Repro locally? yes, can reproduce on my dev-box (A10G) by firing continuous 1t prompts with KV cache warmup disabled.
- Repro steps (dev): 
  1) checkout PR branch: git fetch origin refs/pull/4123/head:pr-4123 && git checkout pr-4123
  2) run ./tools/run_local_infer.sh --model model-x-small --batch 1 --seq 14 --disable-kv-cache 
  3) observe p95 ~420-480ms after ~30s of steady load.
- Observable diff: commit a7f2b3 added an early-exit attention path that avoids kernel A but uses kernel B for very small heads. Suspect kernel B has higher overhead at short seq lengths due to loop unrolling and poor vectorization on A10G.

Immediate TODOs (prioritized):
- [ ] Add perf regression unit test targeting short-seq steady-state (Alex to pair). -> aim EOD 03/12.
- [ ] Instrument to collect kernel selection histogram per-request (attach metadata to trace). -> I will drop a tiny patch to runtime/trace (PR stub linked).
- [ ] Re-run compare on GCP T4 and AWS A10G to see hardware-specific delta. -> Luis owns AWS runs, Maya on GCP.
- [ ] Add fallback policy: if p95 exceeds threshold by >2x within 5m, auto-fallback to previous kernel (config flag). -> design doc sketch below.

Scratch design (fallback policy)
- config: kernel_fallback: {window_sec:300, factor:2.0, cooldown_sec:900} (per-model override)
- runtime flow: metric aggregator monitors per-model p95 (rolling window) -> if exceed -> mark candidate kernel as suspect -> route new requests to fallback kernel until p95 recovers for 3 consecutive windows
- tradeoffs: possible oscillation, increased cost. Add leaky bucket to prevent flip-flop.

Quick kernel notes (raw)
- kernel A: vectorized multi-head fused-attn; efficient for medium->long sequences; lower startup overhead.
- kernel B: per-head unrolled loops, optimizes tiny sequences but we see higher branch mispredictions on Ampere A10G -> plausible CPU fallback/warp divergence.
- Check compiler flags used in CI (we might have toggled -O3 -> -Ofast?). Could alter auto-vectorization heuristics.

Commands / snippets I used today:
- perf-ish run: sudo perf record -g -- ./bin/infer --model model-x-small --seq 14 --batch 1 --disable-kv-cache
- quick flamegraph: ./tools/flamegraph.pl perf.data > flame.svg && open flame.svg
- kernel selection debug: RUNTIME_DEBUG=kernel_select ./bin/infer ...

Open questions / followups:
- Is quantized kernel variant affected similarly? I didn't repro on q4h; need to verify.
- Why only 3 nodes? Are they pinned to a particular kernel version or HW revision? Check node-labels and gpu microcode versions.
- Could scheduling/affinity change cause contended memory paths? Check dmesg / nvidia-smi logs for those hosts.

Meeting notes (quick) 03/12 sync with Maya/Luis
- Agreed: short-term rollback if p95 regression hits customers.
- Action: I will open an incident PR that implements kernel histogram traces + simple alert (Grafana) -> target: 03/13 morning.
- Luis to run cross-hardware A/B test (A10G vs T4) and post CSV results.

Small hacks / ideas to test
- micro-batch pre-warm: keep a tiny steady background stream of 1-token requests to keep kernel warm (cheap on cost?). Pro: hides startup overhead. Con: steady extra qps and accounting complexity.
- adaptive kv prefetch: for sequences below threshold, enable a cheap mini-kernel to prime caches.

Personal todo (short term)
- [ ] Push PR adding kernel hist trace + minimal metric export (inc bpf label) (tracking: PR 4123 is base; create pr-4123-trace)
- [ ] Add regression test (unit harness) for short-seq p95
- [ ] Coordinate with infra to roll canary with kernel fallback enabled on one pool
- [ ] Write short postmortem once root cause pinned

Random notes to self:
- Remember to update CONSOLE runbook if we add auto-fallback. Need clear rollback steps and ownership.
- Gather list of customers on pool-rw-us-west for targeted comms if incident escalates.

End of scratch. (file saved quick; will tidy into PR description later)

source.title: Prelaunch operator daybook
source.content:
Purpose: quick solo-run notes for release cutover. Lightweight, choppiest — my live checklist and reminders. Not the canonical runbook; pared-down for single-operator day run.

Status: draft. Will handoff to oncall if I need to step away. Add any final backports here and blast to #releases.

SUMMARY TIMELINE (my target)
- T-90m: wake + coffee, brief the oncall (if oncall handed over), verify staging smoke green
- T-45m: merge remaining small backports, kick CI for canary image build, start Canary deployment window
- T-20m: smoke test canary, sanity check telemetry, open comms post templates
- T-0m: traffic switch (20% -> 100% over 5min), monitor P99/P95, CPU/GPU util, queue depth
- +30m: confirm rollout healthy, tidy PRs, update postmortem stub if any anomalies

QUICK CHECKS (preflight)
- auth: ensure keyrotation job completed (last run: 2026-03-09 23:10 UTC)
- certs: edge TLS certs show valid in cert dashboard (no expiry within 30 days)
- infra: node-pools autoscaler config unchanged since last release? (run: gcloud container node-pools describe)
- perf baseline: p95 latency for /v1/generate <= 210ms under canary profile (use viz link below)

BACKPORT / CHERRY-PICK NOTES (todo / PRs to merge)
- REL-4821: backport fix for kv-cache eviction off-by-one — PR-9987 (merge to release-2026.03)
- REL-4817: small audit-log metadata rename — PR-9999 (needs rebase; small conflict in schema.json)
- quick & dirty: add healthcheck timeout increase (PR-10001) for host-gateway when downstream slow

COMMANDS I RUN OFTEN (put these in terminal)
- build canary image: ./tools/build_image.sh --tag release-2026.03-canary
- deploy canary: kubectl apply -f k8s/canary-deployment.yaml -n redwood-prod-canary
- check pods: kubectl get pods -n redwood-prod-canary -o wide
- stream logs (gateway): kubectl logs -f svc/gateway -n redwood-prod-canary --since=10m
- curl smoke: curl -sS -X POST https://canary.api.redwood.ai/v1/generate -d '{"input":"smoke test"}' --header 'Authorization: Bearer $SMOKE_KEY' -v

SMOKE TEST LIST (minimal to pass)
- auth token accepted, 200 OK
- token-level billing ID in response metadata present
- truncated generation length consistent with prompt
- streaming chunk ordering preserved (quick eyeball)

METRICS / DASHBOARDS TO WATCH (open these before T-20)
- Canary dashboard: https://dash.internal/redwood/canary-release-2026-03
- Latency: p50/p95/p99 for inference route (/v1/generate)
- Errors: 5xx rate, model-serving timeouts, queue retries
- Cost signal: tokens/sec and GPU utilization (watch sudden spikes)

ALERTS / ESCALATION TRIGGERS (my thresholds)
- >1% 5xx sustained for 5min -> pause rollout and investigate
- P95 > 500ms for 3 consecutive 1-min buckets -> rollback to previous model variant
- GPU OOM events -> scale down rollout, escalate to infra (Roberto)

ROLLBACK PLAN (fast)
- Abort canary scaling: kubectl scale deployment/gateway --replicas=0 -n redwood-prod-canary
- Revert traffic in router: switch route config to release-2026.02 (hash a1b2c3)
- Trigger emergency: #releases: "Rolling back release-2026.03 due to XX" (use template below)

COMMS TEMPLATES (copy-paste)

title: Prelaunch operator daybook
preview:
Purpose: quick solo-run notes for release cutover. Lightweight, choppiest — my live checklist and reminders. Not the canonical runbook; pared-down for single-operator day run.

Status: draft. Will handoff to oncall if I need to step away. Add any final backports here and blast to #releases.

SUMMARY TIMELINE (my target)
- T-90m: wake + coffee, brief the oncall (if oncall handed over), verify staging smoke green
- T-45m: merge remaining small backports, kick CI for canary image build, start Canary deployment window
- T-20m: smoke test canary, sanity check telemetry, open comms post templates
- T-0m: traffic switch (20% -> 100% over 5min), monitor P99/P95, CPU/GPU util, queue depth
- +30m: confirm rollout healthy, tidy PRs, update postmortem stub if any anomalies

QUICK CHECKS (preflight)
- auth: ensure keyrotation job completed (last run: 2026-03-09 23:10 UTC)
- certs: edge TLS certs show valid in cert dashboard (no expiry within 30 days)
- infra: node-pools autoscaler config unchanged since last release? (run: gcloud container node-pools describe)
- perf baseline: p95 latency for /v1/generate <= 210ms under canary profile (use viz link below)

BACKPORT / CHERRY-PICK NOTES (todo / PRs to merge)
- REL-4821: backport fix for kv-cache eviction off-by-one — PR-9987 (merge to release-2026.03)
- REL-4817: small audit-log metadata rename — PR-9999 (needs rebase; small conflict in schema.json)
- quick & dirty: add healthcheck timeout increase (PR-10001) for host-gateway when downstream slow

COMMANDS I RUN OFTEN (put these in terminal)
- build canary image: ./tools/build_image.sh --tag release-2026.03-canary
- deploy canary: kubectl apply -f k8s/canary-deployment.yaml -n redwood-prod-canary
- check pods: kubectl get pods -n redwood-prod-canary -o wide
- stream logs (gateway): kubectl logs -f svc/gateway -n redwood-prod-canary --since=10m
- curl smoke: curl -sS -X POST https://canary.api.redwood.ai/v1/generate -d '{"input":"smoke test"}' --header 'Authorization: Bearer $SMOKE_KEY' -v

SMOKE TEST LIST (minimal to pass)
- auth token accepted, 200 OK
- token-level billing ID in response metadata present
- truncated generation length consistent with prompt
- streaming chunk ordering preserved (quick eyeball)

METRICS / DASHBOARDS TO WATCH (open these before T-20)
- Canary dashboard: https://dash.internal/redwood/canary-release-2026-03
- Latency: p50/p95/p99 for inference route (/v1/generate)
- Errors: 5xx rate, model-serving timeouts, queue retries
- Cost signal: tokens/sec and GPU utilization (watch sudden spikes)

ALERTS / ESCALATION TRIGGERS (my thresholds)
- >1% 5xx sustained for 5min -> pause rollout and investigate
- P95 > 500ms for 3 consecutive 1-min buckets -> rollback to previous model variant
- GPU OOM events -> scale down rollout, escalate to infra (Roberto)

ROLLBACK PLAN (fast)
- Abort canary scaling: kubectl scale deployment/gateway --replicas=0 -n redwood-prod-canary
- Revert traffic in router: switch route config to release-2026.02 (hash a1b2c3)
- Trigger emergency: #releases: "Rolling back release-2026.03 due to XX" (use template below)

COMMS TEMPLATES (copy-paste)
- Canary OK (T-20): "Canary for release-2026.03 deployed. Running smoke checks. Will update at T-0. Owner: @EthanP"
- Rollout start (T-0): "Starting rollout -> release-2026.03. Targeting 100% traffic in 5m. Monitor: p95 latency, errors. Contact: @EthanP, @oncall-sre"
- Rollback notice: "Rolling back release-2026.03 to release-2026.02 due to elevated 5xx. Investigating root cause. Oncall: @EthanP"

NOTES TO SELF / REMINDERS (messy)
- If schema conflict in REL-4817, prioritize metadata surface (analytics) — patch can wait until post-cutover if blocking
- Keep an eye on redis swap; last time we saw queue backpressure when batch size increased
- If need to step away, ping Jan 11:30 (Jana) and handoff log link here: https://notes.local/handovers/2026-03-10

POST-RUN TODOS (after +60m)
- tidy PRs and tag release notes
- open short incident stub if anything >5% errors
- update canary settings to default once stable

OPEN QUESTIONS / TODO (short)
- Should we bump kv-cache TTL in this release or defer? (pros: fewer cold-cache misses; cons: larger mem footprint)
- Confirm final canary traffic ramp plan with product (was 20% -> 100% in 5m ok?)

ENDPOINTS & CONTACTS
- Ops pager: +1-555-0110 (use only after Slack triage)
- Infra owner: Roberto Gomez
- Release owner (product): Maya Thompson

Last manual note: breathe. Keep the checklist tight and do not multitask while the ramp is active. If in doubt, pause the rollout and ask for a second set of eyes.

-- EP

source.title: Bridge notes: PM↔Eng sync prep (API/Console/Dedicated/Private)
source.body:
Purpose
Quick working notes for my 1:1 with Lena (ENG lead) — surface urgent blockers, alignment for next two sprints, and follow-ups on Dedicated capacity pilot and Private deployment checklist. Keep this intentionally rough so I can edit live in the meeting.

Meeting goals (30 min)
- Sync status on Dedicated pilot throughput tuning + expected SLA risk window.
- Decide on console rollout gating criteria for v0.9 (feature flags, telemetry knobs).
- Align on support/resolution plan for two customer escalations tied to Private onboarding.
- Agree owners and timelines for action items; quick check on staffing constraints.

Context snapshot (things Lena will care about)
- Dedicated pilot (customer: SimpleForms): observed bursty tail latency on long sequences; throughput nominal but p99 spikes during autoscaling events. Potential cause: slow KV cache priming + full-model reloads when burst exceeds pool.
- Console v0.9: includes new rollout UI, canary config pages, and token-cost breakdown. QA found an edgecase where route-level budgets are not applied on fallback routing.
- Private onboarding: two customers in pilot (FinAccount & HealthVault). FinAccount hit encryption key rotation failure in staging; HealthVault has incomplete audit log entries when running on customer-managed KMS.

Open questions I want to ask / confirm
1) Dedicated: Are we comfortable with the temporary workaround (increase min pool to avoid reloads) while infra team patches hot path? Cost impact estimate?
2) Console gating: do we require end-to-end rollback tested on staging with live routing fallbacks before exposing to customers? If yes, who signs off?
3) Private: what's the do-not-deploy list for KMS variants until we have a root cause? Can we triage with SOC on the next call?

Key metrics to reference (have them ready in case Lena asks)
- Dedicated pilot: average token/sec per GPU: 1.9k, 95th latency: 180ms, 99th latency: 620ms during bursts.
- Autoscale events: 18 in last 72 hours, 6 of them coincident with p99 spikes.
- Console bug repro rate: 3/12 E2E tests fail (routing budget mismatch).
- Private pilot: FinAccount error rate in staging: 4.2% during key rotation window; HealthVault missing logs: ~1.3% of requests.

Proposed talking points / short scripts
- Dedicated pilot: "We can accept a temporary cost bump to increase min pool by 20% for the next 2 weeks while infra completes the priming fix. We should cap exposure by setting a global enrolment flag on the customer so we can throttle if costs escalate."
- Console: "If we ship v0.9 without an explicit rollback validated against routing fallbacks, we'll risk impacting multi-tenant customers. I'd like to make rollback validation a hard gate; propose eng SRE + product sign-off."
- Private: "For FinAccount, prioritized immediate rollback of recent KMS client change and run a focused replay in staging. For HealthVault, prod-like replay with audit logging turned up to debug-level for a short window."

Risks and escalations (quick list)
- Risk: Increasing min pool will increase monthly commit usage; finance wants a clear estimate. Mitigation: timebox to two weeks and measure cost delta daily. Owner: me (PM) to notify finance.
- Risk: Console feature flag bug could silently bypass budget tiers for customers on fallback routing. Mitigation: disable fallback routing for high-tier customers until fix; add e2e test. Owner: Lena / console eng lead.
- Risk: Private KMS issues may trigger compliance review for FinAccount. Mitigation: engage compliance immediately and schedule a postmortem with SOC. Owner: Samir (security eng).

Proposed concrete actions (owner — ETA)
- ENG: Hotfix KV cache priming path to avoid full reloads. (Samir Gupta — target branch + PR by 2026-03-18)
- PM: Send finance cost-impact note and request temporary budget approval for min-pool increase. (Ethan Park — by 2026-03-13)
- ENG/Console: Add regression E2E that asserts route-level budget applies on fallback. (Lena Rodriguez — tests merged by 2026-03-20)
- PM/Support: Open dedicated incident with FinAccount and set temporary KMS rollback. (Ethan Park / Support — immediate, update by 2026-03-12)
- Security: Triage missing audit logs for HealthVault and provide remediation plan. (Samir Gupta — initial findings by 2026-03-14)
- ENG: Feature-flag toggle to disable fallback routing for enterprise customers until fix confirmed. (Lena — toggle available by 2026-03-15)

Notes for follow-ups after the 1:1
- If Lena agrees to min-pool increase, I will email finance and copy infra with the exact commit numbers and cost forecast (showing daily expected burn).
- Capture canary rollout checklist in Confluence page and attach the new regression test link.
- Schedule cross-functional postmortem for FinAccount KMS incident (include SOC, compliance, support, infra).

Quick reference (links to logs/tests)
- Dedicated pilot dashboards: https://monitoring.redwood.ai/dedicated/simpleforms (note: filter by request_id SF-*)
- Console E2E failures: GH workflow run: https://github.com/redwood/console/actions/runs/987654321
- FinAccount staging logs: s3://redwood-staging/finaccount/kms-rotation/2026-03-10

Talking tips (tone & escalation)
- Keep the 1:1 focused: explicit ask is permission to proceed with timeboxed min-pool increase and sign-off on rollback gating for console.
- If Lena pushes back on cost, offer a phased approach: increase min pool by 10% now, then additional 10% if spikes persist.
- Stress the customer-facing risk if we move forward without rollback validation. Use concrete customer examples.

Personal prep checklist (what I'll bring to the meeting)
- Short cost projection table (2 rows: current vs timeboxed min-pool increase).
- Repro steps for the console routing bug and the failing e2e screenshot(s).
- Copy of staging error logs for FinAccount and HealthVault.

End-state I want from this meeting
- Clear go/no-go on temporary min-pool increase and who to notify.
- Agreement that console rollout has a rollback validation gate and assigned owner for sign-off.
- Confirmed owners and dates for KMS/Private follow-ups and commitment to a postmortem window.

Scratch/divergent ideas (rapid brainstorm)
- Could we implement a short-term cache-warmup lambda that runs synthetic warmup traffic when autoscale slots spin up? Cost: limited; benefit: reduce p99 spikes. Ops complexity though.
- For console, add a flag that forces budget evaluation at the routing proxy layer so fallback never bypasses budgets. Might be more invasive but future-proof.

End of notes.

Action log (copy here after meeting)
- [ ] Fill in after 1:1 — who committed to what, and any changes to ETAs.

- Console E2E failures: GH workflow run: https://github.com/redwood/console/actions/runs/987654321
- FinAccount staging logs: s3://redwood-staging/finaccount/kms-rotation/2026-03-10

Talking tips (tone & escalation)
- Keep the 1:1 focused: explicit ask is permission to proceed with timeboxed min-pool increase and sign-off on rollback gating for console.
- If Lena pushes back on cost, offer a phased approach: increase min pool by 10% now, then additional 10% if spikes persist.
- Stress the customer-facing risk if we move forward without rollback validation. Use concrete customer examples.

Personal prep checklist (what I'll bring to the meeting)
- Short cost projection table (2 rows: current vs timeboxed min-pool increase).
- Repro steps for the console routing bug and the failing e2e screenshot(s).
- Copy of staging error logs for FinAccount and HealthVault.

End-state I want from this meeting
- Clear go/no-go on temporary min-pool increase and who to notify.
- Agreement that console rollout has a rollback validation gate and assigned owner for sign-off.
- Confirmed owners and dates for KMS/Private follow-ups and commitment to a postmortem window.

Scratch/divergent ideas (rapid brainstorm)
- Could we implement a short-term cache-warmup lambda that runs synthetic warmup traffic when autoscale slots spin up? Cost: limited; benefit: reduce p99 spikes. Ops complexity though.
- For console, add a flag that forces budget evaluation at the routing proxy layer so fallback never bypasses budgets. Might be more invasive but future-proof.

End of notes.

Action log (copy here after meeting)
- [ ] Fill in after 1:1 — who committed to what, and any changes to ETAs.

source.title: AZ42 incident debug scratchpad
source.content:
Scratchpad / breadcrumbs for Customer AZ-42 outage (draft internal notes)

Summary:
- Customer: AZ-42 (on Dedicated pool: eu-west-1-dedicated-8xA100)
- Symptom: elevated p95/p99 latency and intermittent 500s for text-generation route (/v1/generate) between 2026-03-10 07:48 UTC and ~10:05 UTC.
- Impact: user-facing errors on a small subset of route keys (~4% of requests) during peak.
- Current status: mitigated via temporary routing to fallback model variant. Root cause still under investigation.

Primary timeline (reconstructed from logs + slack):
- 2026-03-10 07:48 UTC — First anomalies: spike in latency metrics for AZ-42 per-route dashboard (p95 from 120ms -> 820ms).
- 2026-03-10 07:52 UTC — Error rate bump: ~20 500 responses/min. Alerts triggered (pager).
- 2026-03-10 07:53 UTC — SRE oncall (Maya) paged, started triage.
- 2026-03-10 07:58 UTC — Observed errors: a mix of 500 internal and 502 upstream. See sampled error snippet below.
- 2026-03-10 08:04 UTC — Attempted quick mitigation: increased scale target for dedicated pool (manual). No immediate effect.
- 2026-03-10 08:18 UTC — Rolled routing policy to fallback model variant (open-7b-compat) for AZ-42 customer. Error rate dropped to baseline over ~6 minutes.
- 2026-03-10 09:12 UTC — Began deeper investigation: pulled pod logs, heap/kv-cache traces, and GPU telemetry.
- 2026-03-10 09:45 UTC — Hypothesis refinement and reproduce attempts on staging.
- 2026-03-10 10:05 UTC — Post-mitigation: routing remains to fallback variant while we prepare a fix.

Commands & snippets tried (raw outputs truncated for brevity):
1) curl against production endpoint (sample request)
> curl -i -X POST https://api.redwood.ai/v1/generate -H 'Authorization: Bearer ****' -d '{"model":"redwood/open-7b-4k-quant","prompt":"hello"}'
HTTP/1.1 500 Internal Server Error
Content-Type: application/json
{"error":{"message":"internal error: runtime/execution failed: kernel mismatch on device 0","type":"internal_error"}}

2) Pod logs (serving-runtime) around failure window
> kubectl -n redwood logs serving-runtime-5f7d9 -c runtime --since=30m
[2026-03-10T07:51:23Z] WARN: request 10f4a1 - kv-cache: cache-miss for prefix key=az42/conv/abc123
[2026-03-10T07:51:23Z] ERROR: request 10f4a1 - execution failed: RuntimeError("CUDA error: unspecified launch failure")
[2026-03-10T07:51:24Z] ERROR: model-loader - failed to pin weights: EIO: read error at offset 0x1b000000
[2026-03-10T07:51:25Z] INFO: autoscaler - observed cpu spike on node n-45 (85% -> 98%), scaling decision queued

3) GPU telemetry (nvidia-smi snapshot)
> nvidia-smi --query-gpu=timestamp,utilization.gpu,utilization.memory,memory.used --format=csv -l 1 --filename telemetry.csv
Timestamp, GPU Util, Mem Util, Mem Used
2026-03-10 07:51:22, 92 %, 84 %, 26.1 GiB
2026-03-10 07:51:23, 12 %, 10 %, 26.1 GiB  # note sudden drop in utilization; suspicious

4) Model loader debug run on staging (reproduced):
> ./runtime --load-model redwood/open-7b-4k-quant --quantize q4_0 --log-level debug
[DEBUG] model-loader: mmap() size=3.2G offset=0x1b000000
[DEBUG] model-loader: reading weight chunk 98/240
[ERROR] model-loader: EIO while reading weight file: input/output error (errno=5)
[CRITICAL] execution: aborting due to incomplete weights

Error snippets collected:
- "kernel mismatch on device 0"
- "CUDA error: unspecified launch failure"
- "EIO: read error at offset 0x1b000000"
- "execution aborted: incomplete weights"
- occasional "Segmentation fault (core dumped)" in kernel logs

Initial hypotheses (ordered by likelihood):
1) Corrupt disk I/O for the weight cache on the dedicated nodes (EIO errors from model-loader). Weight mmap/read failing leads to partial load and then kernel mismatch crashes at runtime.
2) A recent kernel-level patch (rolling kernel upgrade) on that cluster introduced a CUDA driver/ABI mismatch for the optimized kernels used by our runtime. This would explain kernel mismatch/unified failures across multiple pods + segmentation faults.
3) Autoscaler churn: nodes being Cordon/Drained and GPUs moving may have caused concurrent loads where model loading partially completes while another process evicts content, leading to inconsistent shared mmap-backed state (race).
4) A regression in the quantized weight generator (post-release PR #987) that introduced an occasional corrupt chunk for certain weight offsets.

Evidence mapping to hypotheses:
- Hypothesis 1 supported by explicit EIO in model-loader (pod logs) + model-loader reproduce on staging also showing EIO when reading the same offset.
- Hypothesis 2 supported by the "kernel mismatch" phrasing in the API error and segfaults; need to correlate with kernel/driver version history on the nodes. Check node upgrade window.
- Hypothesis 3 plausible due to autoscaler logs showing scaling during the window. Need to check k8s events for cordon/drain around 07:50–07:52.
- Hypothesis 4 less likely but not ruled out: attempt to verify weight files checksum across nodes.

Immediate next steps runbook (what we did / plan):
- Short term mitigation (applied): routed AZ-42 traffic to fallback model variant (open-7b-compat) which does not use the problematic kernel path.
- Validate integrity: ran sha256 checksums of weight files on several affected nodes and compared to golden artifacts in S3 (s3://redwood-models/open-7b-4k-quant/sha256.txt). Results: 3/8 nodes had mismatched chunks that map to offset range 0x1b000000–0x1c000000.
- Node health audit: flagged nodes n-45, n-46, n-53 for immediate reprovisioning (suspected faulty disk segments).
- If node reprovisioning is needed: follow standard reclaim + reprovision + bring-back workflow (SRE runbook ref: confluence link placeholder).
- Long term: add CRC validation on mmap reads in model-loader and fall back to streaming load if mmap fails. Add an alert for EIO reads from model-loader.

Outstanding asks / items for other teams:
- infra/platform: confirm whether there was any rolling kernel/driver upgrade in eu-west-1-dedicated-8xA100 cluster between 2026-03-09 and 2026-03-10. If yes, provide kernel/driver versions for nodes.
- storage: investigate disk-level SMART logs for n-45/n-46/n-53 and verify if underlying EBS/disk had errors during the window.
- runtime: quick PR to add an mmap/read retries and checksum compare (hotfix candidate). See PR idea: github.com/redwood-inference/runtime/compare/hotfix-mmap-retry.

Draft internal status update (short) - to paste into incident channel:
> Quick update: AZ-42 experienced elevated latency/500s ~07:48–08:18 UTC. Root cause is under investigation; early evidence points to partial weight read failures (EIO) on several dedicated nodes leading to runtime kernel mismatches. We have routed AZ-42 to a fallback model variant and are reprovisioning flagged nodes. Next: infra to confirm kernel/driver state, storage to share SMART logs, runtime to push read-retry checksum hotfix.

Draft outbound customer note (for CS/AM to review) — tone: apologetic, technical summary, mitigation + next steps:
Subject: Incident summary for service disruption on 2026-03-10 (AZ-42)
Hi <Customer-Contact>,
We’re sorry for the disruption you saw today between ~07:48–08:18 UTC. Brief summary: a subset of nodes in your dedicated pool had intermittent disk read errors while loading model weights which caused a small percentage of requests to fail. We immediately routed your traffic to a compatible fallback model to restore service and are reprovisioning the affected hosts.
What we did:
- Routed traffic to fallback model variant to restore availability.
- Identified and flagged 3 nodes with read errors; starting reprovisioning.
- Validated model integrity on healthy hosts.
What we’ll do next:
- Rebuild affected nodes and verify end-to-end (ETA: 24–48 hours).
- Deploy a runtime hotfix (read-retry + checksum validation) to reduce risk of recurrence (target: 3 business days).
- Follow up with a post-incident report with full timeline and remediation steps.
We appreciate your patience — reach out to <AM name> if you need immediate help.

Notes / loose ideas for post-incident work:
- Instrument model-loader with per-chunk CRC and emit metrics for any chunk mismatch.
- Make weight loads resilient: if mmap fails, fall back to streaming and verify chunk integrity.
- Add an EIO/high-read-error alert in infra monitoring for dedicated pools.
- Add route-level graceful degradation: if a particular node family reports kernel or mmap errors, temporarily exclude nodes until reprovisioned.

Appendix: useful quick commands used during triage
- kubectl get events -n redwood --field-selector involvedObject.name=serving-runtime-5f7d9
- sudo smartctl -a /dev/nvme0n1 | egrep -i "error|fail|attr"
- gcloud compute ssh node-n-45 --zone=eu-west-1 --command "journalctl -u kubelet --since '2026-03-10 07:30' | tail -n 200"
- aws s3 cp s3://redwood-models/open-7b-4k-quant/weights-idx-*.bin ./ && sha256sum weights-idx-*.bin

Last notes to self:
- Double-check whether PR #987 touched the weight layout offsets referenced in these errors.
- Keep AZ-42 on fallback until we confirm clean reprovision + runtime hotfix.
- Schedule postmortem runbook and invite infra/storage/runtime owners.

-- E.P.

- kubectl get events -n redwood --field-selector involvedObject.name=serving-runtime-5f7d9
- sudo smartctl -a /dev/nvme0n1 | egrep -i \"error|fail|attr\"
- gcloud compute ssh node-n-45 --zone=eu-west-1 --command \"journalctl -u kubelet --since '2026-03-10 07:30' | tail -n 200\"
- aws s3 cp s3://redwood-models/open-7b-4k-quant/weights-idx-*.bin ./ && sha256sum weights-idx-*.bin

Last notes to self:
- Double-check whether PR #987 touched the weight layout offsets referenced in these errors.
- Keep AZ-42 on fallback until we confirm clean reprovision + runtime hotfix.
- Schedule postmortem runbook and invite infra/storage/runtime owners.

-- E.P.

source.title: One-week-out personal handoff notes
source.body:
Purpose:
Quick personal notes to prepare for next three readiness syncs and the go/no-go decision meeting one week out. This is my working checklist + questions I want to raise in the huddle. Not polished — notes to self, known unknowns, and owners for follow-ups.

Quick summary (as I see it right now):
- Feature set: v2 inference routing + canary rollback path. Backend + console done, dedicated autoscale still testing under load.
- Hard blockers: one SLO regression in latency p95 on multi-tenant tests (investigating kernel fallback behavior), and a legal doc pending for EU data processing addendum.
- Soft blockers: some dashboard tiles showing stale metrics (frontend cache TTL misconfigured), small UX copy items.

Agenda I want to run in the 1-week readiness sync (keep it tight):
1) Quick state check (engineering, infra, product) — 5m
2) Blockers and mitigations — 10m
3) Go/No-go checklist walk (we'll mark Yes/No/Partial) — 10m
4) Rollout plan / canary schedule + rollback window confirmation — 5m
5) Owners & actions (who does what by when) — 5m

Preliminary Go / No-Go checklist (my notes; we'll confirm with teams):
- Infra: autoscale policies applied to dedicated pool, QA load test >= target throughput -> PARTIAL (autoscale works but burst capacity test flaky)
- Serving runtime: quantized model variant validated on dev infra with perf parity -> YES
- Latency SLOs: p50/p95/p99 measured against baseline -> NO (p95 regression under multi-tenant stress)
- Security/compliance: EU DPA signed or parallel plan with controls -> NO (DPA pending legal)
- Observability: traces + token-level cost telemetry enabled and dashboards green -> PARTIAL (backend metrics ok, frontend stale tiles)
- Rollback plan: documented and rehearsed (smoke + quick rollback PR) -> YES
- Customer comms: templates ready and owners assigned -> YES

Risks I'm worried about (talk points):
- If p95 regression is due to kernel fallback, we may need an emergency kernel pin or temporary route to smaller model -> ask infra/serving for root cause and quick mitigation plan.
- DPA delay: product/legal need to confirm whether EU customers can be onboarded with mitigations (no EU tenant enablement until signed) — need decision on whether to ship non-EU only.
- Observability gaps during canary could blind us to token-cost anomalies (ask console team to enable ephemeral retention for the canary window).

Owners & immediate actions (my proposed assignments):
- Investigate p95 regression and produce RCA draft (owner: Luis Ortega) — due: 2026-03-12 (priority: high)
- Legal: escalate DPA signature and propose mitigations (owner: Maya Chen) — due: 2026-03-13
- Frontend: fix metrics TTL, validate dashboard refresh (owner: Nicole Ruiz) — due: 2026-03-12
- Infra: re-run burst autoscale test with controlled concurrency and log kernel selection decisions (owner: Arun Nair) — due: 2026-03-12
- Release engineering: prepare rollback playbook and verify playbook steps in dry-run (owner: Priya Shah) — due: 2026-03-13
- Communications: finalize launch email + status page copy (owner: me, Ethan Park) — due: 2026-03-13

Notes to self / things to ask in the meeting:
- Ask Luis if the p95 regression reproduces locally; if yes, request a minimal repro and timeline to fix vs. temporary routing to lighter variant.
- Confirm with Maya whether DPA can be signed with a redline within 48h; if not, propose limiting EU rollout.
- Remind console team to flip ephemeral retention during canary (cost impact discussed in #launch-ops).
- Confirm canary schedule: suggested 2% -> 10% -> 50% over 4 hours; need SRE to own the first 2 hours.

Meeting prep checklist (my personal):
- Pull latest results from JIRA-4521 (RCA notes) -> done? check before sync.
- Attach perf graphs to agenda doc (p95 over time, KV cache hit-rate).
- DM Priya to confirm rollback dry-run time slot (calendar).
- Draft two comms templates: customer-facing and internal ops.

Open questions / unresolved:
- Can we patch the kernel fallback quickly or do we need a model variant? (impact on release date)
- What is the legal team's ETA for the DPA, and is there an interim attestation we can ship under?
- Who will own the cost spike monitoring for the canary window (SRE or platform)?

Follow-up plan after the sync (how I will triage):
1) Triage p95 RCA thread (highest priority). If not resolved within 24h, escalate to Eng director for decision on delay vs partial rollout.
2) Confirm DPA status; if unsigned by 2026-03-13 EOD, prepare EU-excluded rollout messaging.
3) Run final canary on staging with observability retention increased; collect token-cost delta.

Personal meeting notes template (quick fill during the meeting):
- Time: ___
- Attendees: ___
- Key decisions: ___
- Action items (owner -> due): ___
- Next check-in: ___

Final quick thoughts: keep the Go/No-go tight and binary where possible. If too many PARTIALs, escalate to product leadership to decide criteria for proceed-with-conditions vs hold. I want at least the p95 and DPA items either YES or signed mitigation plan before I recommend full rollout.

-- Ethan

(References: JIRA-4521, PR#789, confluence:Launch-Playbook-v2, slack:#launch-ops-thread-327)

source.title: Preflight smoke & rollback runbook (personal notes)
source.content:
Purpose:
- Quick personal checklist and meeting prep for this week's release train weekly (final gate). Narrow focus: smoke verification, go/no-go signal matrix, immediate rollback play, communication + owners.

Attendees I expect: Lina (SRE lead), Arjun (Serving runtime), Samira (Product/PM), me (Release owner), + optional: Ops on-call (TBD).

Meeting anchors (15–25m):
1) Status quick-fire (what changed since last sync) — 3m
2) Blockers / regression surface — 5m
3) Smoke test results + readiness signal (pass/fail) — 7m
4) Go/no-go vote + immediate next steps (rollout window, canary ratio) — 5m
5) If NO, rollback trigger & communication plan — 3m

Go/No-Go signal matrix (personal shorthand):
- Critical infra: Dedicated pool capacity >= committed baseline AND no new kernel OOMs in last 24h -> pass/fail. Owner: Lina.
- API latency: p50 < 50ms, p95 < 220ms for 1k-sim load tests -> pass/fail. Owner: Arjun.
- Model quality sanity: eval set stable, no >7% drop in rouge/embedding-sim compared to canary -> pass/fail. Owner: Samira.
- Billing/quotas: quota bump applied for launch window, billing alerts linked -> pass/fail. Owner: me (Ethan).
- Security/compliance: KMS keys available and audit hooks enabled for private customers in canary -> pass/fail. Owner: Security on-call (copied).

Preflight smoke checklist (run these in order, tick locally):
- [ ] infra: deploy to staging-dedicated pool (tag: release-rt-8421) -> ensure all pods healthy
- [ ] health endpoints: /health, /metrics, /kv-cache-stats return OK within 2s
- [ ] small generation smoke: prompt="unit test: short" 5x -> responses non-empty, no exceptions
- [ ] latency benchmark: 100 concurrent requests sequentially to canary model variant; capture p50/p95
- [ ] kv cache correctness: start from checkpoint, ensure cache hits > 60% on repeated prefixes
- [ ] streaming paths: simulate SSE/streamed response for function-calls payload -> no truncated frames
- [ ] fallback policy: trigger artificial capacity constraint -> auto-fallback to smaller variant succeeds within 2s
- [ ] observability: token-level cost shows sane numbers for a 256-token generation across canary and baseline
- [ ] smoke on private: verify VPC peering + egress rules for a sample private customer (sanity ping)
- [ ] compliance: verify KMS rotation logs appear in audit log (today)

Quick commands / pointers (notes to self):
- Canary deploy branch: rollouts/pull/234 (GH).
- Smoke script path: tools/release/smoke/run_smoke_local.sh (use --profile canary).
- Load harness: load/mini-harness (100qps sim) -> currently configured for eu-west only.
- Rollback tag: vRT-8421-rollback (prebuilt image).

Immediate rollback play (<= 10m target to complete):
1) Pause rollout in Console (me) -> notify on #launch-announce -> call out canary id and timestamp.
2) Trigger rollback job: tools/release/rollback.sh --target vRT-8421-rollback (Arjun executes if I'm tied).
3) Validate rollback: smoke 5 short gens + health endpoints. Lina verifies infra stability.
4) Post-mortem note stub: CONFLUENCE:Launch-Postmortem-RT-8421 (I draft initial sections).

Communication protocol (who says what):
- If GO: I post a short message in #launch-announce with canary window, expected impact, customer list. Lina posts infra health confirmation. Samira posts product acceptance note.
- If NO/rollback: Arjun posts rollback confirmation + timeline, I escalate to leadership DM list and schedule follow-up post-mortem within 48 hours.

Action items I want to surface in the weekly:
- Lina: validate dedicated pool headroom and confirm autoscaler thresholds by EOD (due: 2026-03-11)
- Arjun: run full 1k-sim latency suite and paste p50/p95 into meeting notes (due: before meeting)
- Samira: re-run evals on holdout prompt set and highlight any hallucination spikes (due: 2026-03-11)
- Ethan (me): ensure billing quota applied & set alert runbook link (done/verify by 2026-03-11)
- Ops on-call: standby during canary window, ensure PagerDuty escalation is tested (due: pre-window test)

Open questions / risks (to raise early):
- Risk: quantized variant shows subtle quality degradation on long-form prompts > 512 tokens. Mitigation: limit canary to short-form initially. (Owner: Samira)
- Risk: private deployment KB keys not rotated on schedule for one customer (investigating). (Owner: Security)
- Question: Do we want 10% canary vs 5%? Lean 5% for first hour, escalate to 20% if stable. Need leadership ok.

Notes to self for 1:1 with Release Manager (post-meeting):
- confirm owners for post-mortem timeline and who drafts exec summary
- ask for green light on incremental canary bump policy (5% -> 20% threshold)
- capture any blockers that need Product Ops involvement for customer communication

Meeting follow-up template (to paste into Slack after call):
- Short: "GO/NO-GO: [GO/NO-GO]. Canary id: xxxxx. Window: hh:mm - hh:mm UTC. Actions: Lina(validate infra): status, Arjun(latency): results link, Samira(eval): summary."
- Longer: attach smoke logs + link to GH PR + rollback tag + postmortem stub link.

Personal quick reminders:
- Don't assume autoscaler has applied new thresholds until Lina confirms.
- If any metric looks marginal (p95 within 10% of threshold) -> call NO and run extended diag.
- Keep the meeting tight; we don't need a long demo, just binary signals.

End of notes.

(added 2026-03-11: small update after dry-run: KV cache hit rate on staging was 54% not 60% -> ask Arjun to re-check cache retention settings and increase warmup samples in harness)

- [ ] latency benchmark: 100 concurrent requests sequentially to canary model variant; capture p50/p95
- [ ] kv cache correctness: start from checkpoint, ensure cache hits > 60% on repeated prefixes
- [ ] streaming paths: simulate SSE/streamed response for function-calls payload -> no truncated frames
- [ ] fallback policy: trigger artificial capacity constraint -> auto-fallback to smaller variant succeeds within 2s
- [ ] observability: token-level cost shows sane numbers for a 256-token generation across canary and baseline
- [ ] smoke on private: verify VPC peering + egress rules for a sample private customer (sanity ping)
- [ ] compliance: verify KMS rotation logs appear in audit log (today)

Quick commands / pointers (notes to self):
- Canary deploy branch: rollouts/pull/234 (GH).
- Smoke script path: tools/release/smoke/run_smoke_local.sh (use --profile canary).
- Load harness: load/mini-harness (100qps sim) -> currently configured for eu-west only.
- Rollback tag: vRT-8421-rollback (prebuilt image).

Immediate rollback play (<= 10m target to complete):
1) Pause rollout in Console (me) -> notify on #launch-announce -> call out canary id and timestamp.
2) Trigger rollback job: tools/release/rollback.sh --target vRT-8421-rollback (Arjun executes if I'm tied).
3) Validate rollback: smoke 5 short gens + health endpoints. Lina verifies infra stability.
4) Post-mortem note stub: CONFLUENCE:Launch-Postmortem-RT-8421 (I draft initial sections).

Communication protocol (who says what):
- If GO: I post a short message in #launch-announce with canary window, expected impact, customer list. Lina posts infra health confirmation. Samira posts product acceptance note.
- If NO/rollback: Arjun posts rollback confirmation + timeline, I escalate to leadership DM list and schedule follow-up post-mortem within 48 hours.

Action items I want to surface in the weekly:
- Lina: validate dedicated pool headroom and confirm autoscaler thresholds by EOD (due: 2026-03-11)
- Arjun: run full 1k-sim latency suite and paste p50/p95 into meeting notes (due: before meeting)
- Samira: re-run evals on holdout prompt set and highlight any hallucination spikes (due: 2026-03-11)
- Ethan (me): ensure billing quota applied & set alert runbook link (done/verify by 2026-03-11)
- Ops on-call: standby during canary window, ensure PagerDuty escalation is tested (due: pre-window test)

Open questions / risks (to raise early):
- Risk: quantized variant shows subtle quality degradation on long-form prompts > 512 tokens. Mitigation: limit canary to short-form initially. (Owner: Samira)
- Risk: private deployment KB keys not rotated on schedule for one customer (investigating). (Owner: Security)
- Question: Do we want 10% canary vs 5%? Lean 5% for first hour, escalate to 20% if stable. Need leadership ok.

Notes to self for 1:1 with Release Manager (post-meeting):
- confirm owners for post-mortem timeline and who drafts exec summary
- ask for green light on incremental canary bump policy (5% -> 20% threshold)
- capture any blockers that need Product Ops involvement for customer communication

Meeting follow-up template (to paste into Slack after call):
- Short: \"GO/NO-GO: [GO/NO-GO]. Canary id: xxxxx. Window: hh:mm - hh:mm UTC. Actions: Lina(validate infra): status, Arjun(latency): results link, Samira(eval): summary.\"
- Longer: attach smoke logs + link to GH PR + rollback tag + postmortem stub link.

Personal quick reminders:
- Don't assume autoscaler has applied new thresholds until Lina confirms.
- If any metric looks marginal (p95 within 10% of threshold) -> call NO and run extended diag.
- Keep the meeting tight; we don't need a long demo, just binary signals.

End of notes.

(added 2026-03-11: small update after dry-run: KV cache hit rate on staging was 54% not 60% -> ask Arjun to re-check cache retention settings and increase warmup samples in harness)

source.title: Anchored Weekly KPI Notebook — Ethan Park
source.content:
Purpose / how I use this
- Quick running notebook for one-week sprints: capture metric blips, micro-hypotheses, tiny experiments, and things I want to remember for retros. Not a charter — ephemeral, editable.
- Anchor each week to one north-star micro-KPI + two leading indicators. Avoid more than 3 measurable things.

This week snapshot (week starting 2026-03-02)
- North-star micro-KPI: median 95th-percentile request latency on rtx-pool (50–512 tokens) <= 230ms.
  - Current: 247ms (up from 221ms last week).
  - Delta: +26ms, primary regression window 11:00–14:00 PT.
- Leading indicator A: cache hit ratio on prefix-cache >= 28%
  - Current: 24.6% (down 3.4ppt).
- Leading indicator B: average tokens/session for prod-chat flows (subset A) = 98 tokens
  - Current: 101 tokens (unchanged).
- Cost: estimated per-1M tokens token-cost (gpu-hours + infra) = $175 (est), +3% week-over-week.

Quick diagnostic notes (raw)
- Regression seems correlated with a change in routing weight that moved a small set of production traffic to a newer kernel variant (PR 678).
- Logs show timeouts and additional retries for requests hitting long prompt prefix lengths (>6KB). KV cache miss rates spiked for those customers.
- Observability: tracing shows a tail in kernel warmup on node types g4x-large. Need to validate scheduling behavior.

Hypotheses (micro experiments)
1) H1: Kernel variant v2.3 has a warmup penalty for certain sequence length distribution -> causes higher 95th.
   - Test: route 8% of traffic back to v2.2 for a canary period and compare 95th.
   - Quick metric: compare latency percentiles, retry rate, CPU spikes.
   - Slack note: flagged to Maya (thread linked).
2) H2: Autoscaler config leads to delayed new-pod readiness (affecting tail latency at midday).
   - Test: temporarily reduce scale-down grace to 90s and monitor.
   - Risk: potential overprovisioning cost. Keep for 48h only.
3) H3: Cache eviction policy too aggressive for high-entropy prefixes — improve TTL for keys seen >3x/day.
   - Test: bump TTL in canary pool for 12h window, measure cache-hit ratio and tokens/second.

Immediate action items (today / next 48h)
- [ ] Roll a 8% routing change to v2.2 (DEV ticket -> ENG-3421). Need SRE approval; pinged Leah.
- [ ] Launch short-lived autoscaler tweak on staging, run soak 6h, capture readiness metrics.
- [ ] Add additional logging around cache key size + prefix length for the top 10 customers by volume.
- [ ] Draft quick postmortem blurb if routing rollback needed; prepare customer-facing note template.

What to watch (dashboard widgets)
- 95th latency (tokens 50–512) split by kernel variant + node type.
- Cache hit ratio / eviction rate (last 24h, moving 1h).
- Retry rate and retry-origin (client vs server).
- Scaling events per minute (pod churn during 11:00–14:00 PT).

Retro notes (from previous week)
- Good: Canary for quantized-ops reduced median CPU by ~12% and saved ~$1.8k/week on the dev cluster — keep rolling.
- Bad: delayed decision to rollback v2.3 — should set a 2-hour decision window when canary effects exceed threshold.
- Weird: a storage node in us-west reported elevated iowait that coincided with one tail spike; unclear if related.

Personal process tweaks (meta)
- Keep this doc intentionally messy: humans over machines for flagging immediate pain.
- Shorten stand-up update to 90s: 15s metric, 30s action, 45s blocker + ask.
- Schedule a 20-minute "latency triage" on Friday with Maya + Leah.

Open questions / TODOs (notes to self)
- Q1: Is the traffic shift to v2.3 the root cause or a trigger for an orthogonal constraint (IO / scheduling)? Need A/B with same infra.
- Q2: If autoscaler is the culprit, how quickly can ops revert without manual intervention? Document rollback playbook.
- Q3: For customers affected by higher tail, do we offer temporary routing to dedicated capacity? Cost/comm tradeoffs — draft policy.

Customer-facing language (draft snippet)
- "We're investigating a subset of requests experiencing higher-than-expected latency during midday hours. We've identified a routing change as likely contributor and are rolling a targeted canary rollback while we complete deeper diagnostics. We'll follow up with affected teams individually by EOD."

Mini-retro checklist before Saturday
- [ ] Confirm rollback threshold and execute if 95th > 240ms for two consecutive 1h windows.
- [ ] Publish a short note in #prod-incident and tag top 5 customers.
- [ ] Capture a follow-up once we have kernel profiling results.

Reference snippets (links I keep returning to)
- ENG-3421: short notes on canary rollback plan (Jira).
- PR 678: kernel selection tweaks (GitHub) — went out last Thursday.
- Slack convo: SRE thread for midday tail (link above).

End-of-week reflection (prompt for retrospective)
- What small change this week most impacted my ability to move fast? (answer: excessive meetings on Friday — remove recurring unless strictly actionable)
- Where did I under-communicate? (customer comms during canary decisions)
- What hypothesis will I ruthlessly test next week? (H1: kernel warmup penalty)

---
Meta: save as rolling note (keep last 6 weeks), archive snapshots into Confluence if we end up producing a customer postmortem.

Notes-to-self: breathe. keep checklist short. ship small experiments and measure.

source.title: Weightbundle reassembly + streaming race - scratchpad
source.content:
Context / symptom:
- Intermittent model load failures during cold-starts on Dedicated pool (p95 of failures ~2/day on prod-dedicated-us-west). Failures usually occur when model is being downloaded from GCS and reassembled into weightbundle.
- Crash signature differs slightly across runs but common themes: tar/tarstream errors, missing shard parts, checksum mismatches.
- Replicated locally on k8s dev cluster but only under parallel-fetch stress (scale workers to 6).

Commands & repro steps I've been using:
- Parallel fetch (original strategy):
  gsutil -m cp -r gs://model-buckets/prod/model-abc/weights/ ./tmp/weights/
- Reassembly script (in runtime): python3 reassemble_weights.py --input ./tmp/weights --out ./model_bundle
- To reproduce race: run 6 fetch workers with staggered starts (0,0.2,0.5s) and run reassemble while workers still active.

Observed error snippets (copied from logs):
- "tarfile.ReadError: unexpected EOF while reading tar file"
- "RuntimeError: shard manifest mismatch: expected 8 parts, found 7 (missing part index 5)"
- "Checksum mismatch for blob weights_blob_03 (expected 2f4a1d..., got 7e3c9b...)"
- "ValueError: found overlapping tar header offsets, can't stream assemble"
- "OSError: [Errno 5] Input/output error during write: '/model_bundle/weights.part3'"

What I tried so far (results):
1) Serial download + assemble
   - gsutil without -m (single threaded) then run reassemble: failure rate dropped to ~0.1/day. Good signal that parallelism matters.
2) Increase chunk size for range requests (from 4MB to 16MB) in blob downloader
   - local: slightly fewer tar header corruption errors but still see checksum mismatches.
3) Force EOF-safe tar extraction (use tarfile.open(mode='r|*') streaming API)
   - Fixes some "unexpected EOF" exceptions but doesn't resolve missing-part errors.
4) Add a temporary per-object pre-download SHA256 check (download .sha256 sidecar then compare)
   - Catches checksum mismatch earlier; root cause still unknown (why expected vs actual differ).
5) Disable gzip content-encoding on client side (some intermediate proxy was gzipping responses)
   - Allowed tar streaming to be consistent in some repros.
6) Serial prefetch of manifest first, then fetch parts one-by-one using exponential backoff on failures
   - Lowers failure rate but increases cold-start by ~3s (undesirable).

Hypotheses (ordered by likelihood):
A) GCS eventual consistency / object overwrite race: a sharded upload/replace operation from model pipeline sometimes replaces a blob while a reader is mid-download -> partial reads + checksum mismatch.
   - Evidence: transient checksum mismatches and sometimes re-upload events in GCS logs (but timestamps fuzzy).
B) Reassembly code assumes deterministic ordering of streaming tar members but parallel downloads produce chunks that are concatenated in wrong order when writer scheduling interleaves writes -> overlapping headers/offset corruption.
   - Evidence: "overlapping tar header offsets" error and repro only when multiple workers write to same tmp dir without strict atomic rename.
C) Transfer-level proxy (internal CDN/proxy) occasionally injects Content-Encoding: gzip on-range responses, breaking tar streaming framing.
   - Evidence: disabling gzip on client helped in some runs. Also saw proxy logs with 1xx transforms.
D) Off-by-one in shard index parsing when manifest contains both numbered and hashed names -> occasionally drop part 0 or part N-1 during assemble.
   - Evidence: manifest parsing code has a brittle split('/') and int(index_str) parse; I added guard but haven't fully validated on all manifests.
E) Worker node disk I/O transient errors causing partial writes (less likely because I/O error logs are rare).

Immediate mitigation implemented in prod-dedicated:
- 2026-03-07: change reassembly to write to .tmp unique filenames (uuid) and atomically rename when complete to avoid cross-writer clobber.
- Pre-download of manifest and validate expected count before starting parallel fetch; if mismatch, abort and retry manifest fetch (adds small latency but reduces corrupted assemblies).
- Add per-part temporary filename + per-part sha256 validation; if mismatch, retry that part up to 3x before failing.
Result: failure rate reduced by ~70% in week after rollout.

Next investigative steps (todo):
- Reproduce exact "missing part" case with traffic capture: enable VPC flow logs + object change logs around upload time, coordinate with infra to preserve GCS event timing (INF-4821).
- Add an end-to-end test harness that simulates concurrent uploads + parallel downloads with deliberate mid-upload replacement to confirm hypothesis A.
- Add extra logging to reassembly: record per-part download start/end time, byte ranges requested, response headers (content-encoding, content-length, x-goog-generation) to correlate with proxy/GCS logs. Keep logs for 7 days behind a controlled retention.
- Audit manifest generation in the model publishing pipeline: ensure publishers write manifest atomically (upload manifest last once all parts are finalized). If not, change uploader to manifest-first-lease/lock or manifest-last.
- Consider small-time window (500ms) drain before allowing readers after an upload completes (not ideal but possible quick fix).

Notes to self / reminders: 
- Check GH PR 1298 changes to downloader retry logic; rebase my patch on top once merged.
- Talk to infra about disabling transformation proxy for range requests (Riley to open infra ticket).
- If we can't fix uploader atomicity, plan to add server-side reassembly: upload parts to a staging prefix + single finalize call that moves to production prefix.

Useful commands I used during triage:
- Verify sha locally: sha256sum ./tmp/weights/part-03 || openssl dgst -sha256 part-03
- Inspect tar headers: hexdump -C part-03 | head -n 40
- Python quick check for tar: python3 -c "import tarfile,sys; t=tarfile.open(sys.argv[1]); print([m.name for m in t.getmembers()[:20]])" part-03
- GCS object meta: gsutil ls -L gs://model-buckets/prod/model-abc/weights/part-03

Attachments / scratch snippets:
- small script I used to emulate mid-upload replacement (replace_emulator.py) - drop in /tmp and run with two threads.
- grepped logs for "overlapping tar header" and saved to /var/log/reassembly/overlap-grep-2026-03-04.txt

Final thought: root cause is most likely a combination: uploader not atomic (manifest appears before all parts finalized) + our parallel-download + naive file-write strategy. Priority: make reassembly atomic and add per-part integrity checks, then harden publishers.

Open questions for owners:
- Maya: can you confirm model-publish pipeline guarantees (do we upload manifest last)?
- Riley: can infra provide proxy logs for range requests between 2026-03-01 and 2026-03-08 for bucket model-abc?

-- Ethan

C) Transfer-level proxy (internal CDN/proxy) occasionally injects Content-Encoding: gzip on-range responses, breaking tar streaming framing.
   - Evidence: disabling gzip on client helped in some runs. Also saw proxy logs with 1xx transforms.
D) Off-by-one in shard index parsing when manifest contains both numbered and hashed names -> occasionally drop part 0 or part N-1 during assemble.
   - Evidence: manifest parsing code has a brittle split('/') and int(index_str) parse; I added guard but haven't fully validated on all manifests.
E) Worker node disk I/O transient errors causing partial writes (less likely because I/O error logs are rare).

Immediate mitigation implemented in prod-dedicated:
- 2026-03-07: change reassembly to write to .tmp unique filenames (uuid) and atomically rename when complete to avoid cross-writer clobber.
- Pre-download of manifest and validate expected count before starting parallel fetch; if mismatch, abort and retry manifest fetch (adds small latency but reduces corrupted assemblies).
- Add per-part temporary filename + per-part sha256 validation; if mismatch, retry that part up to 3x before failing.
Result: failure rate reduced by ~70% in week after rollout.

Next investigative steps (todo):
- Reproduce exact \"missing part\" case with traffic capture: enable VPC flow logs + object change logs around upload time, coordinate with infra to preserve GCS event timing (INF-4821).
- Add an end-to-end test harness that simulates concurrent uploads + parallel downloads with deliberate mid-upload replacement to confirm hypothesis A.
- Add extra logging to reassembly: record per-part download start/end time, byte ranges requested, response headers (content-encoding, content-length, x-goog-generation) to correlate with proxy/GCS logs. Keep logs for 7 days behind a controlled retention.
- Audit manifest generation in the model publishing pipeline: ensure publishers write manifest atomically (upload manifest last once all parts are finalized). If not, change uploader to manifest-first-lease/lock or manifest-last.
- Consider small-time window (500ms) drain before allowing readers after an upload completes (not ideal but possible quick fix).

Notes to self / reminders: 
- Check GH PR 1298 changes to downloader retry logic; rebase my patch on top once merged.
- Talk to infra about disabling transformation proxy for range requests (Riley to open infra ticket).
- If we can't fix uploader atomicity, plan to add server-side reassembly: upload parts to a staging prefix + single finalize call that moves to production prefix.

Useful commands I used during triage:
- Verify sha locally: sha256sum ./tmp/weights/part-03 || openssl dgst -sha256 part-03
- Inspect tar headers: hexdump -C part-03 | head -n 40
- Python quick check for tar: python3 -c \"import tarfile,sys; t=tarfile.open(sys.argv[1]); print([m.name for m in t.getmembers()[:20]])\" part-03
- GCS object meta: gsutil ls -L gs://model-buckets/prod/model-abc/weights/part-03

Attachments / scratch snippets:
- small script I used to emulate mid-upload replacement (replace_emulator.py) - drop in /tmp and run with two threads.
- grepped logs for \"overlapping tar header\" and saved to /var/log/reassembly/overlap-grep-2026-03-04.txt

Final thought: root cause is most likely a combination: uploader not atomic (manifest appears before all parts finalized) + our parallel-download + naive file-write strategy. Priority: make reassembly atomic and add per-part integrity checks, then harden publishers.

Open questions for owners:
- Maya: can you confirm model-publish pipeline guarantees (do we upload manifest last)?
- Riley: can infra provide proxy logs for range requests between 2026-03-01 and 2026-03-08 for bucket model-abc?

-- Ethan
text:
Context / symptom:
- Intermittent model load failures during cold-starts on Dedicated pool (p95 of failures ~2/day on prod-dedicated-us-west). Failures usually occur when model is being downloaded from GCS and reassembled into weightbundle.
- Crash signature differs slightly across runs but common themes: tar/tarstream errors, missing shard parts, checksum mismatches.
- Replicated locally on k8s dev cluster but only under parallel-fetch stress (scale workers to 6).

Commands & repro steps I've been using:
- Parallel fetch (original strategy):
  gsutil -m cp -r gs://model-buckets/prod/model-abc/weights/ ./tmp/weights/
- Reassembly script (in runtime): python3 reassemble_weights.py --input ./tmp/weights --out ./model_bundle
- To reproduce race: run 6 fetch workers with staggered starts (0,0.2,0.5s) and run reassemble while workers still active.

Observed error snippets (copied from logs):
- \"tarfile.ReadError: unexpected EOF while reading tar file\"
- \"RuntimeError: shard manifest mismatch: expected 8 parts, found 7 (missing part index 5)\"
- \"Checksum mismatch for blob weights_blob_03 (expected 2f4a1d..., got 7e3c9b...)\"
- \"ValueError: found overlapping tar header offsets, can't stream assemble\"
- \"OSError: [Errno 5] Input/output error during write: '/model_bundle/weights.part3'\"

What I tried so far (results):
1) Serial download + assemble
   - gsutil without -m (single threaded) then run reassemble: failure rate dropped to ~0.1/day. Good signal that parallelism matters.
2) Increase chunk size for range requests (from 4MB to 16MB) in blob downloader
   - local: slightly fewer tar header corruption errors but still see checksum mismatches.
3) Force EOF-safe tar extraction (use tarfile.open(mode='r|*') streaming API)
   - Fixes some \"unexpected EOF\" exceptions but doesn't resolve missing-part errors.
4) Add a temporary per-object pre-download SHA256 check (download .sha256 sidecar then compare)
   - Catches checksum mismatch earlier; root cause still unknown (why expected vs actual differ).
5) Disable gzip content-encoding on client side (some intermediate proxy was gzipping responses)
   - Allowed tar streaming to be consistent in some repros.
6) Serial prefetch of manifest first, then fetch parts one-by-one using exponential backoff on failures
   - Lowers failure rate but increases cold-start by ~3s (undesirable).

Hypotheses (ordered by likelihood):
A) GCS eventual consistency / object overwrite race: a sharded upload/replace operation from model pipeline sometimes replaces a blob while a reader is mid-download -> partial reads + checksum mismatch.
   - Evidence: transient checksum mismatches and sometimes re-upload events in GCS logs (but timestamps fuzzy).
B) Reassembly code assumes deterministic ordering of streaming tar members but parallel downloads produce chunks that are concatenated in wrong order when writer scheduling interleaves writes -> overlapping headers/offset corruption.
   - Evidence: \"overlapping tar header offsets\" error and repro only when multiple workers write to same tmp dir without strict atomic rename.

   - Evidence: disabling gzip on client helped in some runs. Also saw proxy logs with 1xx transforms.

Immediate mitigation implemented in prod-dedicated:
- 2026-03-07: change reassembly to write to .tmp unique filenames (uuid) and atomically rename when complete to avoid cross-writer clobber.
- Pre-download of manifest and validate expected count before starting parallel fetch; if mismatch, abort and retry manifest fetch (adds small latency but reduces corrupted assemblies).
- Add per-part temporary filename + per-part sha256 validation; if mismatch, retry that part up to 3x before failing.
Result: failure rate reduced by ~70% in week after rollout.

Next investigative steps (todo):
- Reproduce exact \"missing part\" case with traffic capture: enable VPC flow logs + object change logs around upload time, coordinate with infra to preserve GCS event timing (INF-4821).
- Add an end-to-end test harness that simulates concurrent uploads + parallel downloads with deliberate mid-upload replacement to confirm hypothesis A.
- Add extra logging to reassembly: record per-part download start/end time, byte ranges requested, response headers (content-encoding, content-length, x-goog-generation) to correlate with proxy/GCS logs. Keep logs for 7 days behind a controlled retention.
- Audit manifest generation in the model publishing pipeline: ensure publishers write manifest atomically (upload manifest last once all parts are finalized). If not, change uploader to manifest-first-lease/lock or manifest-last.
- Consider small-time window (500ms) drain before allowing readers after an upload completes (not ideal but possible quick fix).

Notes to self / reminders: 
- Check GH PR 1298 changes to downloader retry logic; rebase my patch on top once merged.
- Talk to infra about disabling transformation proxy for range requests (Riley to open infra ticket).
- If we can't fix uploader atomicity, plan to add server-side reassembly: upload parts to a staging prefix + single finalize call that moves to production prefix.

Useful commands I used during triage:
- Verify sha locally: sha256sum ./tmp/weights/part-03 || openssl dgst -sha256 part-03
- Inspect tar headers: hexdump -C part-03 | head -n 40
- Python quick check for tar: python3 -c \"import tarfile,sys; t=tarfile.open(sys.argv[1]); print([m.name for m in t.getmembers()[:20]])\" part-03
- GCS object meta: gsutil ls -L gs://model-buckets/prod/model-abc/weights/part-03

Attachments / scratch snippets:
