source.title: SDK v2 launch — all-hands scratch
source.content:
Purpose and context
- Quick internal sketch for the all-hands talk track + Q&A prep for SDK v2 rollout.
- Not public-facing: heavy on migration caveats and who-does-what.

High-level launch message (one-liner)
- "Redwood SDK v2: simpler streaming, better defaults, and ergonomics for production — with a clear migration path and a two-month deprecation window for v1."

Audience buckets (who we need to address)
- External: product engineers using hosted API, platform engineers running private deployments, customers on Dedicated (comms to be handled by AMs).
- Internal: DevEx, Docs, Support, Sales, Solutions Eng, Security.

Key things to make sure are covered in the talk
- Why v2? developer ergonomics + streaming billing accuracy + consistent default timeouts.
- Non-breaking by default for existing requests, but some helpers/shortcuts deprecated.
- Migration timeline: soft-launch today -> docs + examples live -> 8-week migration window -> automatic redirect/compat layer disabled at EoW-8.
- Support plan: extended support for enterprise customers with designated AMs.

Top-level talking points for the CEO/CTO (concise)
- Emphasize user pain: "we heard SDK quirks + surprising streaming costs".
- v2 solves those with safer defaults and clearer hooks for caching & batching.
- No customer will be cut off immediately — our priority is low-friction migration.

FAQ (predicted / prep answers)
- Q: Will this break existing apps?
  A: For most, no. v1 remains available for 8 weeks while we surface migration guides and an opt-in compatibility shim. (See migration FAQ doc link.)
- Q: What about private deployments / on-prem customers?
  A: Private customers get a configurable rollout — we will coordinate with their infra contacts and provide a v1 backport package if necessary.
- Q: Does this change billing?
  A: No billing model change — but stream framing changes may alter per-request token accounting for some edge cases. We’ll flag customers likely affected and coordinate.
- Q: Are there performance regressions?
  A: Benchmarking shows parity/slight improvement on common paths. We have a dedicated regressions dashboard in Console (link).

Migration notes (developer-focused)
- Main diffs: new Client.constructConversation API (replaces startConversation), streaming framing uses chunked=true default, helper sendMessage now returns structured events.
- Depracated helpers: .legacySend etc. -> migration codemods available in GH PR.
- Example migration snippet (to put in docs):
  - v1: client.startConversation({onDelta})
  - v2: client.constructConversation().on('delta', handler).stream()
- Local test checklist for engs: run unit tests with both clients, smoke test streaming in staging, validate token accounting.

Rollout timeline (draft)
- Week 0 (soft-launch): announce internal, open beta docs live, GH examples updated.
- Weeks 1–3: invite early adopters + proactive outreach to heavy users.
- Weeks 4–7: migration sprints, automated codemod runs for org-owned repos, Support triage channel active.
- Week 8: compatibility shim will be deprecated; final notice + migration help.

Owners & responsibilities
- DevEx (Maya): docs, codemods, migration guide.
- SDK eng (Rafael): rollout automation, compatibility shim, monitoring.
- Support (Priya): surface affected customers, prepare canned responses and escalation flow.
- Comms (Ethan): all-hands script + internal announcement draft + Slack post.

Dry-run checklist (pre-all-hands)
- [ ] CEO/CTO rehearse 10-min slot with developer callouts.
- [ ] 1 pager for AMs + Sales with migration impact matrix.
- [ ] Publish migration guide and code examples to docs site.
- [ ] Open a dedicated #sdk-v2-migration Slack channel and pin resources.
- [ ] Update Console banners for affected projects (temporary banner template).

Open questions / TODOs
- TODO: confirm exact date for compatibility shim shutdown (legal/SLAs consult).
- TODO: finalize the list of customers we proactively contact (Priya to provide list by 2026-03-12).
- TODO: finalize the wording for the external blog (team draft needed).

Notes to self (conversational)
- Keep the all-hands message upbeat: migration is a win for users but we must own friction.
- Prepare 2–3 short real-customer examples where v2 reduces surprise cost or simplifies code.
- Rehearse a quick demo of a streaming conversation (30s) — shows value.

Slack/links to call out in comms
- #sdk-launch-meeting (internal dry-run)
- #support-biz (customer escalations)
- GH PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/sdk/pull/432
- Docs draft: CONFLUENCE-REDWOOD-SDK-V2-PLAN

End of scratchpad — eyeballs welcome, please add to comments inline.

source.title: Pager surge prophylaxis notes
source.content:
Context:
This is a working note collecting hypotheses, quick mitigations, and follow-ups from the recent week of pager surge events (week of 2026-01-12 -> 2026-01-19) where we saw three high-volume waves originating from degraded telemetry and one SLO burn alert that cascaded to noise. The goal: synthesize repeatable prophylactic steps that reduce noisy pages without eroding detection fidelity for real incidents.

Executive summary (short):
- Symptom cluster: rapid increase in low-fidelity alerts (thresholds firing repeatedly), followed by partial metric disappearance for dependent dashboards.
- Primary pain points: alert duplication across layers, missing histograms for token-latency, and an SLO rule that double-counted a synthetic bucket leading to a false SLO burn.
- Immediate outcome: on-call teams manually muted several routes and applied emergency fallbacks, which reduced pages but hid real degradation for ~18 minutes in one incident.
- Action: this doc enumerates short-term mitigations, an updated checklist for oncall, and medium-term telemetry/instrumentation fixes.

Timeline (sketch):
- 2026-01-13 03:42 UTC: first burst — 60 pings/min of "route_latency_high" from edge aggregator.
- 03:45 UTC: histogram ingestion lag increases; aggregated percentiles report stale values.
- 03:48 UTC: Dependents (cache layer) alert on derived latency increase -> cascade.
- 03:55 UTC: SRE oncall mutes noisy channel; restores synthetic traffic for health checks.
- 2026-01-15 11:12 UTC: second wave — missing measurements for token-kvcache_hits for 9 minutes.
- 2026-01-18 22:03 UTC: SLO burn alert generated due to double-counting synthetic bucket (bug in alert rule).

Detection notes / why we missed early signals:
- Alerts are high-sensitivity but low-specificity. Several rules fire on raw percentiles that become meaningless when histogram buckets reset.
- We lacked a short-lived diagnostic metric that distinguishes "ingestion-lag" from "real-latency".
- Silence windows (quieting of alerts) were applied manually rather than via orchestrated suppression with context.

Hypotheses (prioritized):
1) Ingestion/processing lag in the metrics pipeline caused stale histogram resets which made percentile-based alerts spike.
2) A configuration mismatch between aggregator and downstream query engine produced duplicate alerts for the same underlying event (de-dup mapping missing).
3) The SLO alert included synthetic traffic in two places due to a rule join that double-counted when the synthetic flag was null.

Quick mitigations (playbook entries to apply immediately during next wave):
- Emergency checklist for first responder (short):
  * Confirm whether ingestion-lag metrics (metric_ingest_latency_ms) are > 90th percentile for last 5m. If yes, prioritize pipeline status over app-level pages.
  * Switch problematic percentile-based rules to the degraded-rate rule: use derivative over 5m + ingestion-lag guard (see guard snippet below).
  * If duplicate alert sources: mute lower-fidelity channel (not the primary incident channel) and post in #sre-observability with short reason.
  * If SLO alert fires: check synthetic-tagged sum; if double-counting suspected, escalate to instrumentation lead and open SRE-3421.

- Guard snippet (pseudo-rule):
  "fire if (p95_latency_derivative_5m > X) AND (metric_ingest_latency_ms < Y)"
  Purpose: prevent percentiles that are spiking due to delayed histogram resets from firing.

Runbook additions (for reproduction and response):
- New diagnostic command (telemetry CLI): 'telemetry diagnose route <route> --time=5m' returns ingestion lag, bucket counts, and last-processed-offset. Add to runbook steps.
- Add a one-click dashboard tile for "metric_ingest_latency_ms" alongside each high-priority route dashboard.
- Add a policy: any SLO alert sourced from a derived rule must include synthetic traffic exclusion logic; if not present, fail the alert at PR review.

Investigation artifacts to produce (owners + ETA):
- Root cause audit of aggregator <-> query engine mappings (owner: Asha Kumar, ETA: 2026-02-05) -> verify de-dupe config and field mappings.
- Telemetry pipeline Kafka offset resume test (owner: Rafael Gomez, ETA: 2026-01-29) -> reproduce ingestion-lag with synthetic replay.
- SLO rule regression test harness to include synthetic flag permutations (owner: Sophie Li, ETA: 2026-02-12).

Medium-term fixes (next 3 sprints):
- Stronger alert quality gate in CI: require a historical false-positive estimate based on last 90 days for any new percentile alert.
- Add an ingestion-lag-aware transform in the pipeline to annotate metrics with 'ingestion_state: live|catchup' so rules can be gated.
- Implement dedupe service that maps canonical event ids across aggregator layers to prevent cross-channel duplication.

Experiment ideas (low cost):
- Canary a change where percentile alerting is suppressed for 2m if histogram bucket count < N. Track missed true-positive incidents via manual review.
- Run a 48-hour "synthetic-only" SLO fire window (pre-announced) to stress test alert rules and catch double-counting logic.

Communication templates (copy-paste friendly):
- Slack initial page response (example):
  "SRE: Acknowledged. Investigating high volume alerts for route <X>. First check: metric ingestion latency. Posting updates here. If you see suppression in the dashboard it is an active mitigation, not a service change."

Retrospective questions for the team:
- Are our percentile thresholds tuned to the right signal-primitives? Should we prefer derivative or rate-based triggers?
- How quickly can we add a short diagnostic metric to every route? Is this engineering or telemetry backlog?
- What escalation path removed human error from muting vs automated suppression?

Immediate action items (next 7 days):
- [Asha] run mapping audit and post summary in Confluence (link to be attached) - due 2026-02-05.
- [Rafael] synthetic replay of Kafka offsets + runbook for how to isolate ingestion-lag - due 2026-01-29.
- [Sophie] add synthetic exclusion test cases to SLO harness + add CI gate - due 2026-02-12.
- [Maya] prototype a dashboard tile for ingest-latency on two high-traffic routes - due 2026-01-22.

Notes to self / scratch ideas:

title: Pager surge prophylaxis notes
preview:
Context:
This is a working note collecting hypotheses, quick mitigations, and follow-ups from the recent week of pager surge events (week of 2026-01-12 -> 2026-01-19) where we saw three high-volume waves originating from degraded telemetry and one SLO burn alert that cascaded to noise. The goal: synthesize repeatable prophylactic steps that reduce noisy pages without eroding detection fidelity for real incidents.

Executive summary (short):
- Symptom cluster: rapid increase in low-fidelity alerts (thresholds firing repeatedly), followed by partial metric disappearance for dependent dashboards.
- Primary pain points: alert duplication across layers, missing histograms for token-latency, and an SLO rule that double-counted a synthetic bucket leading to a false SLO burn.
- Immediate outcome: on-call teams manually muted several routes and applied emergency fallbacks, which reduced pages but hid real degradation for ~18 minutes in one incident.
- Action: this doc enumerates short-term mitigations, an updated checklist for oncall, and medium-term telemetry/instrumentation fixes.

Timeline (sketch):
- 2026-01-13 03:42 UTC: first burst — 60 pings/min of \"route_latency_high\" from edge aggregator.
- 03:45 UTC: histogram ingestion lag increases; aggregated percentiles report stale values.
- 03:48 UTC: Dependents (cache layer) alert on derived latency increase -> cascade.
- 03:55 UTC: SRE oncall mutes noisy channel; restores synthetic traffic for health checks.
- 2026-01-15 11:12 UTC: second wave — missing measurements for token-kvcache_hits for 9 minutes.
- 2026-01-18 22:03 UTC: SLO burn alert generated due to double-counting synthetic bucket (bug in alert rule).

Detection notes / why we missed early signals:
- Alerts are high-sensitivity but low-specificity. Several rules fire on raw percentiles that become meaningless when histogram buckets reset.
- We lacked a short-lived diagnostic metric that distinguishes \"ingestion-lag\" from \"real-latency\".
- Silence windows (quieting of alerts) were applied manually rather than via orchestrated suppression with context.

Hypotheses (prioritized):
1) Ingestion/processing lag in the metrics pipeline caused stale histogram resets which made percentile-based alerts spike.
2) A configuration mismatch between aggregator and downstream query engine produced duplicate alerts for the same underlying event (de-dup mapping missing).
3) The SLO alert included synthetic traffic in two places due to a rule join that double-counted when the synthetic flag was null.

Quick mitigations (playbook entries to apply immediately during next wave):
- Emergency checklist for first responder (short):
  * Confirm whether ingestion-lag metrics (metric_ingest_latency_ms) are > 90th percentile for last 5m. If yes, prioritize pipeline status over app-level pages.
  * Switch problematic percentile-based rules to the degraded-rate rule: use derivative over 5m + ingestion-lag guard (see guard snippet below).
  * If duplicate alert sources: mute lower-fidelity channel (not the primary incident channel) and post in #sre-observability with short reason.
  * If SLO alert fires: check synthetic-tagged sum; if double-counting suspected, escalate to instrumentation lead and open SRE-3421.

- Guard snippet (pseudo-rule):
  \"fire if (p95_latency_derivative_5m > X) AND (metric_ingest_latency_ms < Y)\"
  Purpose: prevent percentiles that are spiking due to delayed histogram resets from firing.

Runbook additions (for reproduction and response):
- New diagnostic command (telemetry CLI): 'telemetry diagnose route <route> --time=5m' returns ingestion lag, bucket counts, and last-processed-offset. Add to runbook steps.
- Add a one-click dashboard tile for \"metric_ingest_latency_ms\" alongside each high-priority route dashboard.
- Add a policy: any SLO alert sourced from a derived rule must include synthetic traffic exclusion logic; if not present, fail the alert at PR review.

Investigation artifacts to produce (owners + ETA):
- Root cause audit of aggregator <-> query engine mappings (owner: Asha Kumar, ETA: 2026-02-05) -> verify de-dupe config and field mappings.
- Telemetry pipeline Kafka offset resume test (owner: Rafael Gomez, ETA: 2026-01-29) -> reproduce ingestion-lag with synthetic replay.
- SLO rule regression test harness to include synthetic flag permutations (owner: Sophie Li, ETA: 2026-02-12).

Medium-term fixes (next 3 sprints):
- Stronger alert quality gate in CI: require a historical false-positive estimate based on last 90 days for any new percentile alert.
- Add an ingestion-lag-aware transform in the pipeline to annotate metrics with 'ingestion_state: live|catchup' so rules can be gated.
- Implement dedupe service that maps canonical event ids across aggregator layers to prevent cross-channel duplication.

Experiment ideas (low cost):
- Canary a change where percentile alerting is suppressed for 2m if histogram bucket count < N. Track missed true-positive incidents via manual review.
- Run a 48-hour \"synthetic-only\" SLO fire window (pre-announced) to stress test alert rules and catch double-counting logic.

Communication templates (copy-paste friendly):
- Slack initial page response (example):
  \"SRE: Acknowledged. Investigating high volume alerts for route <X>. First check: metric ingestion latency. Posting updates here. If you see suppression in the dashboard it is an active mitigation, not a service change.\"

Retrospective questions for the team:
- Are our percentile thresholds tuned to the right signal-primitives? Should we prefer derivative or rate-based triggers?
- How quickly can we add a short diagnostic metric to every route? Is this engineering or telemetry backlog?
- What escalation path removed human error from muting vs automated suppression?

Immediate action items (next 7 days):
- [Asha] run mapping audit and post summary in Confluence (link to be attached) - due 2026-02-05.
- [Rafael] synthetic replay of Kafka offsets + runbook for how to isolate ingestion-lag - due 2026-01-29.
- [Sophie] add synthetic exclusion test cases to SLO harness + add CI gate - due 2026-02-12.
- [Maya] prototype a dashboard tile for ingest-latency on two high-traffic routes - due 2026-01-22.

Notes to self / scratch ideas:
- Consider a temporary rule that marks certain alert channels as \"supplemental\" and routes them to a buffer queue instead of immediate paging during known telemetry instability.
- Investigate if the RSA/worker process restarting correlates with our Kafka lag spikes (saw worker restarts in SRE logs around 2026-01-13 03:40).

Appendix: quick diagnostic checklist for oncall (compact)
1) Acknowledge page and post initial note to #sre-observability.
2) Run: telemetry diagnose route <route> --time=5m.
3) If metric_ingest_latency_ms > 2000ms (5m median): mark incident type = telemetry-catchup.
4) If telemetry-catchup: suppress percentile-based alerts for affected route for 10m; do not mute primary incident channel.
5) If SLO alert: check synthetic inclusion (query to compare synthetic_tag counts). If double-count suspected, escalate and tag SRE-3421.

Endnotes:
This doc is intentionally informal. Move items into formal playbooks once validated by experiments. Tag me (Ethan) on Slack for clarifications.

Related tickets and references: SRE-3421 (dup alerts mapping), OBS-118 (ingest latency investigation), PR#512 (telemetry: annotate metrics with ingestion_state).

Last updated by: Ethan Park on 2026-03-10

source.title: Booth Day Zero — Ops Cheatsheet
source.content:
Purpose: Day 0 rapid checklist and expense handoff for the NYC roadshow booth team. This is a lightweight, portable cheat sheet to keep on my phone and hand to the on-site lead. Not the formal runbook (see Confluence) — uses shorthand, quick contacts, and expense reminders.

Arrival + setup (high-priority)
- ETA to venue dock: 07:30 local. Aim to be at check-in desk by 07:45.
- Primary on-site lead: Maya (Maya cell: +1 646-555-0172) — meet at loading dock B.
- AV vendor pick-up window: 08:00–09:30. Contact: Nico Alvarez (BrightAV) — +1 917-555-3321, quote ref: BR-9812.
- Drayage & rigging: Event Logistics Co. on-site foreman: Karim — +1 646-555-1189. Confirm crate labels (A/B/C) and sign manifest.
- Power drops: verify 2x 20A circuits at booth grid north corner. If missing, escalate to venue ops and cc Karima (venue) + Ola.

Booth inventory quick-check (tick when done)
- 1x 10ft backwall (fabric) — unboxed at location C3
- 2x demo laptops (M-grade) + chargers — tag with asset stickers
- 1x standing monitor + VESA mount + HDMI (extra HDMI in AV kit)
- 1x ethernet switch & 1x travel router (fallback wifi)
- Misc: extension cords, gaffer tape, zip ties, printed name badges (stack A)

Expense reminders (reimbursement, don't forget)
- Overt receipts needed: courier/drayage invoices, AV day rental, booth install labor, hotel nights for core ops (Maya/Ethan), taxi/ride-share receipts.
- Snap photo of every paper receipt and upload to Ramp within 48h — tag with project code: ROADSHOW-NYC-26.
- Meal per diem: use per-diem policy (Confluence) — if team dinner > $100 check with finance before submitting.
- Cash petty: keep petty float $150 in envelope labeled ROADSHOW — receipts go in envelope and photo-upload daily.
- Expense notes to include: booth_id, date, vendor, brief purpose (e.g., "AV day rental for Day0 audio check").

Receipts filing shorthand (how I will do it)
- Folder on phone: "NYC-Roadshow-Receipts" (camera auto-upload to Drive on wifi).
- Ramp upload tags: [events, ops, roadshow, nyc] + approver: "Maya Singh".
- If billed by PO: forward vendor invoice to procurement@redwood with subject: PO-SUBMIT ROADSHOW-26.

Vendor quick rolodex (save to contacts)
- BrightAV (AV supplier) — Nico Alvarez — +1 917-555-3321 — nicob@brightav.com — BR-9812
- Event Logistics Co. (drayage) — Karim El-Sayed — +1 646-555-1189 — karim@evlogco.com — manifest ref: DRT-44NYC
- Courier (same-day) — CityExpress — dispatch +1 212-555-4410 — account manager: Priya Rao (priya@cityexpress.com)
- Catering (on-site snacks) — LocalBites — contact Sonya — +1 718-555-9023 — try to cap at $120/day.

Comms templates (copy-paste)
- To AV on arrival: "Hi Nico, we are at dock B with crate A. Can you confirm ETA for your tech and that you have HDMI and sound bar in kit BR-9812? Contact: Ethan 646-555-0109."
- To Drayage: "Karim — signing manifest now. Please hold crate C until lead confirms. Send final invoice to procurement@redwood with manifest ID DRT-44NYC."

On-call escalation (who to ping)
- Venue power/network issues: Ola Johansson (ops) — +1 415-555-6721 — slack: @ola (primary)
- Budget/expense exceptions (> $500 unplanned): Finance on-call — Priya (finance) — priya.n@redwood.com (cc: events@redwood)
- Legal/sponsor contracts questions: Raj Patel (legal) — slack: @rajp

Post-day handoff (within 24h)
- Collect all paper receipts and crate manifest; take final booth photos (3 angles); do a quick inventory.
- Create a single zip: photos + receipts + vendor invoices + final checklist. Upload to Drive folder: /shared_drives/company/events/roadshow/nyc/2026-03.
- Slack-thread summary in #events-roadshow with final costs and any vendor follow-ups (tag finance).

Open questions / to resolve before Day 0
- Confirm whether we have a backup SIM card for demo laptops (need approval to buy if not).
- Is there an on-site lockbox for overnight security? Venue contact said yes but need code process.
- Confirm who will handle tax-exempt paperwork for local catering invoice.

Notes to self:
- Keep calm. If something breaks, photo it, get vendor quote in writing, and prioritize customer-visible fixes (demo laptops, monitor) over cosmetic items.
- If you hand cash to vendor, get a signed receipt with vendor business name and phone.

Quick checklist (tick in person)
- [ ] Meet Maya at loading dock B at 07:45
- [ ] Sign drayage manifest (Karim)
- [ ] Confirm AV kit BR-9812 delivered and tested
- [ ] Tag receipts and upload to Ramp
- [ ] Take final booth photos + upload

End — keep this on phone and hand copy to Maya on arrival.

- Create a single zip: photos + receipts + vendor invoices + final checklist. Upload to Drive folder: /shared_drives/company/events/roadshow/nyc/2026-03.
- Slack-thread summary in #events-roadshow with final costs and any vendor follow-ups (tag finance).

Open questions / to resolve before Day 0
- Confirm whether we have a backup SIM card for demo laptops (need approval to buy if not).
- Is there an on-site lockbox for overnight security? Venue contact said yes but need code process.
- Confirm who will handle tax-exempt paperwork for local catering invoice.

Notes to self:
- Keep calm. If something breaks, photo it, get vendor quote in writing, and prioritize customer-visible fixes (demo laptops, monitor) over cosmetic items.
- If you hand cash to vendor, get a signed receipt with vendor business name and phone.

Quick checklist (tick in person)
- [ ] Meet Maya at loading dock B at 07:45
- [ ] Sign drayage manifest (Karim)
- [ ] Confirm AV kit BR-9812 delivered and tested
- [ ] Tag receipts and upload to Ramp
- [ ] Take final booth photos + upload

End — keep this on phone and hand copy to Maya on arrival.

source.title: Private Deploy: GitOps, Terraform & Image Signing — Draft
source.content:
Purpose:
This is a working draft playbook and notes collection for CI/CD and release mechanics specific to Redwood Private deployments (VPC and on-prem). Focus: Terraform modules, Argo/GitOps patterns, Helm upgrade strategies, and an image signing + verification workflow that fits air-gapped/regulated environments.

Audience:
- Eng: platform, private-deploy, SRE
- Solutions eng / customer success for runbook handoff

Goals:
- Define a reproducible pipeline for building, signing, promoting, and deploying images to private clusters (harbor/airgapped registries).
- Capture Terraform module patterns for network, cluster, and registry resources used by private customers.
- Provide safe upgrade mechanics (canary/blue-green/rolling) that work with ArgoCD and Helm without requiring cloud-managed services.
- Ensure verification (cosign) and admission enforcement are practical in air-gapped environments.

Non-goals:
- Full CI templates per provider (we provide patterns + snippets).
- Complete SLSA attestation coverage (we note required parts).

High-level flow (recommended):
1) Build: CI builds image and SBOM, produces build metadata.
2) Sign: cosign signs image + attestation; signatures pushed to accessible store (OCI registry or detached sigs copied to artifact store for air-gapped).
3) Push: image + SBOM + signature promoted to private registry (harbor/minio-backed gateway).
4) Promote: tag promotion (build -> staging -> release), update GitOps manifests in a promotion branch/tags.
5) Deploy: ArgoCD (app-of-apps) syncs from promoted tag; admission webhook verifies signature before admit.
6) Validate: automated smoke tests run in cluster; observability checks for latency/cost regressions.
7) Rollout complete / rollback if necessary.

Terraform module notes (pattern + constraints):
- Modules to maintain: network/vpc, cluster/k8s (eks/gke/k0s/private k8s), registry (harbor/registry-proxy), storage (minio/backed), dns, bastion/jump.
- State backend: prefer S3-compatible (minio) for on-prem; require locking (consul or dynamo-like lock shim) to prevent concurrent terraform apply.
- Workspaces: use per-environment workspaces (customer-name-prod, -staging) to isolate state. 
- Outputs: standardize outputs for Argo app manifests: cluster_kubeconfig, registry_endpoint, image_pull_secrets_ref, ingress_cert_name.
- Secrets: do NOT put long-lived secrets in TF state; integrate with Vault/KMS/HSM for keys (image signing keys, DB creds).
- Module versioning: tag modules semver; consumers pin to an exact tag or commit for production.
- Upgrade hooks: modules should export a "pre_apply_check" and "post_apply_migration" guidance fields (docs) that operators can run.
- Recommended practice: terraform plan -> upload plan artifact to artifacts store -> require human approval (or automated policy) for apply in prod.

GitOps / Argo patterns:
- App-of-apps: single root app per customer pointing to per-environment repo/folder. Keeps Argo scope small.
- Branching: use promotion branches: refs: refs/heads/promotion/staging -> release. Argo tracked by tag or commit SHA to avoid drift.
- Self-heal: enable automated self-heal for known safe resources, but require manual sync for CRDs and cluster-scoped changes.
- Argo Rollouts: use when you need canary + analysis, especially for model-serving pods where traffic shaping matters. Keep a fallback route to previous version.
- Sync windows: use Argo sync windows for maintenance/outage windows. Document these in customer runbooks.
- Image updater: either use automated image-updater with signed image verification or a promotion job that writes the new image tag to manifests in a PR that triggers review.

Helm upgrade strategies (practical advice):
- Use helmfile/helm3 for templating; keep charts in a chartmuseum or registry.
- Pre-upgrade: run "helm diff" or "helm upgrade --dry-run" to spot resource changes and deletions.
- Hooks: avoid heavy reliance on hooks for critical DB migrations; instead create explicit migration jobs that are triggered and observed by pipeline.
- Rollback: use "helm rollback" for simple failures, but for data migrations follow the migration runbook (reverse migration script).
- StatefulSets: scale down replicas before schema changes where necessary, then scale up.
- Chart values: maintain an explicit "upgradePolicy" block (e.g., maxSurge, maxUnavailable, healthChecks) to make rollouts consistent across deployments.

Image signing & verification (bottom line):
- Tooling: cosign (recommended), notary2 optional. Support detached signatures for air-gapped scenarios.
- Keys: prefer KMS/HSM-managed keys. For customers that require local keys, document key provisioning + rotation process.
- Attestations: generate SBOM (syft) + build provenance (in-toto) and attach as ORAS/OCI attestation.
- Admission: deploy an admission webhook (cosign-webhook or custom) to verify signatures and accept only signed images for protected namespaces.
- Air-gapped flow: push image + signature + attestation to a secure artifact bundle (tar) and transfer via secure channel to customer registry; webhook verification should be able to read a local signature store.

CI pipeline snippets (examples):
- Build step (GitHub Actions style):
  - docker build -t ${REGISTRY}/${REPO}:${SHA} .
  - syft -o json ${REGISTRY}/${REPO}:${SHA} > sbom.json
  - cosign sign --key $COSIGN_KEY ${REGISTRY}/${REPO}:${SHA}
  - cosign verify --key $PUB_KEY ${REGISTRY}/${REPO}:${SHA}  # sanity check
  - oras push ${REGISTRY}/${REPO}:${SHA} sbom.json --attestation att.json

- Terraform pipeline:
  - terraform fmt && terraform init -backend-config=...
  - terraform validate && terraform plan -out=tfplan
  - upload tfplan artifact, require "apply" approval for prod
  - terraform apply tfplan

- Promotion job (example):
  - create PR to gitops repo that updates images.yaml or kustomize/values.yaml
  - CI runs helm diff and auto-approves if diff within allowed list (configurable)

Preflight checks (automatable):
- Cluster capacity check (CPU/GPU/Memory) for target zone
- Node taints/tolerations compatibility for workloads (GPU workloads use nodeSelector/gpu=true)
- Storage class sanity (iops/throughput), PVC quotas
- Registry connectivity and auth (pull test)
- Admission/webhook connectivity and signature verification test

Rollback & recovery playbook (summary steps):
1) Detect: alerts from smoke tests or ARGO/CI failures.
2) Halt promotion: block next release tag.
3) Immediate action: Argo rollback to previous healthy commit or helm rollback (note: Argo rollback requires updating app to previous commit).
4) If DB migration involved: follow DB rollback script (pre-built reversible migration preferred).
5) Postmortem: capture metrics, create CHART to track root cause and follow-up.

Upgrade plan (example timeline for a major runtime/serving change):
- Week -4: announce window, freeze non-critical changes in charts/infra
- Week -3: run compatibility tests in staging clusters (model and runtime tests)
- Week -2: run shadow traffic in staging via Argo Rollouts analysis
- Week -1: dry-run in canary customer cluster (opt-in)
- Day 0: maintenance window, apply infra changes (TF) early morning, then promote images and sync via Argo
- Day 0+1: monitor 72h, have rollback window for 48h with automated rollback triggers on critical signals

Runbook snippets (commands):
- Check Argo app health: argocd app get <app>
- Force sync: argocd app sync <app> --revision <sha>
- Helm dry-run: helm upgrade --install <rel> ./chart -f values.yaml --dry-run --debug
- Verify cosign: cosign verify --key $PUB_KEY ${REGISTRY}/${REPO}:${TAG}
- Apply Terraform plan: terraform apply tfplan  # ensure the plan artifact is used

Docs & handoff checklist for customer-success/solutions-eng:
- Provide kubeconfig + instructions for Argo root app access
- Provide key rotation schedule & instructions (if local keys used)
- Provide maintenance windows and expected outage durations
- Provide runbook link and emergency rollback contacts

Open questions / TODOs:
- Image signature storage for air-gapped customers: prefer detached sig + artifact bundle; need operations SOP for secure transfer (TODO: define transport + verify checksums).
- Admission webhook offline mode: how to configure cosign verification lookup against local store reliably? (Investigate cosign "public key file" mode and secure caching).
- Terraform locking for pure-onprem customers without consul/dynamo: build a small lock-service (lightweight postgres-backed lock) or recommend manual lock step.
- Define exact RBAC boundaries for Argo App-of-Apps root app vs child apps to minimize blast radius.
- Decide on requirement baseline: do we require SBOM + sign for all images or only for privileged namespaces?

Next steps (near-term):
- Convert this doc to a Confluence page and link TF module examples (Ethan + Priya).
- Prototype cosign detached signature flow for air-gapped copy (Noah).
- Add terraform plan artifact and approval gating into the private-deploy pipeline (Ava).
- Schedule a runbook drill with SRE and Solutions Eng to rehearse rollback (T+3 weeks).

Notes-to-self / rough ideas:
- Consider shipping a small "signing-agent" container for customer clusters that can hold a rotated key in HSM and perform local verification for speed.
- For customers that demand hardware trust, map cosign keys -> HSM slot and document key import/export process.

End of draft.

source.title: shortburst-prefetch-eval-scratch
source.content:
Purpose:
Quick personal log for the short-burst prefetching experiments. Trying to validate a small client+server prefetch pattern that primes KV cache / tokenized prefix for very short requests (<= 48 tokens) and measure tail improvement vs cost. This is exploratory — notes to self and Mira/Arjun.

Hypothesis:
- Many production requests are short (single-turn completions, search snippets). These suffer disproportionately from cold KV cache and startup kernel overhead.
- If the client sends a tiny proactive prefetch probe (zero-output, low-cost context) 15–50ms before the actual request, server can prime attention KV cache and warm short-path kernels. For mixed traffic the cost can be amortized and reduce p95/p99 for short requests.

Testbed / infra:
- Fleet: 4x A10g nodes, runtime image: rwinf/runtime:v2.1.3-bench
- Model: rw-v-small-1.3B (FP16 quant path), typical seqlen 32, context window 1024
- Load generator: custom python harness (~/bench/shortburst_loader.py) using asyncio, simulates arrival traces
- Baseline config: batching disabled for small requests (max_batch=1, batching_window=3ms), async-preempt disabled
- Prefetch config: client issues a prefetch call of special type "prefetch_ping" (no tokens decoded returned) T_prefetch=30ms before request; server handles prefetch by loading KV and marking session as warm for 120s TTL.

Workloads run:
- W1: 500rps sustained, 80% short (16 tokens), 20% medium (128 tokens); Poisson arrivals
- W2: 200rps with microbursts: 10x bursts of 200 requests over 200ms every 60s
- W3: mixed long-run: 50rps long (512 tokens) + 300rps short background

Metrics collected:
- p50/p95/p99 latency for token-first-byte and request-complete (ms)
- GPU utilization, kernel-switch count (proxy logs), KV cache hit rate per session
- Extra cost: additional prefetch QPS and total decoded tokens

Command lines / harness notes:
- Load script: python shortburst_loader.py --workload W1 --duration 600 --prefetch 30
- Perf collector: bench/collect_top.py (samples every 2s), plus GPU metrics from prom: node_gpu.*
- PR for server patch that honors prefetch: runtime/pull/812

Raw observations (W1, 600s, averaged over 3 runs):
- Baseline (no-prefetch):
  - request-complete: p50=34ms, p95=112ms, p99=310ms
  - token-first-byte: p50=12ms, p95=60ms, p99=220ms
  - KV session hit-rate: 14%
  - GPU util: 38% (saw micro-scheduling gaps when small requests arrived)
- Prefetch (client prefetch 30ms):
  - request-complete: p50=36ms, p95=78ms, p99=140ms
  - token-first-byte: p50=14ms, p95=32ms, p99=70ms
  - KV session hit-rate: 62% (session TTL 120s)
  - GPU util: 43% (more consistent steady utilization)
  - Prefetch QPS overhead: +48rps (prefetch executed only for short requests; cost approx 0.16 tokens/prefetch in server accounting due to internal no-op decode path)

Interpretation W1:
- Prefetch improves tails dramatically (p99 ~ halved) at cost of small extra QPS. p95 also improved by ~30–40%. p50 slightly worse or flat (likely introduced 15–30ms client spacing before actual request). For latency-sensitive endpoints that pay for p99 this looks promising.

W2 (microbursts) summary:
- Baseline: p99 spikes >1.2s during bursts (due to scheduling jitter + cold KV misses)
- Prefetch: p99 spikes reduced to 260–420ms depending on burst timing; if prefetches are sent within 100ms before burst they help more. If burst arrives without prefetch then fallback behavior unchanged.
- Noticed an interaction: prefetch flood during a burst can cause short-lived queueing if prefetches are naive; need rate-limit and coalesce prefetches per-session.

Implementation notes / gotchas:
1) Client timing discipline: prefetch needs to be issued with consistent lead time (empirically 20–40ms). If too early (>500ms) session TTL helps but cache may be evicted. If too late (<5ms) warmup not effective.
2) Server must treat prefetch as low-priority but fast-path for KV warming. It should avoid decoding tokens to save cycles; we implemented a "warm-only" path that loads KV and executes the first-attention-kernel microstep to prime caches.
3) Coalescing: when many prefetches for same session arrive within 10ms, coalesce into one internal prefetch operation.
4) Cost accounting: prefetch should be reflected in metrics differently (charged as microcold-warm ops) so billing/telemetry teams can evaluate cost-benefit.
5) Security: prefetch must not reveal model output; return 204 / no-body and mark session id.

Quantitative summary (delta vs baseline for short requests):
- p95: -34% (112 -> 78 ms)
- p99: -55% (310 -> 140 ms)
- KV-hit: +48ppt (14% -> 62%)
- Extra QPS: +9.6% (48rps on top of 500rps workload)

Quick conclusions:
- Prefetching appears to be an effective lever for improving tails on short-request-heavy workloads with modest cost.
- Microburst safety requires coalescing and soft-rate limiting for prefetch traffic; otherwise prefetch can amplify bursts.
- For endpoints where p99 matters (chat heads, conversation startup), exposing a small client-side prefetch SDK helper + server warm-only path could be a good product toggle.

Next steps / experiments:
- Run with smaller lead times (10ms) and adaptively tune prefetch lead using RTT sampling (client measures RTT and sets prefetch lead accordingly).
- Test on larger model family (rw-v-medium-8B) to confirm behavior with longer kernel paths and heavier KV sizes.
- Add synthetic attacker scenario: many clients requesting prefetch without follow-ups — measure worst-case resource waste and design quota rules.
- Evaluate cost model with billing: what does 9–15% QPS overhead translate to $/month for typical tenant? Draft a short brief for pricing team.

Notes to self:
- Save the exact experiment traces under bench_runs/shortprefetch/W1-run1.tar.gz (telnet to bench machine, archive logs).
- Follow up with PR comments on runtime/pull/812 to add coalescing and per-session prefetch TTL config.

End of scratch log.

source.title: Release rollback debug scratchpad
source.content:
Context / quick summary:
- Investigating last-night canary failure for release v2026.02.26.3 (api-serving group). Canary latency spiked -> 502s errors from nginx sidecar -> downstream model process OOM-killed on node.
- Quick rollback triggered to v2026.02.19.1 via automated pipeline (circleci -> k8s rollingRestart) but observed partial traffic split mismatch in traffic-router causing some nodes still hit canary image. Need deterministic rollback playbook and commands I used.

Commands tried (timestamps local):
- Confirmed images: docker images in registry
  - curl -sS -H "Accept: application/vnd.docker.distribution.manifest.v2+json" -u token: https://registry.internal.redwood/v2/api-serving/manifests/v2026.02.26.3 | jq -r .config.digest
- k8s review (checked pods & events):
  - kubectl -n api-serving get pods -o wide --selector=app=api-service
  - kubectl -n api-serving describe rs api-service-26 (saw image v2026.02.26.3 on 3/6 pods)
  - kubectl -n api-serving get events --field-selector involvedObject.name=api-service-26 | tail -n 50
- Found OOMKill in logs of model-worker container:
  - kubectl -n api-serving logs api-service-26-7f9bz -c model-worker --since=2h | grep -i oom
    -> "Memory cgroup out of memory: Kill process 21342 (model-worker) score 187"
- Pipeline rollback attempt (automated):
  - circleci trigger: curl -X POST https://circleci/api/v2/project/.../pipeline -d '{"branch":"main","params":{"rollback_to":"v2026.02.19.1"}}'
  - Observed git tag update and helm release patch: helm upgrade --install api-service ./charts/api --set image.tag=v2026.02.19.1
- Post-rollback verification steps I ran:
  - kubectl -n api-serving get pods -l release=api-service -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name}{"\t"}{.status.containerStatuses[0].image}{"\n"}{end}'
  - curl -v -H "X-Canary: 1" https://staging-api.redwood/v1/health (sometimes returned 502 intermittently)

Error snippets / oddities observed:
- Traffic-router still sending ~10% of requests to pods with image v2026.02.26.3 even after helm upgrade. Router config looked synced in S3, but node-level config cache persisted.
- Skaffold/wiring: helm release history showed 3 rollouts within 20m but envoy config push lagged.
- CircleCI job logs show successful deploy step but rotation-hook reported non-zero exit code (exit 1) without stacktrace.
- Logs: "failed to apply config: version mismatch (local=1251 remote=1249)" -> suggests concurrent config writes during rollback.

Hypotheses / quick theory list:
1) The canary image pushes a slightly different config schema; during canary the config schema migrated and router caches now incompatible, causing partial fallback and 502s.
2) Autoscaler increased pod count quickly and new nodes scheduled with older kubelet or stale image pull policy resulted in mix of images.
3) The rollback procedure is racing with the config propagation step (traffic-router / envoy), leaving a window where traffic rules point to the canary pods.
4) Artifact immutability not enforced (tags reused), so image digest mismatch could cause registry to return older layers unexpectedly.

Local reproductions I tried:
- Force re-pull on nodes: kubectl -n kube-system cordon/evict patterns (drain & restart) -> expensive, did on 2/12 nodes as a test, saw traffic move to healthy pods.
- Forced router reload: s3 sync + POST /reload to router admin -> seemed to clear cached route on router-3 but router-1 still had stale routes (investigate leader election).
- Simulated traffic via hey/vegeta with header X-Canary:1 and measured 502 ratio before/after reload.

Quick manual checklist (for future incident runbook):
- [ ] Verify artifact immutability: registry digest vs tag (reject if tag points to different digest than release manifest).
- [ ] Pause automated canary traffic (traffic-router) before rollout if config schema migration included.
- [ ] Ensure router config versioning: add explicit barrier/lock during rolling updates (etcd lock or central lockfile in S3).
- [ ] Node image pull policy: set to IfNotPresent+digest pinning in helm values for all prod charts.
- [ ] Add a probe that detects mixed-image deployments (script: query pods image tag list -> fail pipeline if >1 distinct tag).

Short snippets to include in consolidated playbook (copy-paste):
- Check registry digest vs helm values in CI: 
  DIGEST=$(curl -sS -H "Accept: application/vnd.docker.distribution.manifest.v2+json" -u $REG_TOKEN https://registry.internal/v2/api-serving/manifests/$TAG | jq -r .config.digest)
  helm template ./charts/api --set image.tag=$TAG | grep -q $DIGEST || echo "digest mismatch" && exit 1

- Safe rollback sequence (manual):
  1) Pause traffic-router canary splits: curl -X POST $ROUTER/admin/pause_canary
  2) Update helm image tag -> helm upgrade --install api-service ./charts/api --set image.tag=$ROLLBACK_TAG --wait --timeout 10m
  3) Wait for all pods Ready & image digest matches: kubectl -n api-serving get pods -o jsonpath='{.items[*].status.containerStatuses[*].imageID}' | tr ' ' '\n'
  4) Force router reload and verify route table shows new image digests: curl $ROUTER/admin/reload && curl $ROUTER/admin/routes | jq .
  5) Unpause canary gradually if metrics stable.

Open questions / TODOs for follow-up:
- Is registry enforcing immutability for release tags? (ask infra/registry team)
- Add a pre-deploy check in CircleCI that fails build if helm values.tag != registry digest mapping (see snippet above).
- Determine why router leader has stale cache after reload (check leader-election logs, consul/etcd connectivity).
- Add a canary-safety gate: block canary for 30m after schema-changing commits.

Notes to self (less formal):
- Talk to Marco about making the traffic-router reload idempotent and exposing /status that shows route->image mapping.
- Jillian will own updating the CircleCI pipeline to include digest checks; ping her in the morning.
- Consider an emergency script: emergency-rollback.sh that does the 5-step sequence and logs everything to S3 with timestamps.

Last-ditch quick commands I kept in terminal history (for copying back into runbook):
- kubectl -n api-serving get pods -o wide | grep v2026.02.26.3
- kubectl -n api-serving delete pod api-service-26-7f9bz --grace-period=0 --force  # test eviction of bad pod
- helm history api-service --max=10

If I forget: primary ticket JIRA-RELEASE-4123, slack thread #release-ops (pinned message), infra on-call: Marco.

End scratchpad.

  1) Pause traffic-router canary splits: curl -X POST $ROUTER/admin/pause_canary
  2) Update helm image tag -> helm upgrade --install api-service ./charts/api --set image.tag=$ROLLBACK_TAG --wait --timeout 10m
  3) Wait for all pods Ready & image digest matches: kubectl -n api-serving get pods -o jsonpath='{.items[*].status.containerStatuses[*].imageID}' | tr ' ' '
'
  4) Force router reload and verify route table shows new image digests: curl $ROUTER/admin/reload && curl $ROUTER/admin/routes | jq .
  5) Unpause canary gradually if metrics stable.

Open questions / TODOs for follow-up:
- Is registry enforcing immutability for release tags? (ask infra/registry team)
- Add a pre-deploy check in CircleCI that fails build if helm values.tag != registry digest mapping (see snippet above).
- Determine why router leader has stale cache after reload (check leader-election logs, consul/etcd connectivity).
- Add a canary-safety gate: block canary for 30m after schema-changing commits.

Notes to self (less formal):
- Talk to Marco about making the traffic-router reload idempotent and exposing /status that shows route->image mapping.
- Jillian will own updating the CircleCI pipeline to include digest checks; ping her in the morning.
- Consider an emergency script: emergency-rollback.sh that does the 5-step sequence and logs everything to S3 with timestamps.

Last-ditch quick commands I kept in terminal history (for copying back into runbook):
- kubectl -n api-serving get pods -o wide | grep v2026.02.26.3
- kubectl -n api-serving delete pod api-service-26-7f9bz --grace-period=0 --force  # test eviction of bad pod
- helm history api-service --max=10

If I forget: primary ticket JIRA-RELEASE-4123, slack thread #release-ops (pinned message), infra on-call: Marco.

End scratchpad.

- Quick rollback triggered to v2026.02.19.1 via automated pipeline (circleci -> k8s rollingRestart) but observed partial traffic split mismatch in traffic-router causing some nodes still hit canary image. Need deterministic rollback playbook and commands I used.

Commands tried (timestamps local):
- Confirmed images: docker images in registry
  - curl -sS -H "Accept: application/vnd.docker.distribution.manifest.v2+json" -u token: https://registry.internal.redwood/v2/api-serving/manifests/v2026.02.26.3 | jq -r .config.digest

"}{end}'

source.title: Handshake Latency & Slab Scheduling Probe
source.content:
Purpose:
Investigate a recurring tail-latency pattern we observed in mixed small/medium token workloads on 4xA100 nodes: periodic p99 spikes coincident with short NVLink bursts and NCCL handshake retries. Hypothesis: host-to-device handshake timing + slab-style host staging (our host staging queueing) interacts poorly with NCCL collective startup, producing microbursts that saturate interconnect and induce backpressure into the runtime scheduler. Goal is to (a) reproduce deterministically, (b) identify contributing knobs (NCCL params, host staging order, prefetch window), and (c) propose a mitigator that can be implemented in the serving runtime with minimal perf regression on steady-state throughput.

Quick summary of findings (TL;DR):
- We can reproduce the p99 blips by driving a mix of short (<=16 tokens) and medium (128 tokens) requests with varying inter-arrival jitter.
- Root cause appears to be misaligned handshake bursts: several shards begin NCCL collective init within a few ms window, producing short NVLink saturation and triggering tail-latency across replicas.
- Increasing prefetch window reduces tail spikes but raises p50 in some configs.
- A cheap runtime mitigation (staggered host-stage offsets + handshake coalescing) reduces p99 by ~40% with <3% throughput impact on steady-state.

Hardware & baseline topology used:
- Node: 4x NVIDIA A100 (40GB), NVLink fully connected mesh, PCIe x16 to host.
- interconnect: nvlink peer -> within-node, 100GbE between nodes (not used for this test).
- OS: Ubuntu 20.04, CUDA 11.8, NCCL 2.14.6, driver 525.xx.
- Runtime: latest canary branch (commit sha: b3f9c2) with default host-staging and prefetch=2.

Traffic pattern & test harness:
- Synthetic client harness (python asyncio) that issues requests with a 70/30 split (short/medium).
- Arrival pattern: Poisson with configurable jitter. We tried jitter sigma 0.5ms -> 10ms.
- Payloads: GPT-style causal model with batch size 1 for request-level latency realism.
- Metrics captured: per-request latency (p50/p90/p95/p99), per-GPU SM utilization, NVLink bytes/sec (nvlink_top), NCCL traces (NCCL_LOG=INFO), kernel timelines via nsys (15s windows).

Instrumentation notes (how we measured):
- nsight-systems (nsys) to get kernel/CU timelines, host->device copies, and inter-GPU comm landmarks.
- nvlink_top (internal wrapper) polled at 100ms intervals to show short bursts; useful for correlating spikes with p99 events.
- NCCL logs collected with timestamps, plus enabling NCCL_COLLNET=0, NCCL_DEBUG=INFO in some runs.
- tcpdump on host to ensure no cross-node interference for intra-node-only runs.
- host CPU perf sampling to catch dispatch stalls (perf record -F 99).

Experiment matrix (key knobs):
- NCCL_ALGO: RING vs TREE
- NCCL_P2P_LEVEL: NVL vs SYS
- Host-staging queue depth: 1, 4, 16
- Prefetch window (our runtime): 1, 2, 4, 8 tokens
- Staggered offset: 0ms, 2ms, 5ms, 10ms between shard start times
- Kernel-level pacing: enabled/disabled (noop shim in runtime to insert nanosleep)

Representative run (baseline):
- NCCL_ALGO=RING, NCCL_P2P_LEVEL=NVL, host-staging=4, prefetch=2, offset=0ms
- Results (30m run): p50=32ms, p95=76ms, p99=248ms, throughput=~620 tok/s per node
- NVLink trace: bursts of 12–18GB/s for ~6–12ms aligned with p99 events

Key observations with short analysis:
1) Handshake clustering:
   - NCCL collective init log lines clustered within 1–4ms windows across GPUs when offset=0. This aligns with the NVLink bursts.
   - When staggers are >2ms, the clustering disperses and NVLink burst amplitude reduces 35–50%.
   - Interpretation: many shards try to start comms nearly simultaneously causing transient interconnect saturation.

2) Prefetch trade-off:
   - Increasing prefetch window from 2->8 reduces the frequency of p99 spikes (fewer collective inits because more tokens served from local kv-cache), but increases p50 by ~6–8% for medium requests (more memory pressure and slightly longer per-request handling).
   - Suggests a dynamic policy: raise prefetch for short-heavy traffic but avoid across-the-board increases.

3) NCCL algorithm sensitivity:
   - TREE algo marginally better for p99 in fully-local NVLink configs, but worse throughput for large-batch synthetic tests. RING produced more consistent throughput but higher tail without staggering.
   - NCCL_P2P_LEVEL=NVL is critical for intra-node performance; forcing SYS drops perf and increases CPU involvement.

4) Host-staging queue depth:
   - Very deep staging (16) amplifies clustering because more requests are queued and then dispatched in bursts; shallow queue depth (1) reduces clustering but increases overhead and jitter. Depth=4 with staged offsets gives best trade-off.

5) Pacing + coalescing:
   - A runtime shim that coalesces handshake windows and artificially spaces start times (staggered offsets randomized in a small window) reduced p99 by ~40% without adding much overhead.
   - Microbenchmark: staggered offsets uniformly random in [0,5ms] reduced NVLink peak by ~45% and p99 from 248ms->150ms.

Anomalies & open questions:
- On one rack (mixed PCIe gen differences), staggering increased p50 unexpectedly. Hypothesis: in heterogeneous topologies, stagger may increase pipeline imbalance. Need targeted repro on heterogeneous node mixes.
- Occasional NCCL retry log entries that correlate with a host PCIe interrupt storm; not yet reproduced reliably.
- Interplay with runtime's batching heuristic: when batching is enabled, the batching coalescer sometimes defeats our stagger (they are both trying to batch request starts). Need a harmonized approach.

Suggested mitigations (short-term -> medium-term):
- Short-term (runtime change): implement handshake coalescer + staggerer: when multiple shards will invoke NCCL within X ms, spread them within a configurable window (default 5ms). Add metric to surface "handshake density" in Console.
- Medium-term: adaptive prefetch policy driven by traffic mix classifier: if short-heavy, automatically increase prefetch and enable staggerer; if medium/long-heavy, revert.
- NCCL tuning: set NCCL_ALGO heuristics per-model/sequence-length profiles (we can store model-specific recommended NCCL envs in model catalog).
- Long-term: kernel-level backpressure indicators (from nvlink or device perf) exposed to runtime for closed-loop pacing.

Action items / TODOs:
- INF-1423: implement handshake coalescer prototype in runtime (owner: Ethan) -> branch created, PR referenced in linked artifacts.
- Run repro on heterogeneous node mixes and 8x GPU trays (owner: Maya) by 2026-03-20.
- Add a telemetry metric to Console: "handshake_density_per_minute" and NVLink_peak_5s (owner: Sofia).
- Evaluate interaction with batching; two approaches: (A) let batching drive stagger decisions, (B) handshake coalescer sits above batching and coordinates. Implement tests for both.

Notes to self / rough ideas:
- Could try piggybacking handshake on the first host staging copy so collectives start only when the last copy arrives -> basically make collectives waiter-driven (might increase tail for the earliest shards).
- Explore if NCCL_TUNE environment knobs exist to smooth collective startup.
- Try disabling cudaLaunchBlocking in debug to see if kernel queuing jitter is a factor.

Appendix: raw snippets (selected)
- Example NCCL clustered init excerpt:
  [gpu0] ncclCommInitRank() start ts=1678932.123456
  [gpu1] ncclCommInitRank() start ts=1678932.125112
  [gpu2] ncclCommInitRank() start ts=1678932.126998
  [gpu3] ncclCommInitRank() start ts=1678932.130004
- NVLink peak sampled around same window: nvlink_top: 14.2GB/s (gpu0->gpu1) at ts=1678932.127

References / links:
- INF-1423 (Jira): prototype runtime coalescing work
- PR: runtime/pull/678 contains the first staggerer prototype (instrumentation-only, off by default)

If you want to reproduce locally: steps:
1) Checkout canary b3f9c2, build runtime with DEBUG_TRACING=1
2) Set up 4x A100 node (single host) with NCCL_DEBUG=INFO
3) Run harness: tools/perf/synthetic_mixed_short_medium.py --runtime-host localhost --duration 1800 --jitter 2 --short_frac 0.7
4) Collect nsys and nvlink_top logs in /tmp/handshake_probe/*

Questions for reviewers:
- Acceptable default stagger window? 5ms seems low-risk but we should validate on all rack types.
- Do we prefer a model-catalog driven static NCCL envs or a runtime auto-tuner?
- Is adding telemetry for handshake_density OK for privacy/metrics budget?

End notes: this feels actionable and low-risk to prototype. The ephemeral NVLink bursts are the most plausible single-point cause of the p99s we've been chasing. If reviewers agree, I'll move the prototype out of branch and open a small rollout on a canary pool; measure p99 reductions over one week.

-- Ethan

- Host-staging queue depth: 1, 4, 16
- Prefetch window (our runtime): 1, 2, 4, 8 tokens
- Staggered offset: 0ms, 2ms, 5ms, 10ms between shard start times
- Kernel-level pacing: enabled/disabled (noop shim in runtime to insert nanosleep)

Representative run (baseline):
- NCCL_ALGO=RING, NCCL_P2P_LEVEL=NVL, host-staging=4, prefetch=2, offset=0ms
- Results (30m run): p50=32ms, p95=76ms, p99=248ms, throughput=~620 tok/s per node
- NVLink trace: bursts of 12–18GB/s for ~6–12ms aligned with p99 events

Key observations with short analysis:
1) Handshake clustering:
   - NCCL collective init log lines clustered within 1–4ms windows across GPUs when offset=0. This aligns with the NVLink bursts.
   - When staggers are >2ms, the clustering disperses and NVLink burst amplitude reduces 35–50%.
   - Interpretation: many shards try to start comms nearly simultaneously causing transient interconnect saturation.

2) Prefetch trade-off:
   - Increasing prefetch window from 2->8 reduces the frequency of p99 spikes (fewer collective inits because more tokens served from local kv-cache), but increases p50 by ~6–8% for medium requests (more memory pressure and slightly longer per-request handling).
   - Suggests a dynamic policy: raise prefetch for short-heavy traffic but avoid across-the-board increases.

3) NCCL algorithm sensitivity:
   - TREE algo marginally better for p99 in fully-local NVLink configs, but worse throughput for large-batch synthetic tests. RING produced more consistent throughput but higher tail without staggering.
   - NCCL_P2P_LEVEL=NVL is critical for intra-node performance; forcing SYS drops perf and increases CPU involvement.

4) Host-staging queue depth:
   - Very deep staging (16) amplifies clustering because more requests are queued and then dispatched in bursts; shallow queue depth (1) reduces clustering but increases overhead and jitter. Depth=4 with staged offsets gives best trade-off.

5) Pacing + coalescing:
   - A runtime shim that coalesces handshake windows and artificially spaces start times (staggered offsets randomized in a small window) reduced p99 by ~40% without adding much overhead.
   - Microbenchmark: staggered offsets uniformly random in [0,5ms] reduced NVLink peak by ~45% and p99 from 248ms->150ms.

Anomalies & open questions:
- On one rack (mixed PCIe gen differences), staggering increased p50 unexpectedly. Hypothesis: in heterogeneous topologies, stagger may increase pipeline imbalance. Need targeted repro on heterogeneous node mixes.
- Occasional NCCL retry log entries that correlate with a host PCIe interrupt storm; not yet reproduced reliably.
- Interplay with runtime's batching heuristic: when batching is enabled, the batching coalescer sometimes defeats our stagger (they are both trying to batch request starts). Need a harmonized approach.

Suggested mitigations (short-term -> medium-term):
- Short-term (runtime change): implement handshake coalescer + staggerer: when multiple shards will invoke NCCL within X ms, spread them within a configurable window (default 5ms). Add metric to surface "handshake density" in Console.
- Medium-term: adaptive prefetch policy driven by traffic mix classifier: if short-heavy, automatically increase prefetch and enable staggerer; if medium/long-heavy, revert.
- NCCL tuning: set NCCL_ALGO heuristics per-model/sequence-length profiles (we can store model-specific recommended NCCL envs in model catalog).
- Long-term: kernel-level backpressure indicators (from nvlink or device perf) exposed to runtime for closed-loop pacing.

Action items / TODOs:
- INF-1423: implement handshake coalescer prototype in runtime (owner: Ethan) -> branch created, PR referenced in linked artifacts.
- Run repro on heterogeneous node mixes and 8x GPU trays (owner: Maya) by 2026-03-20.
- Add a telemetry metric to Console: "handshake_density_per_minute" and NVLink_peak_5s (owner: Sofia).
- Evaluate interaction with batching; two approaches: (A) let batching drive stagger decisions, (B) handshake coalescer sits above batching and coordinates. Implement tests for both.

Notes to self / rough ideas:
- Could try piggybacking handshake on the first host staging copy so collectives start only when the last copy arrives -> basically make collectives waiter-driven (might increase tail for the earliest shards).
- Explore if NCCL_TUNE environment knobs exist to smooth collective startup.
- Try disabling cudaLaunchBlocking in debug to see if kernel queuing jitter is a factor.

Appendix: raw snippets (selected)
- Example NCCL clustered init excerpt:
  [gpu0] ncclCommInitRank() start ts=1678932.123456
  [gpu1] ncclCommInitRank() start ts=1678932.125112
  [gpu2] ncclCommInitRank() start ts=1678932.126998
  [gpu3] ncclCommInitRank() start ts=1678932.130004
- NVLink peak sampled around same window: nvlink_top: 14.2GB/s (gpu0->gpu1) at ts=1678932.127

References / links:
- INF-1423 (Jira): prototype runtime coalescing work
- PR: runtime/pull/678 contains the first staggerer prototype (instrumentation-only, off by default)

If you want to reproduce locally: steps:
1) Checkout canary b3f9c2, build runtime with DEBUG_TRACING=1
2) Set up 4x A100 node (single host) with NCCL_DEBUG=INFO
3) Run harness: tools/perf/synthetic_mixed_short_medium.py --runtime-host localhost --duration 1800 --jitter 2 --short_frac 0.7
4) Collect nsys and nvlink_top logs in /tmp/handshake_probe/*

Questions for reviewers:
- Acceptable default stagger window? 5ms seems low-risk but we should validate on all rack types.
- Do we prefer a model-catalog driven static NCCL envs or a runtime auto-tuner?
- Is adding telemetry for handshake_density OK for privacy/metrics budget?

End notes: this feels actionable and low-risk to prototype. The ephemeral NVLink bursts are the most plausible single-point cause of the p99s we've been chasing. If reviewers agree, I'll move the prototype out of branch and open a small rollout on a canary pool; measure p99 reductions over one week.

-- Ethan

source.title: Relay: Pocket guide for night coverage (draft)
source.content:
Purpose: quick, portable pass for the next person taking coverage — not a full runbook. Keep this on your phone.

TL;DR (60s):
- If paged: check incident title in PagerDuty -> open the Linear ticket if linked.
- Verify alert: hit /metrics/alerts dashboard and the service health widget for redwood-serving.
- If CPU/GPU saturation and latency spike: toggle fallback-route to small-models (route: /fallback/compact) and notify #sre-night.
- If OOMs or CUDA errors: restart the affected worker group (cmd below), leave 1 instance warm, watch KV cache hit-rate.

Quick commands (use with care):
- List running workers: gcloud compute instances list --filter=\"labels:rw-worker\" --format=\"table(name,status,zone)\"
- Restart worker group (temp): terraform apply -target=module.worker_pool[\"night-temp\"] -var='region=us-west1' (note: this is a blunt hammer, use only when evicted jobs > 5 and metrics show all kernels stuck)
- Soft restart (preferred): curl -XPOST https://ops-api.redwood.internal/worker/rotate -d '{"pool":"hot-night","keep_warm":1}'
- Kill runaway process on host: ssh ops@<host> sudo pkill -f \"redwood-serving --worker-id=<id>\" (double-check host)

Immediate triage checklist (first 10 minutes):
1) Confirm alert (false positives happen): check metrics and logs.
2) Identity: which pool /region is affected? (hosted vs dedicated vs private)
3) Scope: single customer / route / entire model? Check recent deploys (deploy dashboard) and recent kernel updates.
4) Mitigate: apply temporary route/fallback or reduce concurrency for the model.
5) Communicate: post to #sre-night with short statement + ETA for fix.
6) Document: append notes to the Linear incident.

Common alarm fingerprints and quick fixes:
- High p95 latency + token queue growth: likely batching backlog. Quick fix: reduce batch_max_size or enable aggressive flush with curl -XPOST https://ops-api/.../batching?mode=aggressive
- GPU memory OOMs with new model version: rollback model version in model-catalog to previous stable; if rollback blocked, force-enable quantized-variant (model id: m-algo-q4_0)
- 502s from model host pool: check nginx upstream health, restart nginx on 1 or 2 hosts to avoid bringing whole pool down 
- Autoscaler oscillation (scale up/down repeatedly): apply temp min_instances=2 and disable autoscaler cooldown tweaks until morning

Escalation matrix (who to ping quickly):
- Pager triage: you (primary responder) -> ping Maya (SRE lead) if more than 30m unresolved or multiple pages
- If it's model-quality/outputs: notify Marcus (applied-ml) and cc product-hosted-api
- Infra-level (hardware faults): call Data Center on-call (internal: +1-555-0199) and page infra-oncall

Known fragile spots (notes to self):
- KV cache rebuild takes ~12–15 minutes after full pool recycle; prefer rolling restarts.
- The us-west1 burst pool loses prefix caching after scaling to zero — watch latency 10–20 minutes post-scale.
- Recent kernel 2.7.1 introduced a rare deadlock under long-sequence attention. Ticket: ONCALL-298 (link above). Workaround: pin model to kernels 2.6.x for now.

Temporary procedures (for this week only):
- Night coverage aggressive fallback enabled between 01:00–07:00 PST: any latency >1.2s triggers model fallback to compact variants and an automated note posted to incidents channel.
- If throughput drops >40% and autoscaler failed to add nodes: manually set min_nodes=2 for night-pool and scale up by 1.
- For private deployments: if customer VPN flaps, toggle regional-fallback in routing policy to route to dedicated-hosted variant (make sure you have authorization; see linked_confluence page).

Things to check in dashboards:
- Serving runtime dashboard: p50/p90/p95, queue depth, KV cache hit-rate, in-flight token rate.
- Autoscaler dashboard: desired vs actual nodes, last scale events, cooldown intervals.
- Logs: look for repeated stack traces containing \"TensorMemoryError\" or \"cudaLaunchKernel\" (filter by last 30 minutes).

Notes-to-self (things I need to follow up):
- Investigate why last night's incident had a slow rollback — maybe the deploy rollback hook didn't trigger when model-catalog was being updated. Action: open a ticket with deploy-team and capture sequence of API calls.
- Draft a short playbook for quantized fallbacks: which variants are safe for which model families. Target: complete by next Tuesday.

Communications template (use when posting to Slack/incident):
- Header: [INCIDENT] short-title | impact summary | region | ETA
- Body: brief timeline, mitigation applied (e.g., \"fallback to compact models enabled\"), next steps, who is investigating.
- Footer: link to Linear incident + console links.

Unresolved questions (for morning):
- Why did the autoscaler underreport available GPUs for ~8 minutes? Hypothesis: node registration lag after restart. Need logs from node-init.
- Are there any customers with experimental pinned quant variants? Could be causing incompatibility.

After-shift TODOs (what I will hand off in morning):
- Add timeline entries to the Linear incident covering actions I took and why.
- If any temporary min_instances set, revert and note reasoning.
- Follow up with deploy-team on the rollback hook test.

Random micro-hacks that saved time (history):
- If a host refuses to drain because of stuck agents, use the drain-tolerant script: /opt/ops/scripts/drain-stubborn.sh <host> (use only after consulting SRE lead)
- To quickly check which model variant the router chose for a request id: curl -s https://ops-api/.../trace/<request_id> | jq -r '.chosen_variant'

Personal reminder: keep the pass short. If it's >5 minutes to explain an incident, escalate and handoff — don't try to fix everything alone at 3am.

End of pocket guide. If you're holding this and need me specifically, ping @ethan on slack (I check overnight less often). If it's urgent, page the SRE rotation instead.

---
Draft history: initial notes 2025-11-20; added autoscaler section 2026-01-15; patched with kernel workaround 2026-02-28.

source.title: Manager sync alignment canvas — prep and evidence
source.content:
Purpose:
- Prepare a tight 1:1 agenda to align on priorities, promotion calibration signals, and 90-day commitments. Keep it under 30 minutes.

Context / What I want from this sync:
- Get manager alignment on the promotion readiness signal set for IC3 -> IC4.
- Lock a measurable project scope that I can deliver in the next 90 days that ties to platform reliability and developer onboarding.
- Agree on two stretch outcomes (impact metrics) and one mitigation plan if baseline delivery slips.

Top-line narrative I want to get across (2–3 sentences):
- Over the last 6 months I shifted from handling ad-hoc onboarding tickets to leading a small cross-functional effort that reduced time-to-first-success for new accounts. The work shows early signs of increased adoption (onboarding completion) but needs a defined rollout and measuring plan to prove sustained impact.

Evidence & signals (ordered by strength):
1) Quantitative metrics: onboarding funnel conversion improved from 42% -> 58% in pilot (Q4 -> Q1). Source: onboarding-tracker sheet (link above).
2) Cost/efficiency: automated checklist cut manual triage time by ~18 developer-hours/week (tracked in INF-342).
3) Cross-team influence: set up weekly sync with SE and infra, resulting in two infra optimizations merged (PR #88).
4) Customer feedback: 3 enterprise pilots reported faster MLP (notes in Confluence).
5) Gaps: no A/B style control group; sample size small (5 pilots). Need a clear rollout to validate.

Concrete asks (ranked):
- Primary ask: Manager approval to run a staged rollout to 25 new accounts and the go/no-go metric (onboarding completion >= 65% within 14 days).
- Resourcing ask: 0.1 FTE for 8 weeks from Solutions Eng (ask: Maya or Priya) to instrument analytics.
- Career ask: Consider my readiness for IC4 review in next council cycle if rollout hits metrics and I present results.

Proposed 90-day plan (milestones + owner + metric):
- Week 0–2: Finalize rollout plan, measurement spec, dashboard (Owner: me; Metric: dashboard prototype).
- Week 3–6: Run pilot cohort A (12 accounts) with instrumentation; weekly check-ins (Owner: me + SE).
- Week 7–10: Iterate and expand to cohort B (13 accounts); adjust onboarding flow based on feedback (Owner: me).
- Week 11–12: Consolidate results, write a one-pager + demo for leadership and the promotion packet (Owner: me).

Potential blockers and mitigation:
- Blocker: instrumentation delays (analytics bandwidth). Mitigation: ask for 0.1 FTE and start with manual tagging for early signals.
- Blocker: competing infra work in the same sprint. Mitigation: negotiate 2-week protected window for rollout tasks.
- Blocker: pushback on metric definitions. Mitigation: pre-read sent 48h before sync and a single decider (manager) defined.

Manager conversation bullets (30s soundbites):
- "This is a metrics-driven 90-day experiment that maps directly to onboarding adoption and developer time saved."
- "If we hit 65% completion within 14 days across 25 accounts, I want a calibration conversation for IC4 in the next cycle."
- "I need brief support from SE for instrumenting events; loaning 0.1 FTE for 8 weeks would unblock the analytics."

Calibration signals I care about (for promotion discussion):
- Observable impact on a measurable company metric (onboarding completion).
- Scope: ownership of an end-to-end cross-functional initiative (designing, shipping, measuring).
- Influence: secured changes in infra/SE roadmap, not just suggestions.
- Craft: quality of execution and clear communication of outcomes to leadership.

Unresolved questions to bring up live:
- Is manager comfortable signing off on the 65% threshold or do they want a different bar?
- If rollout misses the metric due to sampling noise, is there a path toward incremental credit based on contribution?
- How do we time the promotion packet with the company council cycle?

Notes to self (do before sync):
- Send pre-read + dashboard screenshots 48h ahead. Attach PR #88 and key pilot notes.
- Practice the 30s narrative and the 3 evidence bullets.
- Prepare two fallback asks (e.g., smaller FTE or extended timeline).

Follow-ups (post-sync template to send within 24h):
- Short note: decisions made, owner of each action, dates.
- Add ticket in JIRA for rollout with acceptance criteria and link to dashboard.
- Schedule a 15-min check-in two weeks after pilot start.

Misc scratch notes (brainstorm / raw):
- If the manager is skeptical, present risk-reward: 0.1 FTE for 8 weeks vs expected dev-hours saved of ~72 hours/wk across the org -> quick ROI math.
- Consider a scoped experiment limited to accounts that had a CSM already engaged to reduce confounding variables.
- Think about a customer quote or short clip for storytelling.

Raw timeline for promotion packet (tentative):
- Collect artifacts and evidence: week 6–10.
- Draft narrative & examples: week 10–11.
- Manager review & edits: week 11.
- Submit packet to council: aligned to next cycle (dates TBD).

Slack snippets to send pre-sync (draft):
- "hey @manager — sending a 2-pager and quick dashboard ahead of our sync. Goal: align on a 90-day rollout and check promotion signals. Want to confirm 30 minutes works for this conversation."
- "If you want, I can include a short ROI calc in the pre-read — ping if that's useful."

End-of-doc: quick checklist
- [ ] pre-read sent 48h before
- [ ] dashboard screenshot attached
- [ ] ask for 0.1 FTE drafted
- [ ] JIRA INF-342 updated with acceptance criteria
- [ ] promotion packet skeleton created

Small log entries (for audit):
- 2026-02-08: initial idea sketched after pilot wrap-up.
- 2026-02-24: first draft of measurement spec (shared with Priya).
- 2026-03-01: dashboard prototype pushed to staging (PR #88).
