- Get manager alignment on the promotion readiness signal set for IC3 -> IC4.
- Lock a measurable project scope that I can deliver in the next 90 days that ties to platform reliability and developer onboarding.
- Agree on two stretch outcomes (impact metrics) and one mitigation plan if baseline delivery slips.

Top-line narrative I want to get across (2–3 sentences):
- Over the last 6 months I shifted from handling ad-hoc onboarding tickets to leading a small cross-functional effort that reduced time-to-first-success for new accounts. The work shows early signs of increased adoption (onboarding completion) but needs a defined rollout and measuring plan to prove sustained impact.

Evidence & signals (ordered by strength):
1) Quantitative metrics: onboarding funnel conversion improved from 42% -> 58% in pilot (Q4 -> Q1). Source: onboarding-tracker sheet (link above).
2) Cost/efficiency: automated checklist cut manual triage time by ~18 developer-hours/week (tracked in INF-342).
3) Cross-team influence: set up weekly sync with SE and infra, resulting in two infra optimizations merged (PR #88).
4) Customer feedback: 3 enterprise pilots reported faster MLP (notes in Confluence).
5) Gaps: no A/B style control group; sample size small (5 pilots). Need a clear rollout to validate.

Concrete asks (ranked):
- Primary ask: Manager approval to run a staged rollout to 25 new accounts and the go/no-go metric (onboarding completion >= 65% within 14 days).
- Resourcing ask: 0.1 FTE for 8 weeks from Solutions Eng (ask: Maya or Priya) to instrument analytics.
- Career ask: Consider my readiness for IC4 review in next council cycle if rollout hits metrics and I present results.

Proposed 90-day plan (milestones + owner + metric):
- Week 0–2: Finalize rollout plan, measurement spec, dashboard (Owner: me; Metric: dashboard prototype).
- Week 3–6: Run pilot cohort A (12 accounts) with instrumentation; weekly check-ins (Owner: me + SE).
- Week 7–10: Iterate and expand to cohort B (13 accounts); adjust onboarding flow based on feedback (Owner: me).
- Week 11–12: Consolidate results, write a one-pager + demo for leadership and the promotion packet (Owner: me).

Potential blockers and mitigation:
- Blocker: instrumentation delays (analytics bandwidth). Mitigation: ask for 0.1 FTE and start with manual tagging for early signals.
- Blocker: competing infra work in the same sprint. Mitigation: negotiate 2-week protected window for rollout tasks.
- Blocker: pushback on metric definitions. Mitigation: pre-read sent 48h before sync and a single decider (manager) defined.

Manager conversation bullets (30s soundbites):
- "This is a metrics-driven 90-day experiment that maps directly to onboarding adoption and developer time saved."
- "If we hit 65% completion within 14 days across 25 accounts, I want a calibration conversation for IC4 in the next cycle."
- "I need brief support from SE for instrumenting events; loaning 0.1 FTE for 8 weeks would unblock the analytics."

Calibration signals I care about (for promotion discussion):
- Observable impact on a measurable company metric (onboarding completion).
- Scope: ownership of an end-to-end cross-functional initiative (designing, shipping, measuring).
- Influence: secured changes in infra/SE roadmap, not just suggestions.
- Craft: quality of execution and clear communication of outcomes to leadership.

Unresolved questions to bring up live:
- Is manager comfortable signing off on the 65% threshold or do they want a different bar?
- If rollout misses the metric due to sampling noise, is there a path toward incremental credit based on contribution?
- How do we time the promotion packet with the company council cycle?

Notes to self (do before sync):
- Send pre-read + dashboard screenshots 48h ahead. Attach PR #88 and key pilot notes.
- Practice the 30s narrative and the 3 evidence bullets.
- Prepare two fallback asks (e.g., smaller FTE or extended timeline).

Follow-ups (post-sync template to send within 24h):
- Short note: decisions made, owner of each action, dates.
- Add ticket in JIRA for rollout with acceptance criteria and link to dashboard.
- Schedule a 15-min check-in two weeks after pilot start.

Misc scratch notes (brainstorm / raw):
- If the manager is skeptical, present risk-reward: 0.1 FTE for 8 weeks vs expected dev-hours saved of ~72 hours/wk across the org -> quick ROI math.
- Consider a scoped experiment limited to accounts that had a CSM already engaged to reduce confounding variables.
- Think about a customer quote or short clip for storytelling.

Raw timeline for promotion packet (tentative):
- Collect artifacts and evidence: week 6–10.
- Draft narrative & examples: week 10–11.
- Manager review & edits: week 11.
- Submit packet to council: aligned to next cycle (dates TBD).

Slack snippets to send pre-sync (draft):
- "hey @manager — sending a 2-pager and quick dashboard ahead of our sync. Goal: align on a 90-day rollout and check promotion signals. Want to confirm 30 minutes works for this conversation."
- "If you want, I can include a short ROI calc in the pre-read — ping if that's useful."

End-of-doc: quick checklist
- [ ] pre-read sent 48h before
- [ ] dashboard screenshot attached
- [ ] ask for 0.1 FTE drafted
- [ ] JIRA INF-342 updated with acceptance criteria
- [ ] promotion packet skeleton created

Small log entries (for audit):
- 2026-02-08: initial idea sketched after pilot wrap-up.
- 2026-02-24: first draft of measurement spec (shared with Priya).
- 2026-03-01: dashboard prototype pushed to staging (PR #88).

source.title: Evidence threads: 6-week experiments & manager asks
source.content:
Context:
- Short running journal to collect discrete evidence moments, tiny experiments, and phrased asks I can bring to 1:1s and calibration. Use for mid-cycle check and manager prep.
- Goal window: 6 weeks (Feb 1 → Mar 15), then iterate.

High-level goals (what I want to move):
1) Increase cross-functional influence: lead two product-people syncs and ship a hiring rubric change that reduces time-to-hire by measurable steps.
2) Level-up coaching: create 3 repackaged manager coaching templates, pilot with 4 managers.
3) Metrics & ops: reduce offer turnaround time from 6 days → 3 days for mid-level ICs.

Signals I’m tracking (quick proxies):
- Number of cross-team playbooks referenced in Slack/Confluence (target +2)
- Manager coaching NPS (post-session survey) ≥ 4.0
- Offer turnaround median <= 72 hours for target roles

Evidence entries (atomic, timestamped):
- 2026-02-03: Ran first "interview-rubric alignment" session with Eng-Platform (notes link: confluence). Outcome: unified rubric v0.1, eng-l2 agreed to pilot on two roles. Ask -> need support from hiring ops to bake rubric into ATS.
- 2026-02-11: Quick coaching pilot w/Manager A. Feedback: valued structure but wanted shorter templates. Next step: create 10-15 min micro-coaching card.
- 2026-02-18: Drafted offer-package checklist that removed redundant approval step; used on Offer #45, saved ~24h.
- 2026-02-24: Facilitated retro with Solutions Eng where we identified 3 process gaps impacting onboarding (doc: retro notes).

Micro-experiments (hypothesis → experiment → result):
- H1: Short templates (10–15 min) increase manager adoption.
  Experiment: Ship 3 cards, pilot with 4 managers for 2 weeks (Feb 12–Feb 26).
  Result: 3/4 managers used them, average session shortened 9 minutes, adoption note: still need reminders.
- H2: One-click offer checklist reduces friction and approval wait.
  Experiment: Manual checklist used on 6 offers.
  Result: median turnaround dropped from 6d → 4.25d.

Patterns / Themes observed:
- Managers want "bite-sized" playbooks, not full checklists during 1:1s.
- Process change adoption stalls without an owner in hiring ops; initial wins regress otherwise.
- Cross-team alignment is easier when we ship minimal runnable artifacts (templates + one pager) vs full training.

Manager 1:1 agenda (2 pages max):
- 3 quick wins since last 1:1 (atomic evidence bullets)
- Ask (specific): e.g., "Support to assign hiring-ops owner for rubric pilot"
- Calibration snippet: 2–3 lines framing impact + risk if not addressed
- Closing: 1 concrete next step we want from manager (assign owner, approve budget, unblock hiring pipeline)

Example phrasing for asks (short, testable):
- "Can you assign a hiring ops owner for the rubric pilot this week so we can instrument ATS changes by Mar 1?"
- "I need 4 manager volunteers for a 2-week micro-coaching pilot starting Feb 12 — can you recommend two?"

Promotion/comp conversation prep (notes to self):
- Frame around measurable outcomes (offers sped up, manager coaching adoption, cross-team artifacts shipped) not just time spent.
- Bring 5-7 short evidence bullets each with link + quick metric.
- Have a timeline ask: "Target calibration for level bump in Q3; what milestones should I hit by end of Q2?"

Unresolved questions / hypotheses to test next window:
- Which hiring roles show the biggest latency bottleneck? (need better breakdown by role family)
- Can we automate reminders for managers to use micro-coaching cards (Slack workflow)?
- What is the minimum instrumentation needed to claim a 30% reduction in time-to-offer?

Action backlog (next 6 weeks):
- Finalize rubric v0.1 and create ATS field mapping (owner: hiring-ops) — due Mar 1
- Ship micro-coaching cards v0.2 based on pilot feedback — due Feb 28
- Instrument offer checklist and capture time-to-offer events in analytics — due Mar 8
- Book calibration sync with my manager + EM peer (30m) — request this week

Meta notes to self (style + signals):
- Keep asks binary and timeboxed.
- Use "impact + risk" framing rather than long backstory.
- Archive evidence bullets as separate short notes so they’re copy/paste-ready for promotion packet.

Quick copy-paste evidence set for manager: (3 bullets)
- Reduced median time-to-offer from 6d → 4.25d across 6 pilot offers (Feb). Link: JIRA-HR-298.
- Piloted micro-coaching with 4 managers; adoption 75% and average session length reduced by 9 minutes (Feb 12–26).
- Aligned Eng-Platform on interview rubric v0.1; two L2 roles onboarded to pilot.

Meeting prompts I want from manager (for coaching):
- How do you see the priority between hiring speed vs candidate experience at this scale?
- Any suggestions on cross-team leverage points to make rubric adoption stick?

Notes to file/next steps: split evidence bullets into a short "promotion-ready" doc and a public playbook for managers. Keep this scratch doc as the raw journal.

-- end of scratch journal --

source.title: Safety sprint notes: control gaps & token caching thoughts
source.content:
Context / purpose:
- Quick personal scratchpad from the "safety sprint" sync (2/11) — trying to capture potential control gaps that are specific to inference runtime features (KV cache, model fallback, routing).
- Goal: produce a minimal set of evidentiary ideas and owners to iterate into a formal control mapping next week.

Top-of-mind risks (short bullets):
- Untracked temporary state in GPU-hosted KV caches that could leak PII across tenants if eviction/flush not enforced on tenant boundaries.
- Inconsistent encryption for cache snapshots used for warm starts in Private deployments (some infra paths rely on local disk, others use encrypted S3).
- Model fallback chain (hosted->dedicated->private) could surface different model versions with different logging levels — creates gaps in audit logging and traceability.
- Rollout/canary logic that silently routes requests to fallback model without generating a policy-aligned audit event.
- Secrets (model weights, certs) staged on build nodes during on-prem installs — ephemeral but not always rotated/cleared.

Initial control mapping brainstorm (very rough) — map feature -> control idea -> quick evidence / next steps:
1) KV cache tenancy isolation
   - control idea: enforce per-customer namespaced caches, mandatory zeroing of cache on instance reassign, and runtime assertions rejecting cross-tenant cache hits.
   - evidence: runtime logs showing cache namespace creation + last-zero timestamps; unit/integration test output for eviction; design doc section.
   - next: add an automated smoke test (ENGINF-2345) that simulates tenant reassign and validates no stale cache hits. Owner: @Maya.

2) Cache snapshot encryption at rest (Private deployments)
   - control idea: require KMS-backed encryption for any persistent cache snapshot or warm-start artifact; policy to forbid cleartext local snapshot.
   - evidence: deploy templates showing KMS key usage, sample terraform module, and deploy logs with key id.
   - questions: do we have a standardized KMS integration for non-cloud/on-prem? If not, doc fallback expectations. Owner: @Noah.

3) Model fallback audit events
   - control idea: all routing decisions that deviate from primary policy generate an immutable audit event with request id, reason, and model version.
   - evidence: sample audit stream (S3/Kafka) items; alert runbook referencing the audit field.
   - next: spike patch to ensure fallback path calls Audit.emit(...). Owner: eng-serving-runtime (TBD).

4) Release/rollout traceability across environments
   - control idea: promote a canonical release manifest artifact that travels with deployment and is captured in logs for every inference request (model-version + bundle-hash).
   - evidence: manifest generation script, console screenshot showing rollout mapping.

5) Secrets lifecycle for on-prem installers
   - control idea: ephemeral credential broker for installers; mandatory rotation at first boot; installer must zero any local staging files.
   - evidence: installer log snippets, rotated key timestamp.

Open questions / TODOs for the sprint:
- Q: How granular are we required to be for SOC 2 docs here? Do we need independent evidence for each node type (GPU vs CPU) or is service-level evidence acceptable? (Ask compliance lead: Priya)
- Q: For Private customers that manage their own KMS, is our control "enforce use of KMS" or "document customer-managed KMS configuration"? Need a policy decision.
- TODO: draft a short runbook for fallout scenarios where cache leakage is suspected (who to page, immediate mitigation commands). Owner: Ethan.
- TODO: generate a one-page diagram showing data flow through cache + model routing for the auditor; use simplified customer A/B example. Owner: Maya.

Evidence priority matrix (quick prioritization):
- P1 (high): cache tenancy isolation tests + fallback audit events (these have clear risk to customer data / traceability)
- P2: KMS usage documentation for snapshots, installer secret lifecycle
- P3: rollout manifest screenshots and release manifest automation

Notes from 2/11 pairing with runtime team (raw):
- "We currently namespace by tenant id in memory, but when instances scale down we persist a warm snapshot to /var/cache/warm-snap if enabled — not always encrypted" — (quote from @arun).
- Runtime team thinking: flip default for warm-snapshot to OFF for Private until we have standard KMS integration. That seems like an easy interim control.
- SRE mentioned: metric 'cache.namespace.eviction.failures' exists but no alert tied to it; should probably add an alert threshold.

Quick checklist to convert this into a control artifact:
- [ ] Draft control statement (one-liner) for each of the 5 items above.
- [ ] Identify and collect 3 evidence artifacts per control (logs, screenshots, test output).
- [ ] Assign an owner and a date for evidence collection.

title: Safety sprint notes: control gaps & token caching thoughts
preview:
Context / purpose:
- Quick personal scratchpad from the "safety sprint" sync (2/11) — trying to capture potential control gaps that are specific to inference runtime features (KV cache, model fallback, routing).
- Goal: produce a minimal set of evidentiary ideas and owners to iterate into a formal control mapping next week.

Top-of-mind risks (short bullets):
- Untracked temporary state in GPU-hosted KV caches that could leak PII across tenants if eviction/flush not enforced on tenant boundaries.
- Inconsistent encryption for cache snapshots used for warm starts in Private deployments (some infra paths rely on local disk, others use encrypted S3).
- Model fallback chain (hosted->dedicated->private) could surface different model versions with different logging levels — creates gaps in audit logging and traceability.
- Rollout/canary logic that silently routes requests to fallback model without generating a policy-aligned audit event.
- Secrets (model weights, certs) staged on build nodes during on-prem installs — ephemeral but not always rotated/cleared.

Initial control mapping brainstorm (very rough) — map feature -> control idea -> quick evidence / next steps:
1) KV cache tenancy isolation
   - control idea: enforce per-customer namespaced caches, mandatory zeroing of cache on instance reassign, and runtime assertions rejecting cross-tenant cache hits.
   - evidence: runtime logs showing cache namespace creation + last-zero timestamps; unit/integration test output for eviction; design doc section.
   - next: add an automated smoke test (ENGINF-2345) that simulates tenant reassign and validates no stale cache hits. Owner: @Maya.

2) Cache snapshot encryption at rest (Private deployments)
   - control idea: require KMS-backed encryption for any persistent cache snapshot or warm-start artifact; policy to forbid cleartext local snapshot.
   - evidence: deploy templates showing KMS key usage, sample terraform module, and deploy logs with key id.
   - questions: do we have a standardized KMS integration for non-cloud/on-prem? If not, doc fallback expectations. Owner: @Noah.

3) Model fallback audit events
   - control idea: all routing decisions that deviate from primary policy generate an immutable audit event with request id, reason, and model version.
   - evidence: sample audit stream (S3/Kafka) items; alert runbook referencing the audit field.
   - next: spike patch to ensure fallback path calls Audit.emit(...). Owner: eng-serving-runtime (TBD).

4) Release/rollout traceability across environments
   - control idea: promote a canonical release manifest artifact that travels with deployment and is captured in logs for every inference request (model-version + bundle-hash).
   - evidence: manifest generation script, console screenshot showing rollout mapping.

5) Secrets lifecycle for on-prem installers
   - control idea: ephemeral credential broker for installers; mandatory rotation at first boot; installer must zero any local staging files.
   - evidence: installer log snippets, rotated key timestamp.

Open questions / TODOs for the sprint:
- Q: How granular are we required to be for SOC 2 docs here? Do we need independent evidence for each node type (GPU vs CPU) or is service-level evidence acceptable? (Ask compliance lead: Priya)
- Q: For Private customers that manage their own KMS, is our control "enforce use of KMS" or "document customer-managed KMS configuration"? Need a policy decision.
- TODO: draft a short runbook for fallout scenarios where cache leakage is suspected (who to page, immediate mitigation commands). Owner: Ethan.
- TODO: generate a one-page diagram showing data flow through cache + model routing for the auditor; use simplified customer A/B example. Owner: Maya.

Evidence priority matrix (quick prioritization):
- P1 (high): cache tenancy isolation tests + fallback audit events (these have clear risk to customer data / traceability)
- P2: KMS usage documentation for snapshots, installer secret lifecycle
- P3: rollout manifest screenshots and release manifest automation

Notes from 2/11 pairing with runtime team (raw):
- "We currently namespace by tenant id in memory, but when instances scale down we persist a warm snapshot to /var/cache/warm-snap if enabled — not always encrypted" — (quote from @arun).
- Runtime team thinking: flip default for warm-snapshot to OFF for Private until we have standard KMS integration. That seems like an easy interim control.
- SRE mentioned: metric 'cache.namespace.eviction.failures' exists but no alert tied to it; should probably add an alert threshold.

Quick checklist to convert this into a control artifact:
- [ ] Draft control statement (one-liner) for each of the 5 items above.
- [ ] Identify and collect 3 evidence artifacts per control (logs, screenshots, test output).
- [ ] Assign an owner and a date for evidence collection.
- [ ] Link to Jira tasks and add a review meeting with Compliance (Priya) next week.

Possible quick mitigations to propose:
- Disable warm-snap by default in Private packages and mark as opt-in (config flag) until KMS enforced.
- Add audit emission on any routing decision that results in model variant change (low-code change in router).
- Add a post-install hook to installer that securely wipes staging credential files and reports rotation.

Random notes / thoughts to remember:
- Keep auditor examples simple: show a single request id flow from API gateway -> router -> model host -> audit sink.
- If we need a table to show mapping to the Trust Services Criteria, do it in Confluence not here. This doc is a scratchpad.
- Consider adding an integration test runner that executes in a Private demo cluster overnight and produces a 'privacy smoke report' (pass/fail + artifacts).

Meeting seeds to raise at next Compliance sync:
- Proposal: default-off warm snapshots (timeline: 1 week)
- Audit event addition to routing (timeline: 2 weeks)
- Installer secret broker prototype (timeline: 4 weeks)

End of notes — will convert to proper control mapping doc once we have owners confirmed and sample logs collected.

source.title: Asymmetric slotting & epoch-based batching: proposal for hybrid token/request fairness
source.body:
Summary:

This note proposes a hybrid continuous-batching scheme that combines asymmetric slot assignment with an epoch-based opportunistic flush to reconcile token-level efficiency and request-level fairness. The goal is to keep high GPU utilization (by packing tokens) while bounding per-request latency variance and avoiding starvation for long-running or low-token-rate requests.

Why a new hybrid?
- Token-level batching maximizes throughput but can starve low-rate requests or produce large tail-latency for small requests when dominated by large-token requests.
- Request-level batching preserves request semantics (finish whole request together) but underutilizes kernels when sequence lengths are short/variable.
- Existing continuous batching drafts explored fairness apportioners and sliding-window schedules; this design tries to be operationally simple and robust to KV-cache locality and kernel switching costs.

Design goals (explicit):
1) Maintain >85% steady-state token throughput on standard benchmarks (web-chat mixes).
2) 95th percentile request latency within 1.5x of request-level baseline for 90% of traffic slices.
3) Prevent starvation: every request gets a guaranteed forward progress token budget within T_progress (configurable epoch length).
4) Keep kernel-switch overheads low by favoring batches with homogeneous sequence-length groups and KV-cache affinity.
5) Simple rollout path (feature flag + tunable params) and observable metrics for debugging.

High-level idea: epochs + asymmetric slots
- Time is partitioned into short epochs (configurable, e.g., 8-50ms depending on SLOs). Within each epoch the scheduler assigns 'slots' that represent micro-batches of token work.
- Asymmetric slotting: slots can be sized differently (number of tokens) and prioritized by request class. Heavy requests (long sequences, many tokens pending) get larger slots but lower frequency; light requests get smaller slots more often.
- Every request is guaranteed at least one slot per epoch window every N epochs (configurable), which bounds starvation.
- On epoch boundary, an opportunity flush groups compatible slots into an actual GPU batch considering kernel choice and KV cache locality. If compatibility is low, flush policy falls back to prioritized packing to maximize utilization.

Key components:
1) Slot allocator (fast, in-memory):
   - Tracks requests in a ready queue with attributes: pending_tokens, seq_len_hint, kv_keyset_fingerprint, class_tag (interactive/background/priority), arrival_ts, accumulated_wait
   - Each epoch the allocator emits a list of candidate slots. A slot = (request_id, token_count, slot_deadline_ts, slot_priority_score).
   - Token_count is chosen by a slot-sizing heuristic: min(max_chunk, pending_tokens, ceil(target_epoch_token_budget * request_share)).
   - Accumulated_wait influences priority_score to ensure aging.

2) Epoch packer (at epoch boundary):
   - Collects candidate slots from current epoch and previous outstanding slots with unfilled work.
   - Computes compatibility groups: same kernel bucket (short/med/long), similar seq_len distribution, and KV cache affinity score.
   - Runs a fast greedy pack: pick highest combined score groups up to GPU token budget for the next kernel launch.
   - If kernel switching cost is prohibitive, prefer larger single-kernel pack and defer incompatible slots to next epoch.

3) KV-cache affinity score:
   - Each slot has a small fingerprint summarizing its KV keys (e.g., top-k embedding keys or last-requested token prefix hash).
   - Affinity score between two slots = Jaccard_estimate or hash-prefix match term. If affinity high -> co-locate in same batch to reuse KV cache pages across requests.

Fairness mechanisms:
- Epoch guarantee: every request must receive at least G_min tokens within every E epochs (G_min, E are config). This prevents indefinite deferral of low-rate requests.
- Lost-progress credit: when a slot is partially filled or preempted, the request accumulates a credit that increases its priority next epoch (compensatory fairness).
- Max-wait soft-SLO: requests with wait > W_max are escalated to a high-priority service lane and get dedicated small slots until satisfied.
- Interleaving countermeasure: don't coalesce too many small requests into one giant token batch if doing so would create spikes in per-request completion time; set an upper bound on tokens-per-request-per-epoch.

Kernel and execution considerations:
- Kernel buckets: short/medium/long by sequence-length distribution. We attempt to minimize kernel switching frequency within a single GPU launch.
- Kernel selection cost modeled as a fixed penalty + per-token variance; scheduler should only switch when expected utilization gain > penalty threshold.
- For mixed groups, if affinity low and kernel switching penalty low (e.g., unified kernel available), prefer mixing to reduce latency.

KV-cache integration:
- Prefetch hints: the allocator provides kernel with predicted keysets for the next K slots so runtime can prefetch KV pages (warm start), particularly useful for LRU-backed GPU cache.
- Cache-aware packing metric: score = alpha * affinity + beta * kernel_homogeneity + gamma * priority_score. Tunable weights.
- Eviction policy interplay: avoid batching lots of requests that evict each other's working set within same epoch. Add anti-eviction penalty if predicted working sets conflict.

Observability and metrics (must be emitted per-epoch):
- epoch_id, epoch_duration_ms, slots_emitted, tokens_scheduled, kernel_bucket_selected, kernel_switch_count, average_slot_wait_ms, per-class 50/95/99p completion latency, starvation_count, kv_affinity_mean, prefetch_hit_rate, lost_progress_credits_issued
- Heatmaps for per-request progress (tokens completed vs time) to assess fairness.

Simulation and validation plan:
1) Simulator (Py): implement slot allocator + packer + simple KV cache model. Run on recorded traces (web-chat, api-burst, background-embed).
2) Metrics: throughput, mean/95/99p latency, worst-case starvation length, kernel-switch frequency, KV cache hit rate.
3) Baselines: token-continuous (current), strict request-batcher (baseline), prioritized-epoch-lite (our previous prototype).
4) Sweep key params: epoch_ms (8, 16, 32, 64), G_min (1, 4, 8 tokens), alpha/beta/gamma weights.

Operational rollout path:
- Feature-flagged scheduler rollout to canary fleet. Start with conservative params: epoch=32ms, G_min=2, prefer kernel homogeneity (beta high).
- Observe per-tenant tail latency and kernel-switch counts for 24h, compare to baseline for rollback criteria.

Open questions / risks:
- Complexity vs debuggability: multiple moving parts (epoching, credits, affinity) may make root-cause hard. Need clear logs and synthetic tests.
- KV fingerprint collisions: small fingerprint may mislead affinity; need to instrument real collision rate and fallback behavior.
- Interaction with autoscaling: if scheduler defers work too aggressively, autoscaler sees lower instantaneous queue depth and may scale down prematurely. We need a 'virtual backlog' metric that reflects pending tokens.
- Multi-tenant fairness: current plan is tenant-aware, but policy for cross-tenant slotting and billing/cost allocation needs product input.

Implementation TODOs:
- [ ] Py simulator + trace runner (owner: Priya)
- [ ] Slot allocator skeleton in C++ runtime, exposed via feature flag (owner: Ethan)
- [ ] Small API addition: request metadata includes seq_len_hint and KV fingerprint (owner: Lina)
- [ ] Instrumentation hooks for epoch metrics (owner: Daniel)
- [ ] Small canary rollout doc with rollback thresholds (owner: product-eng lead)

Short checklist for experiment configs (initial):
- epoch_ms: 32
- G_min: 2 tokens
- E (epochs window for guarantee): 4
- max_tokens_per_request_per_epoch: 128
- kernel_switch_penalty_estimate: 4ms (conservative)
- affinity_weights: alpha=0.6, beta=0.25, gamma=0.15

Notes to reviewers:
- This is intentionally a hybrid — we trade a bit more scheduler logic to get both utilization and bounded fairness. Please challenge the assumptions about kernel penalty and KV fingerprint reliability.
- If we like the simulation results, we should push a minimal server-side pilot that only implements epoching + simple G_min guarantee (no affinity) to see the fairness curve change quickly.

References and related work:
- prior drafts in this dir: sliding-window, token-level apportioner sketches, and probabilistic-windowing notes (see linked artifacts).
- internal slack thread: discussion on kv affinity heuristics (linked).

End.

- On epoch boundary, an opportunity flush groups compatible slots into an actual GPU batch considering kernel choice and KV cache locality. If compatibility is low, flush policy falls back to prioritized packing to maximize utilization.

Key components:
1) Slot allocator (fast, in-memory):
   - Tracks requests in a ready queue with attributes: pending_tokens, seq_len_hint, kv_keyset_fingerprint, class_tag (interactive/background/priority), arrival_ts, accumulated_wait
   - Each epoch the allocator emits a list of candidate slots. A slot = (request_id, token_count, slot_deadline_ts, slot_priority_score).
   - Token_count is chosen by a slot-sizing heuristic: min(max_chunk, pending_tokens, ceil(target_epoch_token_budget * request_share)).
   - Accumulated_wait influences priority_score to ensure aging.

2) Epoch packer (at epoch boundary):
   - Collects candidate slots from current epoch and previous outstanding slots with unfilled work.
   - Computes compatibility groups: same kernel bucket (short/med/long), similar seq_len distribution, and KV cache affinity score.
   - Runs a fast greedy pack: pick highest combined score groups up to GPU token budget for the next kernel launch.
   - If kernel switching cost is prohibitive, prefer larger single-kernel pack and defer incompatible slots to next epoch.

3) KV-cache affinity score:
   - Each slot has a small fingerprint summarizing its KV keys (e.g., top-k embedding keys or last-requested token prefix hash).
   - Affinity score between two slots = Jaccard_estimate or hash-prefix match term. If affinity high -> co-locate in same batch to reuse KV cache pages across requests.

Fairness mechanisms:
- Epoch guarantee: every request must receive at least G_min tokens within every E epochs (G_min, E are config). This prevents indefinite deferral of low-rate requests.
- Lost-progress credit: when a slot is partially filled or preempted, the request accumulates a credit that increases its priority next epoch (compensatory fairness).
- Max-wait soft-SLO: requests with wait > W_max are escalated to a high-priority service lane and get dedicated small slots until satisfied.
- Interleaving countermeasure: don't coalesce too many small requests into one giant token batch if doing so would create spikes in per-request completion time; set an upper bound on tokens-per-request-per-epoch.

Kernel and execution considerations:
- Kernel buckets: short/medium/long by sequence-length distribution. We attempt to minimize kernel switching frequency within a single GPU launch.
- Kernel selection cost modeled as a fixed penalty + per-token variance; scheduler should only switch when expected utilization gain > penalty threshold.
- For mixed groups, if affinity low and kernel switching penalty low (e.g., unified kernel available), prefer mixing to reduce latency.

KV-cache integration:
- Prefetch hints: the allocator provides kernel with predicted keysets for the next K slots so runtime can prefetch KV pages (warm start), particularly useful for LRU-backed GPU cache.
- Cache-aware packing metric: score = alpha * affinity + beta * kernel_homogeneity + gamma * priority_score. Tunable weights.
- Eviction policy interplay: avoid batching lots of requests that evict each other's working set within same epoch. Add anti-eviction penalty if predicted working sets conflict.

Observability and metrics (must be emitted per-epoch):
- epoch_id, epoch_duration_ms, slots_emitted, tokens_scheduled, kernel_bucket_selected, kernel_switch_count, average_slot_wait_ms, per-class 50/95/99p completion latency, starvation_count, kv_affinity_mean, prefetch_hit_rate, lost_progress_credits_issued
- Heatmaps for per-request progress (tokens completed vs time) to assess fairness.

Simulation and validation plan:
1) Simulator (Py): implement slot allocator + packer + simple KV cache model. Run on recorded traces (web-chat, api-burst, background-embed).
2) Metrics: throughput, mean/95/99p latency, worst-case starvation length, kernel-switch frequency, KV cache hit rate.
3) Baselines: token-continuous (current), strict request-batcher (baseline), prioritized-epoch-lite (our previous prototype).
4) Sweep key params: epoch_ms (8, 16, 32, 64), G_min (1, 4, 8 tokens), alpha/beta/gamma weights.

Operational rollout path:
- Feature-flagged scheduler rollout to canary fleet. Start with conservative params: epoch=32ms, G_min=2, prefer kernel homogeneity (beta high).
- Observe per-tenant tail latency and kernel-switch counts for 24h, compare to baseline for rollback criteria.

Open questions / risks:
- Complexity vs debuggability: multiple moving parts (epoching, credits, affinity) may make root-cause hard. Need clear logs and synthetic tests.
- KV fingerprint collisions: small fingerprint may mislead affinity; need to instrument real collision rate and fallback behavior.
- Interaction with autoscaling: if scheduler defers work too aggressively, autoscaler sees lower instantaneous queue depth and may scale down prematurely. We need a 'virtual backlog' metric that reflects pending tokens.
- Multi-tenant fairness: current plan is tenant-aware, but policy for cross-tenant slotting and billing/cost allocation needs product input.

Implementation TODOs:
- [ ] Py simulator + trace runner (owner: Priya)
- [ ] Slot allocator skeleton in C++ runtime, exposed via feature flag (owner: Ethan)
- [ ] Small API addition: request metadata includes seq_len_hint and KV fingerprint (owner: Lina)
- [ ] Instrumentation hooks for epoch metrics (owner: Daniel)
- [ ] Small canary rollout doc with rollback thresholds (owner: product-eng lead)

Short checklist for experiment configs (initial):
- epoch_ms: 32
- G_min: 2 tokens
- E (epochs window for guarantee): 4
- max_tokens_per_request_per_epoch: 128
- kernel_switch_penalty_estimate: 4ms (conservative)
- affinity_weights: alpha=0.6, beta=0.25, gamma=0.15

Notes to reviewers:
- This is intentionally a hybrid — we trade a bit more scheduler logic to get both utilization and bounded fairness. Please challenge the assumptions about kernel penalty and KV fingerprint reliability.
- If we like the simulation results, we should push a minimal server-side pilot that only implements epoching + simple G_min guarantee (no affinity) to see the fairness curve change quickly.

References and related work:
- prior drafts in this dir: sliding-window, token-level apportioner sketches, and probabilistic-windowing notes (see linked artifacts).
- internal slack thread: discussion on kv affinity heuristics (linked).

End.

source.title: Steering Decision Graph & Control Plane API
source.content:
Purpose:
This note sketches a single, opinionated model for multi-region traffic steering that we can operate from the control plane: a decision graph that consumes real-time telemetry and static intent (region preference, tenant policy) and produces routing actions (DNS weight adjustments, region affinity hints, hard failover). Goal is to converge quickly to correct routing in common partial-failure/overload scenarios while keeping safety rails (rate limits, slow rollouts).

Motivation / problems we need to solve:
- Customers want regional preference (serve from nearest region unless explicit isolation required) but also predictable behavior under partial outages and capacity pressure.
- GeoDNS and Anycast are complementary but we need a coherent control-plane API to express policies and automated fallback.
- Avoid global flapping: too aggressive switching increases tail latency and cache misses.
- Allow per-tenant overrides (e.g., regulatory/latency SLOs) and be cost-aware (route to cheaper region when performance impact is small).

High-level architecture (decision flow):
1) Inputs:
   - Static intent: customer policy (preferred regions, acceptable fallback regions, max latency budget), global/regional SLOs, weight presets.
   - Telemetry: region-level capacity (GPU count, free slot %), egress latency p50/p95, error rates, DNS health-checks, anycast active node counts, BGP withdrawal signals, probe health.
   - Recent steering history: last 15–60m of routing decisions and resulting client metrics.

2) Decision graph stages (ordered):
   A) Safety checks: is there a hard outage signal (BGP withdraw, region unreachable, control-plane unreachable)? If yes, emit hard-failover action (exclude region).
   B) Capacity filter: mark regions as overloaded if free_capacity < threshold OR token_bucket exhausted for that tenant.
   C) Preference reconciliation: compute a set of candidate regions ordered by (tenant preference > proximity score > cost_score).
   D) Probabilistic weighting: convert candidates into weights using smoothing window and hysteresis; apply a min-weight floor to avoid blackholing traffic.
   E) Probing & validation: before committing large weight shifts, kick off short probes from representative edge nodes; validate latency and correctness (200 vs 5xx).
   F) Commit & monitor: update GeoDNS weight / Anycast steering config; create audit event and short-term rewind checkpoint for rollback.

Routing action types (what the control plane can do):
- GeoDNS weight change: adjust weighted DNS responses for top-level domain or service CNAMEs.
- Anycast egress hints: update BGP prepends or route filters (where supported by partners) to bias Anycast distribution.
- Edge-level header hint: set region-pref header in edge response (for downstream affinity).
- Fallback gating: configure CDN/origin routing to move traffic to a fallback origin pool.
- Hard exclude: remove region entirely from candidate set for the tenant.

Control Plane API (sketch) — REST endpoints we should expose:
- POST /v1/steering/policy { tenant_id, policy_name, preferred: [region], fallbacks: [region], max_latency_ms, cost_weight }
- GET /v1/steering/policy/{id} -> returns active policy + audit version
- POST /v1/steering/evaluate { tenant_id, client_geo_hint, header_hints } -> dry-run decision graph, returns proposed action and reason chain
- POST /v1/steering/commit { decision_id, mode: immediate|canary(percent), rollback_window_min }
- GET /v1/steering/history?tenant_id=... -> recent decisions + telemetry snapshot
- POST /v1/steering/force-exclude { region, reason } (admins only)

State machine semantics (convergence & hysteresis):
- Each region for a tenant has a state: Healthy, Degraded, Overloaded, Excluded.
- Transitions are triggered by windows over telemetry and explicit admin actions. Example: Healthy -> Degraded if p95_latency > policy.max_latency * 1.25 for 3m. Degraded -> Overloaded if error_rate > 2% for 5m or free_capacity < 10%.
- We add a cooling period on upward actions (to Excluded) of at least 5m and a longer reconverge window on returning to Healthy (10–30m depending on severity). This prevents oscillation from brief spikes.

Probing & validation details:
- Representative probes: must come from the same client-perspective egress points used by customers (not just internal control-plane). Use lambda-edge probes + synthetic clients in major IX points.
- Probe success criteria: median latency below threshold and <1% 5xx over probe sample; otherwise mark candidate as Degraded.
- Canary commit: if switching weights total > 30% of traffic across regions, require a canary window (percent param) with validation before global commit.

GeoDNS vs Anycast tradeoffs (operational guidance):
- GeoDNS: fine-grained per-tenant weighting, easy to control, but subject to DNS TTLs and recursive resolver caching. Good for planned rollouts and per-tenant preferences.
- Anycast: fast failover for client routing but gives less fine-grained control per-tenant; works best for coarse-grained global capacity shaping.
- Recommended pattern: prefer GeoDNS for tenant-level preferences + cost steering. Use Anycast as a last-resort region-reachability mechanism and for load-shedding when we need immediate global pressure relief.

Metrics & SLOs to feed into graph (examples):
- region.p95_latency_ms (by tenant)
- region.error_rate_5m (4xx/5xx split)
- region.free_capacity_pct (GPU slots free, normalized)
- dns.response_distribution (clients -> regions over TTL window)
- probe.success_ratio and probe.latency_ms
- steering.decision_rate (actions/min) and steering.rollback_rate

Operational controls and safety rails:
- Rate limit decision commits per-tenant (default 1 commit per 10m) to avoid spurious churn.
- Global circuit-breaker: if steering.rollback_rate > X or decision_rate suddenly increases, pause automated commits and switch to manual review.
- Audit trail & replay: store decisions with short-term snapshot of telemetry to allow deterministic replay for debugging.
- Role-based access: only platform-admins can force excludes or override safety checks.

Testing and rollout plan:
1) Local simu tests: deterministic decision-graph unit tests + fuzzing of telemetry inputs.
2) Staging canaries: apply to low-volume tenants with synthetic probe validation.
3) Region-limited beta: enable for subset of customers in a partner region with feature flag.
4) Ramp to 100%: move to full automation after 2 weeks of stable metrics and low rollback rate.

Open questions / unresolved decisions (for next sync):
- TTL strategy: what default DNS TTL to use per change (60s vs 300s) and per-tenant override? Tradeoffs between reactivity and resolver churn.
- Weight smoothing algorithm: should we use exponential smoothing with adaptive factor based on volatility, or a simple sliding-average?
- Anycast control knobs: how much of BGP/BGP-prepend ops do we expose vs keep to network ops?
- Cost-aware steering: how to quantify "cost_score" across cloud regions consistently?

Integration notes / references:
- Tie into existing observability pipeline: add steering-specific rollup metrics in the console and alerting.
- Link to existing playbooks: see confluence/ops/egress-handling (placeholder).
- Add a Jira epic to implement API + decision engine: JIRA-PLAT-2741.

Appendix: quick decision examples (scenarios)
- Scenario A: Single availability region reports 50% free GPU, p95 120ms (policy.max 100ms). Decision: mark Degraded (soft reduce weights by 30%), run probes for 10m; if probe still bad, canary commit 10% -> 50% over 30m.
- Scenario B: BGP withdraw observed for region EU-WEST from major provider -> immediate Exclude + GeoDNS weight shift to next preferred region; notify ops and start rollback checkpoint.
- Scenario C: transient spike in p95 for 3m then back to baseline -> no hard exclude due to cooling window; generate alert and wait for reconverge window before increasing weight back.

Notes to reviewers:
- Focus feedback on the state machine thresholds and API ergonomics. We want conservative defaults but flexible knobs.
- If we prefer a simpler initial rollout, propose a minimal feature set: Safety checks + GeoDNS weight commits + dry-run API.

End of note.

source.title: Triage & Communication Handoff Playbook — status, customer, war-room (draft)
source.content:
Purpose:
This is a working draft playbook for incident communication triage and handoff. Goal: give on-call SREs a compact set of reusable status-page messages, customer-facing templates, and an internal war-room checklist so comms are consistent, fast, and editable in the moment.

Scope and assumptions:
- Applies to platform affecting incidents (hosted API, Dedicated routing, private control plane) where customer-visible degradation or outage is detected.
- Triage severity mapping (quick reference):
  - Sev1: Full outage or majority customers impacted (API errors 5xx, model serving unavailable).
  - Sev2: Significant degradation (high latency, partial capacity loss, isolated zones).
  - Sev3: Degradation affecting small subset or non-critical flows (embeddings delay for background jobs).
- Use Statuspage for public status updates. Use the internal incident channel (e.g., #sre-incident-<id>) for live coordination.

How to use this document in an incident:
1) Post initial public status within 15 minutes for Sev1/Sev2, 60 minutes for Sev3 if confirmed customer impact.
2) Notify CS/Support via the incident template (copy-paste) and update them every major status change or every 60 minutes for long incidents.
3) Maintain a short running log in the incident channel; the communications lead should copy updates to Statuspage and customer channels.

Quick roles (who does what):
- Incident lead: owns triage, timeline, priority decisions.
- Communications lead: crafts and publishes Statuspage and customer messages.
- Engineering lead: owns root cause identification and mitigation steps.
- Support/CS rep: executes customer outreach and tracks open customer escalations.
- On-call ops: executes runbook steps (restarts, rollbacks, scaling).

Statuspage templates (minimal, editable). Replace {{incident_id}} and {{affected}} and link to incident channel. Keep terse.
1) Investigating (initial):
Title: Investigating elevated API error rate — {{affected}}
Message: We are aware of elevated error rates for {{affected}} and are investigating. Customers may see timeouts or 5xx responses. We will post updates in this page and in #sre-incident-{{incident_id}}.
Impact: Partial outage
Status: Investigating
Estimated next update: within 15 minutes

2) Identified (when probable cause found, mitigation in progress):
Title: Identified: routing misconfiguration affecting {{affected}}
Message: We have identified a routing misconfiguration that is impacting requests to {{affected}}. Engineers are applying a remediation now; some traffic will be routed to fallback pools. We expect recovery in ~X minutes.
Impact: Partial outage
Status: Identified
Estimated next update: in 30 minutes

3) Monitoring (post-mitigation):
Title: Monitoring: services recovering after rollback
Message: We implemented a rollback and are monitoring traffic and error rates. Metrics show error rate is decreasing but some customers may still see intermittent errors. We'll close this incident after 2 hours of stable metrics.
Impact: Reduced/Recovering
Status: Monitoring

4) Resolved (final):
Title: Resolved: routing issue mitigated for {{affected}}
Message: A rollback was applied and normal service has resumed for {{affected}}. We will publish a follow-up incident report with root cause and next steps. If you continue to experience issues, contact support.
Impact: Resolved
Status: Resolved

Customer-facing email template (for high-touch customers / enterprise):
Subject: Incident {{incident_id}} — Update on service availability for {{customer_org}}
Hi {{support_contact}},

We wanted to let you know that we are currently handling an incident impacting {{affected}}. What we're seeing: brief summary (e.g., elevated 5xx between 10:12–10:45 UTC affecting requests routed to region us-west1). What we're doing: short bullet list of steps. Current status: Investigating / Identified / Monitoring / Resolved.
Next update: within 30 minutes.
If you need immediate assistance or want a dedicated bridge, reply and we will set one up.

Thank you for your patience —
The Redwood Inference SRE team

Low-touch customer SMS/slack brief (one-liner for status monitors):
"Redwood: We’ve detected an issue affecting {{affected}}. Investigating. See status.redwood.ai/incident/{{incident_id}} for updates."

Internal war-room checklist (copy into #sre-incident-{{incident_id}} when creating a new channel):
- [ ] Post initial Statuspage message (use Investigating template).
- [ ] Create incident doc in Confluence (title: Incident {{incident_id}} — short desc).
- [ ] Add runbook links and incident playbook checklist to channel pinned items.
- [ ] Attach relevant monitoring graphs (error rate, latency, QPS, GPU utilization).
- [ ] Identify and announce scope: services, regions, customers affected.
- [ ] Assign roles (incident lead, comms lead, eng lead, scribe).
- [ ] Triage immediate mitigations (rollback, scale up, route to fallback model).
- [ ] Execute mitigation under change controls and log commands run.
- [ ] Start timeline; scribe updates every action taken + timestamp.
- [ ] Plan short-term customer messaging cadence (15/30/60 min as appropriate).
- [ ] Prepare post-incident actions and owner for RCA.

On-call handoff notes (end of shift):
- Summarize what is known: confirmed root cause? mitigation in progress? key metrics.
- List active actions with owners and ETA.
- Attach links: incident channel, Statuspage, Confluence incident doc, PagerDuty incident.
- Mention any customer escalations open and their owners.
- If unresolved, schedule a takeover sync with incoming on-call.

Examples of Slack messages (short and copyable):
- Initial: @channel Investigating elevated 5xx for /v1/generate impacting hosted-api. Creating incident #SRE-4281, channel #sre-incident-SRE-4281. Posting status update to status.redwood.ai.
- Post-mitigation: Update: rollback to v2.1.3 applied at 10:45 UTC. Error rate decreasing. Monitoring.
- All-clear: Resolved: normal service resumed at 12:01 UTC. Follow-up RCA scheduled.

Escalation matrix (quick):
- If no mitigation within 30 minutes for Sev1: page eng on-call manager and notify product leadership.
- If customer SLA impacted with enterprise contract: loop CS manager and AE.

Templates for internal timeline entries (scribe use):
- [HH:MM UTC] [actor] [action] — brief note, outcome or metrics link.
Example: [10:25 UTC] Samira (eng-oncall) restarted router-gateway-3 — error rate unchanged.

Known quick runbook commands (examples, replace placeholders):
- Check API error rate: prometheus query: sum(rate(http_requests_total{status=~"5..",service=\"hosted-api\"}[5m]))
- Restart router pod: kubectl rollout restart deploy/router-gateway -n serving --selector=zone=us-west1
- Scale fallback pool: autoscalerctl scale pool fallback_gpu_pool --replicas=10
(These are examples; always validate commands for the incident cluster environment.)

Open questions / TODOs in this draft:
- Should we auto-post a Statuspage Investigating message for every Sev2? (tradeoff: noise vs transparency)
- Add a small script to generate the initial Statuspage payload from PagerDuty metadata.
- Finalize which team owns comms lead for Dedicated vs Hosted incidents.

Post-incident follow-up checklist (to include in RCA):
- Confirm final timeline and add to Confluence incident doc.
- Capture root cause, contributing factors, and mitigation steps.
- Action items: owners and due dates for fixes or safeguards.
- Customer outreach summary: who was contacted and what was communicated.

Notes to self (informal):
- Keep templates intentionally short — customers want clarity, not internal jargon.
- Pre-approve one enterprise message variant with legal/PR for Sev1 with broad customer impact.
- Consider a small snippet in Console UI banner for global outages (product request).

- v0.2 2026-01-14 Added customer email template and on-call handoff checklist (Lena)
- v0.3 2026-03-01 Added runbook commands and open questions (Marcus)

End of draft.

source.title: Pre-merge signal diagnostics + follow-ups
source.content:
Quick mental model: this doc is a personal scratchpad for noisy PRs that touch the runtime hot paths (KV cache, batching, kernel selection). Mix of concrete checks, commands to run locally, flaky signals to watch for, and follow-ups to file after merge. Not polished — purposely scribbly.

Goal for pre-merge validation (fast):
- detect obvious perf regressions (p50/p95 token latency increases)
- detect correctness regressions that break decoder/kv cache behavior
- ensure graceful fallback behavior still works (model variants, quantized paths)
- capture follow-ups (tests, metrics, alerts) so we don't forget them after LGTM

Quick checklist (run these before approving):
1) run unit + integration smoke: ./scripts/ci-smoke.sh (use small model)
2) local microbench: python tools/bench/run.py --model tiny --seq 512 --iters 200 
   - compare baseline from artifacts/bench/latest.txt (p50/p95/p99)
3) run kv-regression test: pytest tests/kv_cache/test_invalidation.py -k "small_batch"
4) run CI with GPU profile (if touching kernels): CI_TAG=gpu pytest tests/benchmarks --durations=5
5) quick fuzz decode: python tools/fuzz/decode_fuzzer.py --seed 42 --duration 30s

What to look for in traces / logs:
- sudden 2x spike in step time starting at certain commit: check memset/copy calls in trace
- excessive CPU spin while waiting for CUDA events (possible missing stream sync)
- KV cache miss rate jump (tagging missing prefix or key eviction)
- batching latency outliers due to head-of-line blocking (bad batching key grouping)
- fallback noise: fallbacks to cpu/float32 showing up when they shouldn’t

Example commands & snippets (useful):
- tail -n 200 /var/log/redwood/serving/runtime.log | rg "WARN|ERROR" --no-line-number | sed -n '1,150p'
- python -m cProfile -o /tmp/profile.out run_demo.py --model=small && snakeviz /tmp/profile.out
- run a short soak with --trace: ./run_inference.sh --model small --trace --duration 120s > /tmp/trace.json

Heuristics for false positives (don’t block on these):
- small p99 noise on a single CI run — re-run 3x before flagging
- telemetry gaps during infra maintenance windows (check infra-status slack pinned)
- slight memory allocation variance after allocator changes — only block if it correlates to crashes or OOMs

Refactor / test ideas to add as follow-ups (file tickets):
- add stable microbench harness that records environment hashes (CUDA, driver, python) alongside numbers (ticket: PROJ-xxxx)
- unit test to assert KV cache invariants after partial evictions (edge-case with quantized models)
- test that simulates backlog batching reorder to catch head-of-line blocking early
- wrapper for kernel selection logging: emit human-readable reason when switching kernels (helps future audits)

Post-merge quick actions (create tickets before merging if possible):
- add regression alert for p95 token latency +20% sustained over 5m (owner: SRE)
- schedule a 1-hour soak next day on dedicated cluster (owner: whoever merges)
- add integration test to CI that runs the failing scenario deterministically (owner: author)

Notes about review style (personal):
- focus on intent: if the PR is short but touches sensitive lines, ask for a short 2-line explanation of why it’s safe
- prefer explicit invariants rather than comments that say "should be fine"
- ask for telemetry when behavior is non-obvious ("add a counter/metric so we can see X")

Known quirks to watch in serving-runtime changes (recent painful ones):
- subtle off-by-one in KV offset mapping after refactor of chunking code — looks harmless but causes cache misses
- implicit ownership transfer of buffers across async queues — can manifest as nondeterministic crashes under load
- batch-key hashing changes that reorder requests across worker threads (causes increased p99)

Unresolved questions to clarify in PR description or conversation: 
- does this change the default batching policy? (if yes, document expected latency/cost tradeoff and add a flag)
- are there backend-incompatibilities for pinned model versions? list exact affected versions
- any change to failure modes (new exceptions) that we need to map to fallback policies?

Scratch TODOs (file before merge if anything non-trivial):
- [ ] PROJ ticket stub for microbench harness (link)
- [ ] add metric: runtime.kernel_selection.reason (enum)
- [ ] add e2e scenario to nightly that exercises new code path

Random helpful review phrases I keep using: 
- "Can you add a microbenchmark that reproduces the latency diff?"
- "Please add a short note in the PR about backward compatibility with pinned model versions."
- "If this changes an invariant, add an assert + unit test so regressions fail fast."

Edge-case tests to consider (copy into new test file):
- multi-node request interleave: simulated by running 3 clients with varied prefixes and ensuring KV hits distribution stable
- decode interruption: simulate cancel between decode steps and verify no leaked buffers
- quantized ladder: rotate through quantization profiles and assert outputs within tolerance

Why I keep this file:
- I forget the ad-hoc commands and the small gotchas between PR cycles
- writing follow-ups immediately increases chance they get implemented after merge
- keeps review feedback consistent across runtime-critical PRs

If you read this and want to borrow the checklist, copy freely. If you have additions, ping me in slack and I’ll update.

—— end scratchpad ——

- tail -n 200 /var/log/redwood/serving/runtime.log | rg "WARN|ERROR" --no-line-number | sed -n '1,150p'
- python -m cProfile -o /tmp/profile.out run_demo.py --model=small && snakeviz /tmp/profile.out
- run a short soak with --trace: ./run_inference.sh --model small --trace --duration 120s > /tmp/trace.json

Heuristics for false positives (don’t block on these):
- small p99 noise on a single CI run — re-run 3x before flagging
- telemetry gaps during infra maintenance windows (check infra-status slack pinned)
- slight memory allocation variance after allocator changes — only block if it correlates to crashes or OOMs

Refactor / test ideas to add as follow-ups (file tickets):
- add stable microbench harness that records environment hashes (CUDA, driver, python) alongside numbers (ticket: PROJ-xxxx)
- unit test to assert KV cache invariants after partial evictions (edge-case with quantized models)
- test that simulates backlog batching reorder to catch head-of-line blocking early
- wrapper for kernel selection logging: emit human-readable reason when switching kernels (helps future audits)

Post-merge quick actions (create tickets before merging if possible):
- add regression alert for p95 token latency +20% sustained over 5m (owner: SRE)
- schedule a 1-hour soak next day on dedicated cluster (owner: whoever merges)
- add integration test to CI that runs the failing scenario deterministically (owner: author)

Notes about review style (personal):
- focus on intent: if the PR is short but touches sensitive lines, ask for a short 2-line explanation of why it’s safe
- prefer explicit invariants rather than comments that say "should be fine"
- ask for telemetry when behavior is non-obvious ("add a counter/metric so we can see X")

Known quirks to watch in serving-runtime changes (recent painful ones):
- subtle off-by-one in KV offset mapping after refactor of chunking code — looks harmless but causes cache misses
- implicit ownership transfer of buffers across async queues — can manifest as nondeterministic crashes under load
- batch-key hashing changes that reorder requests across worker threads (causes increased p99)

Unresolved questions to clarify in PR description or conversation: 
- does this change the default batching policy? (if yes, document expected latency/cost tradeoff and add a flag)
- are there backend-incompatibilities for pinned model versions? list exact affected versions
- any change to failure modes (new exceptions) that we need to map to fallback policies?

Scratch TODOs (file before merge if anything non-trivial):
- [ ] PROJ ticket stub for microbench harness (link)
- [ ] add metric: runtime.kernel_selection.reason (enum)
- [ ] add e2e scenario to nightly that exercises new code path

Random helpful review phrases I keep using: 
- "Can you add a microbenchmark that reproduces the latency diff?"
- "Please add a short note in the PR about backward compatibility with pinned model versions."
- "If this changes an invariant, add an assert + unit test so regressions fail fast."

Edge-case tests to consider (copy into new test file):
- multi-node request interleave: simulated by running 3 clients with varied prefixes and ensuring KV hits distribution stable
- decode interruption: simulate cancel between decode steps and verify no leaked buffers
- quantized ladder: rotate through quantization profiles and assert outputs within tolerance

Why I keep this file:
- I forget the ad-hoc commands and the small gotchas between PR cycles
- writing follow-ups immediately increases chance they get implemented after merge
- keeps review feedback consistent across runtime-critical PRs

If you read this and want to borrow the checklist, copy freely. If you have additions, ping me in slack and I’ll update.

—— end scratchpad ——

3) run kv-regression test: pytest tests/kv_cache/test_invalidation.py -k "small_batch"
4) run CI with GPU profile (if touching kernels): CI_TAG=gpu pytest tests/benchmarks --durations=5
5) quick fuzz decode: python tools/fuzz/decode_fuzzer.py --seed 42 --duration 30s

- sudden 2x spike in step time starting at certain commit: check memset/copy calls in trace

- KV cache miss rate jump (tagging missing prefix or key eviction)

- fallback noise: fallbacks to cpu/float32 showing up when they shouldn’t
